translations.source: Ibà Afàbíìgbín (Bilharzia) Ìsọnísókí: Ibà afàbíìgbín jẹ́ àìsàn tí òsì ń fà èyí tí ó ń yọrí sí àìlera ńlá. Wọ́n máa ń kó àrùn yìí nígbà tí ènìyàn bá ní ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú omi odò tí ó ti lùgbàdì pẹ̀lú àwọn ẹ̀yà kòkòrò (cercariae) ajọ̀fẹ́ mùjẹ̀mùjẹ̀ tí a mọ̀ sí sísítósóòmù. Àwọn àgbà kòkòrò wẹ́wẹ́ aláìṣeéfojúrí náà ń gbé nínú àwọn isan tí ó ń gbé ìdọ̀tí kúrò ní òpónà ìtọ̀ àti ìfun. Púpọ̀ àwọn ẹyin tí wọ́n yé máa ń há sínú àwọn ìṣù ara bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni èsì ara sí wọn lè fa ìdíbàjẹ́ ńlá. Ibà afàbíìgbín mú bí mílíọ̀nù 240 ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé, àti ó lé ní ènìyàn mílíọ̀nù 700 ni wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn àgbègbè àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn. Àrùn náà wọ́pọ̀ ní àwọn àgbègbè ipa òòrùn àti àyíká agbègbè ipa òòrùn, ní àwọn àwùjọ tí kò lówó láìsí omi ẹ̀rọ àti ìmọ́tótó tó tó. Kòkòrò Sikisotómà amatóbíọ́ọ̀mù àti sikisotómà ajẹmọ́fun ni ó ń fa ibà afàbíìgbín ajẹmópòónà-òun-ìbálòpọ̀ láti ọwọ́ èyíkéyìí àwọn ajọ̀fẹ́ S. guineensis, S. intercalatum, S. mansoni, S. japonicum, àti S. mekongi. Ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé ní àrùn líle nítorí ibà afàbíìgbín. Ọgbọ́n WHO lórí ìlò àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò ajọ̀fẹ́ nísinsìnyí mú kí ó ṣe é ṣe láti ṣàkóso ibà afàbíìgbín ní àwọn àwùjọ olósì àti àwùjọ adẹ́yẹsí, ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú ìpèsè tako lìǹfàtíìkì filaríásíìsì, onkokasíásí àti àwọn kòkòrò ajọ̀fẹ́ tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn.
translations.reference: Schistosomiasis (Bilharzia) Overview: Schistosomiasis is a disease of poverty that leads to chronic ill-health. Infection is acquired when people come into contact with fresh water infested with the larval forms (cercariae) of parasitic blood flukes, known as schistosomes. The microscopic adult worms live in the veins draining the urinary tract and intestines. Most of the eggs they lay are trapped in the tissues and the body’s reaction to them can cause massive damage. Schistosomiasis affects almost 240 million people worldwide, and more than 700 million people live in endemic areas. The infection is prevalent in tropical and sub-tropical areas, in poor communities without potable water and adequate sanitation. Urogenital schistosomiasis is caused by Schistosoma haematobium and intestinal schistosomiasis by any of the organisms S. guineensis, S. intercalatum, S. mansoni, S. japonicum, and S. mekongi. Several million people all over the world suffer from severe morbidity as a consequence of schistosomiasis. The WHO strategy on use of anthelminthic drugs now makes it possible to control schistosomiasis in poor and marginalized communities, in conjunction with interventions against lymphatic filariasis, onchocerciasis and soil transmitted helminthiasis.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Schistosomiasis (Bilharzia) Information:** Schistosomiasis is a disease caused by worms that can lead to serious illness. It is contracted when humans come into contact with freshwater that contains parasitic larvae (cercariae) known as schistosomes. These immature worms are free-swimming and live in the circulatory and excretory systems. Many of the eggs they produce are deposited in the tissues, and this can cause significant damage. Schistosomiasis affects approximately 240 million people worldwide, and 700 million people live in endemic areas. The disease is common in tropical and subtropical regions, particularly in impoverished communities lacking access to clean water and proper sanitation. *Schistosoma* species, including *Schistosoma haematobium*, *Schistosoma mansoni*, *Schistosoma japonicum*, *Schistosoma mekongi*, and *Schistosoma intercalatum*, are responsible for schistosomiasis transmission from the snails. Many people around the world suffer from disability due to schistosomiasis. The WHO's efforts regarding the use of anti-schistosome drugs have made it possible to control schistosomiasis in vulnerable and at-risk communities, in collaboration with programs for filariasis, cancer, and other parasitic diseases that the country is experiencing.

translations.source: Ní àwọn àgbègbè àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn, àrùn líle nítorí ibà afàbíìgbín lè ṣe é dènà ìtọ́jú ojoojúmọ́ àwọn ẹgbẹ́ tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu tí wọ́n fojúsùn dá lórí àyẹ̀wò àwùjọ nítorí àwọn àkójọpọ̀ ẹ̀sọ́. Wọ́n ti ṣàmúlò Praziquantel pẹ̀lú albendazole àti ivermectin dáadáa, ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn òògùn wọ̀nyí ti jẹ́ ṣíṣe àmúlò ní ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ fún kemotẹ́ráfì aláàbò. Àmì àìsàn: Àwọn àmì àìsàn ibà afàbíìgbín ní àbájáde ara tako àwọn ẹyin kòkòrò náà máa ń fà. Ibà afàbíìgbín ajẹmópòónà ọ̀fun lè fa inú rírun, ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, àti ẹ̀jẹ̀ nínú ìgbẹ́. Ìtóbi ẹ̀dọ̀ wọ́pọ̀ ní àwọn ọ̀ràn líle, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó sáábà máa ń ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú omi tí ó sarajọ sínú káà inú àti ẹ̀jẹ̀ rírú àwọn isan abo inú. Nínú aowm ọ̀ràn wọ̀nyí ìtóbi àbaadì òróòro náà lè wáyé. Àpẹẹrẹ gbòógì ibà afàbíìgbín ajẹmópòónà ìtọ̀-ojú-ara ni haematuria (ẹ̀jẹ̀ nínú ìtọ̀). Ìnípọn àbaadì àpò ìtọ̀ àti òpónà ìtọ̀, àti ìdíbàjẹ́ kíndìnrín sáábà máa ń jẹ́ ṣíṣe àyẹ̀wò nínú àwọn ọ̀ràn líle. Jẹjẹrẹ àpò ìtọ̀ tún jẹ́ ìṣòro mìíràn tí ó ṣe é ṣe kó farahàn ní àwọn ìpele ọjọ́ iwájú. Láàrin àwọn obìnrin, ibà afàbíìgbín ajẹmópòónà ìtọ̀-òun-ojú-ara lè farahàn pẹ̀lú kókó lójú ara, ojú ara obìnrin tí ó ń sun ẹ̀jẹ̀, ìrora lásìkò ìbálòpọ̀, àti rorẹ́ ojú òbò.
translations.reference: In highly endemic areas, severe morbidity due to schistosomiasis can be prevented by regular treatment of at risk groups targeted based on community diagnosis based on sentinel groups. Praziquantel has been safely co-administered with albendazole and ivermectin, in areas where these drugs have been used separately for preventive chemotherapy. Symptoms: Symptoms of schistosomiasis are caused by the body’s reaction to the worms' eggs. Intestinal schistosomiasis can result in abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and blood in the stool. Liver enlargement is common in advanced cases, and is frequently associated with an accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal cavity and hypertension of the abdominal blood vessels. In such cases there may also be enlargement of the spleen. The classic sign of urogenital schistosomiasis is haematuria (blood in urine). Fibrosis of the bladder and ureter, and kidney damage are sometimes diagnosed in advanced cases. Bladder cancer is another possible complication in the later stages. In women, urogenital schistosomiasis may present with genital lesions, vaginal bleeding, pain during sexual intercourse, and nodules in the vulva.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"In areas affected by neglected tropical diseases, schistosomiasis (bilharzia) can hinder the daily assessment of groups at risk, which are focused on community monitoring due to the prevalence of these diseases. Praziquantel has been effectively combined with albendazole and ivermectin in areas where these drugs have been widely used for mass chemotherapy. Symptoms: The symptoms of bilharzia are those that the worms cause. Genital schistosomiasis can cause pain, bloody stool, and blood in the stool. Frequent urination is common in these illnesses, and it can be accompanied by pus entering the bladder and bloody urine from internal infections. In these conditions, hematuria (blood in the urine) can occur. Significant bladder pain, urinary tract obstruction, and kidney damage can also be seen in these illnesses. Persistent urinary pain is another problem that can affect future health. In women, genital schistosomiasis can cause itching in the genitals, vaginal discharge, menstrual irregularities, and eye redness."

translations.source: Láàárín àwọn ọkùnrin, ibà afàbíìgbín ajẹmópòónà ìtọ̀-òun-ojú-ara lè fa àìsàn ọ̀pá àtọ̀, jẹjẹrẹ ìṣétọ̀, àti àwọn ẹ̀yà ara mìíràn. Àìsàn yìí tún lè ní àwọn ìṣòro ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ tí kò ṣe é yí padà mìíràn, tí ó fi mọ́ àìlèbímọ. Àwọn ipa ibà afàbíìgbín lórí ọrọ̀ ajé àti ìlera tóbi bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àìsàn náà sọni di aláàbọ̀ ara ju kí ó pani lọ. Láàárín àwọn ọmọdé, ibà afàbíìgbín lè fa àìlẹ́jẹ̀tó, rírán àti àdínkù agbára láti kẹ́kọ̀ọ́, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé àwọn ipa rẹ̀ sáábà máa ń ṣe é yípadà pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú. Ìtọ́jú: Praziquantel ni ìtọ́jú tí wọ́n lù ní ọ̀ǹtẹ̀ tako gbogbo àwọn ẹ̀yà ibà afàbíìgbín. Ó kójúwọ̀n, ó ní ààbò, àti ó jẹ́ olówó péréte. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé àrùn náà tún lè sọ lẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú, ewu níní àìsàn ńlá ti dín kù bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ti fà sẹ́yìn nígbà tí ìtọ́jú bá ti di àgbékalẹ̀ àti àṣetúnṣe lásìkò èwe. Àkóso ibà afàbíìgbín ni wọ́n ti ṣàmúlò ní ó lé ní àwọn ọdún 40 káàkiri àwọn onírúurú orílẹ̀ èdè, tí ó fi mọ́ Brazil, Cambodia, China, Egypt, Mauritius, Islamic Republic of Iran, Oman, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Morocco, Tunisia, abbl. Ní Burundi, Burkina Faso, Ghana, Niger, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, the United Republic of Tanzania, àti Yemen, ó ti ṣe é ṣe láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ìtọ́jú ibà afàbíìgbín dé ìpele orílẹ̀ èdè bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó nípa lórí àrùn náà ní ọdún díẹ̀. Ìgbéléwọ̀n ipò ìkóràn ṣe pàtàkì ní àwọn onírúurú orílẹ̀ èdè.
translations.reference: In men, urogenital schistosomiasis can induce pathology of the seminal vesicles, prostate, and other organs. This disease may also have other long-term irreversible consequences, including infertility. The economic and health effects of schistosomiasis are considerable and the disease disables more than it kills. In children, schistosomiasis can cause anaemia, stunting and a reduced ability to learn, although the effects are usually reversible with treatment. Treatment: Praziquantel is the recommended treatment against all forms of schistosomiasis. It is effective, safe, and low-cost. Even though re-infection may occur after treatment, the risk of developing severe disease is diminished and even reversed when treatment is initiated and repeated in childhood. Schistosomiasis control has been successfully implemented over the past 40 years in several countries, including Brazil, Cambodia, China, Egypt, Mauritius, Islamic Republic of Iran, Oman, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Morocco, Tunisia, etc. In Burundi, Burkina Faso, Ghana, Niger, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, the United Republic of Tanzania, and Yemen, it has been possible to scale-up schistosomiasis treatment to the national level and have an impact on the disease in a few years. An assessment of the status of transmission is required in several countries.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Among men, schistosomiasis (bilharzia) can cause urinary tract infections, hematuria (blood in urine), and other organ damage. This disease can also cause chronic urinary symptoms that do not resolve, leading to infertility. The impact of schistosomiasis on economic productivity and health is so significant that it can lead to a decline in the quality of life. In children, schistosomiasis can cause learning difficulties, stunted growth, and reduced ability to learn, although its effects are often less severe with proper treatment. Treatment: Praziquantel is the treatment used to combat all stages of schistosomiasis. It is safe, well-tolerated, and inexpensive. Although the disease can recur after treatment, the risk of severe complications is significantly reduced, and recurrence is less likely when treatment is comprehensive and timely. Schistosomiasis control has been implemented in over 40 countries around the world, including Brazil, Cambodia, China, Egypt, Mauritius, Islamic Republic of Iran, Oman, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Morocco, Tunisia, etc. In Burundi, Burkina Faso, Ghana, Niger, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, the United Republic of Tanzania, and Yemen, efforts have been made to integrate schistosomiasis treatment into national health programs, focusing on the disease for a few years. Monitoring the disease situation is crucial in various countries."

translations.source: Fún ọdún 10 sẹ́yìn, àwọn ìpolongo ìdàgbàsókè ìtọ́jú ti wà ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè àyíká Sahara Áfríkà, níbi tí púpọ̀ àwọn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ń gbé.
translations.reference: Over the past 10 years, there has been scale-up of treatment campaigns in a number of sub-Saharan countries, where most of those at risk live.
translations.hypothesis: For 10 years prior, drought resilience programs have existed in many countries across sub-Saharan Africa, where many of those living in them are in danger.

translations.source: Àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú-araẹni fún ìlera Ìsọnísókí: Ìtọ́jú araẹni ni agbára àwọn ènìyàn, ẹbí àti àwùjọ láti ṣe àgbéga ìlera, ìdènà àìsàn, àkóso ìlera, àti faradà àìsàn àti ààbọ̀ ara pẹ̀lú àti láìsí àtìlẹ́yìn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera. Ní báyìí bílíọ́nù 3.6 ènìyàn - ìdajì àgbáyé - kò ní àǹfààní sí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì. WHO dábàá àwọn ìpèsè ìtọ́jú araẹni fún orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan àti ibùdó ọrọ̀ ajé, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀nà pàtàkì láti láti ṣe ìpèsè ìlera káríayé, ìpolongo ìlera, ìdáàbòbò ayé, àti ìpèsè fún àwọn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu. Ìtọ́jú araẹni ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ pàtàkì nínú ṣíṣe àkóso ìlera ara wọn, ní àwọn àgbègbè tí ó fi mọ́ ìpolongo ìlera, ìdènà àti àkóso àìsàn; lílo òògùn fúnra ẹni; ìpèsè ìtọ́jú fún àwọn àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n nílò ìrànlọ́wọ́, àti imúpàdàbọ̀sípò, tí ó fi mọ́ ìtọ́jú ìrànwọ́. Ìpèsè ìtọ́jú araẹni jẹ́ àwọn irinṣẹ́ adálórí-ẹ̀rí, tí ó dára tí wọ́n ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú araẹni. Lára wọn ni òògùn, igbaninímọ̀ràn, àyẹ̀wò àti/tàbí àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ òde òní èyí tí ènìyàn lè ní àǹfààní sí ní pípé tàbí níwọ̀n níta àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera elétò. Dá lórí ìdásí náà, wọ́n lè jẹ́ lílò pẹ̀lú tàbí láìsí àmójútó àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera tààrà. Àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú araẹni lè: ṣíṣe ìrónilágbára fún àwọn ènìyàn àti àwùjọ láti ṣàkóso ìlera àti àlàáfíà wọn. ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn ibùdó orílẹ̀ èdè pẹ̀lú ìlò àwọn ohun èlò abẹ́lé pípé fún ìlera.
translations.reference: Self-care interventions for health Overview: Self-care is the ability of individuals, families and communities to promote health, prevent disease, maintain health, and cope with illness and disability with or without the support of a health worker.Currently 3.6 billion people – half of the world – lack access to essential health services. WHO recommends self-care interventions for every country and economic setting, as a critical path to reach universal health coverage, promote health, keep the world safe, and serve the vulnerable. Self-care recognises individuals as active agents in managing their own health care, in areas including health promotion; disease prevention and control; self-medication; providing care to dependent persons, and rehabilitation, including palliative care. Self-care interventions are evidence-based, quality tools that support self-care. They include medicines, counselling, diagnostics and/or digital technologies which can be accessed fully or partially outside of formal health services. Depending on the intervention, they can be used with or without the direct supervision of health workers. Self-care interventions can: empower individuals and communities to manage their health and well-being strengthen national institutions with efficient use of domestic resources for health
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Community Health Worker Interventions for Primary Health Care:** Community health workers possess the power of individuals, families, and communities to support health, prevent disease, manage illness, and treat disease and disability with and without the support of healthcare professionals. Currently, 3.6 billion people – the majority of the global population – lack access to essential health services. WHO recommends community health interventions for each country and economic zone, as a key strategy to achieve universal health coverage, health promotion, life expectancy improvement, and care for those at risk. Community health workers play a vital role in the management of people's health, particularly in areas that prioritize health promotion, disease prevention, and illness management; medication use; care for people who need assistance; and emergency response, contributing to health care delivery. Community health interventions are evidence-based practices that support community health work. These include medication, counseling, examination and/or modern medical technologies that people can benefit from directly or indirectly through essential health services. Based on this, they can be used with or without the direct involvement of healthcare professionals. Community health worker interventions can: strengthen the capacity of individuals and communities to control illness and well-being; provide support to local communities through the use of advanced health technologies;

translations.source: mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìtọ́jú ìlera abẹ́lé (IIA) àti ipa láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìtọ́jú ìlera káríayé. Ìlànà: Ìlànà ìpèsè ìtọ́jú araẹni adálórí-ènìyàn ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn àìní àti ẹ̀tọ́ àwọn ènìyàn nípasẹ̀ ìlànà adálórí-èèyàn tí ó gbilẹ̀ nínú ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn àti ìṣedéédé fún takọtabo. Èyí jẹ́ ìlànà gbòòrò sí ìlera àti àlàáfíà èèyàn kọ̀ọ̀kan, nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn ìṣòro wọn, àìní àti ìfẹ́ wọn káàkiri ìgbésí ayé, àti irúfẹ́ àyíká tí wọ́n ń gbé. Ìtọ́jú araẹni ni agbára àwọn ènìyàn láti dènà àti tọ́jú àwọn àìsàn fúnra wọn, ní ọ̀gangan àyíká aláàbò àti àyíká tí ó pèsè àtìlẹ́yìn. Kò dípò ètò ìtọ́jú ìlera, ṣùgbọ́n dípò bẹ́ẹ̀ ó pèsè àwọn àṣàyàn àti àkójọ onírúurú fún ìtọ́jú ìlera. Àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú araẹni tí wọ́n wà, tí wọ́n wà ní àrọ́wọ́tó, tí owó wọn kò pọ̀ àti tí wọ́n péye jẹ́ àwọn ẹ̀yà pàtàkì ìgbéga àti ìdáàbòbò ẹ̀tọ́ àwọn ènìyàn sí ìlera. Àwọn ìdásí ìlera lè mú àgbéga bá ìmúnádóko ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera, àǹfààní sí i àti ìkojúwọ̀n. Èyí pè fún àwọn òfin àti ìlànà, ìpèsè owó fún ìlera, àwọn ọjà dídára tí òfin dè àti àwọn ìdásí tí wọ́n ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn, àti ẹ̀kọ́ èyí tí ó ń ṣe àgbéga ìkópa àwọn ènìyàn, pẹ̀lú níní òmìnira àti àṣírí araẹni. Àwọn àyẹ̀wò ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn ń ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti ṣe àdínkù ìwà ipá, lílo ipa, ìdẹ́yẹsí àti ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà nípa mímú ìdàgbàsókè bá àǹfààní ẹ̀tọ́ ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan sí ìlera, àlàyé, àti ṣíṣe ìpinnu olómìnira. Ọ̀gangan pàjáwìrì
translations.reference: improve primary healthcare (PHC) and contribute to achieving UHC Approach: A people-centred approach Self-care interventions support the needs and rights of individuals through a people-centred approach that is grounded in human rights and gender equality. This is a holistic approach to the health and well-being of each person, taking account of their circumstances, needs and desires across a lifetime, as well as the environment in which they live. Self-care is the power of individuals to prevent and treat diseases themselves, in the context of a safe and supportive enabling environment. It does not replace the health care system, but instead provides additional choices and options for health care. Available, accessible, affordable, acceptable and good quality self-care interventions are core components of promoting and protecting people’s right to health. Self-care interventions can improve the efficiency of health service delivery, access and coverage. This requires supportive laws and policies, health financing, regulated quality products and interventions, and education which promotes the participation of individuals, as well as privacy and confidentiality. Human rights considerations help to reduce violence, coercion, stigma and discrimination by improving each person’s exercise of their right to health, information, and independent decision-making. Emergency context
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**The Importance of Individual Health Initiatives (IIH) and their Role in Achieving Global Health.**

**Methodology:** The methodology of personal health assessment, led by individuals, supports the needs and rights of people through human rights frameworks that are rooted in citizenship rights and sustainable development. This is a crucial approach to the health and well-being of each individual, by addressing their challenges, needs, and desires throughout their life, and the overall quality of life they experience. Individual health assessment empowers people to prevent and manage their illnesses in a comprehensive and supportive manner. It is not a health intervention program, but rather it provides options and diverse solutions for health assessment. Individual health initiatives that exist, whether they are in rural areas, have limited financial resources, or have disabilities, are essential components of improving and guaranteeing people's rights to health. Health challenges can lead to increased pressure on the healthcare system, reduced access to it, and a decline in quality. This relates to policies and programs, financial support for health, specific products that governments provide, and initiatives that support individuals, as well as education that enhances the overall well-being of people, including autonomy and self-determination. Human rights guarantees help to increase responsibility, utilization of resources, advice, and awareness regarding the impact of social determinants on individual health rights, explanation, and the exercise of self-determination.

**Confidential**

translations.source: Àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn àti ìrújú ẹ̀dá ọmọnìyàn Ní àwọn ipò níbi tí àwọn ètò ìlera ìsáájú ti fẹ̀, àwọn ìpè tí kò lẹ́gbẹ́ lórí àwọn ènìyàn àti àwùjọ náà ń sáábà wà láti ṣàkóso ìlera wọn. Àwọn ọgbọ́n ọ̀tun tí ó tayọ ẹ̀ka ìlera àtijọ́ jẹ́ èyí tí a nílò kánkán nítorí pé: àkọsílẹ̀ ènìyàn mílíọ̀nù 274 ni wọ́n nílò ìrànlọ́wọ́ ọmọnìyàn àti ààbò bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ìbújáde àìsàn jẹ́ ewu káríayé nígbà gbogbo. ó kéré jù bí mílíọ̀nù 400 ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé kò ní àǹfààní sí púpọ̀ àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì. 1 nínú 5 iye ènìyàn lágbàáyé ni wọ́n ń gbé nínú àwọn ìṣòro ọmọnìyàn, nínú èyí tí àwọn ètò ìlera ní ìṣòro láti pèsè àwọn ìlera pàtàkì. Àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú araẹni lè pèsè àwọn àṣàyàn pàtàkì, àfikún àṣàyàn sí àwọn ibùdó ìlera ìgbà gbogbo- tàbí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera adálórí-òṣìṣẹ́, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ara ètò ìlera tí ó ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn. Èyí pàápàá ní í ṣe lásìkò àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn COVID-19, nígbà tí ìyaraẹnisọ́tọ̀ àti àwọn òté ìtakété síraẹni mú kí ó ṣòro sí i fún àwọn ènìyàn láti ní àǹfààní sí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera ojoojúmọ́ àti òògùn àti àwọn ètò ìlera orílẹ̀ èdè lè dàrú. WHO mú àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú araẹni gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn iṣẹ́ pàtàkì nínú ìtọ́nisọ́nà iṣẹ́ lórí àkóso àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì fún ọ̀gangan COVID-19.
translations.reference: Pandemics and humanitarian crisesIn situations where existing health systems are stretched, there is often an unprecedented demand on individuals and communities to manage their health. Innovative strategies that go beyond the conventional health sector response is urgently needed given that: a record 274 million people are currently in need of humanitarian assistance and protection and disease outbreaks are a constant global threat at least 400 million people worldwide lack access to the most essential health services. 1 in 5 of the world’s population are now living in humanitarian crises, in which health systems are challenged to deliver essential services. Self-care interventions can provide valuable, additional options to the usual health facility- or health worker-based services, as part of a supportive health system. This is particularly relevant during a pandemic like COVID-19, when self-isolation and physical distancing measures make it more difficult for people to access their normal health care services and medications and national health systems can be disrupted. WHO includes self-care interventions as priority actions in operational guidance on maintaining essential health services for the COVID-19 context.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Communicable Diseases and Adolescent Health**

In situations where advance healthcare programs are lacking, the burdens on individuals and society are often overwhelming in managing illnesses. The respected experts in the field of public health agree that this is critically needed because: 274 million people require adolescent health and reproductive health services, and these health challenges remain a global threat at all times. Approximately 400 million people worldwide lack access to essential health services. 1 in 5 people globally live in humanitarian crises, within which healthcare programs struggle to address critical illnesses.  Addressing the social determinants of health can address critical challenges, adding options to health facilities at all times - or essential health services for workers, especially as supported by a robust healthcare system. This was particularly evident during the COVID-19 pandemic, when travel restrictions and lockdowns made it difficult for people to access routine health services, vaccines, and national healthcare programs. WHO considers addressing the social determinants of health as essential components in the strategy for managing essential health services for the COVID-19 response.

translations.source: Sẹ́púsì Ìsọnísókí: Sẹ́púsì jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó léwu tí ó máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ nígbà tí ara bá fèsì sí àrùn tí ó fà ìfarapa fún ìṣù ara àti ẹ̀yà rẹ̀. Ó jẹ́ òpin ipa ọ̀nà tí ó wọ́pọ̀ sí ikú fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àrùn káàkiri àgbáyé. Ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìṣedéédé ẹ̀yà ara èyí tí ó jẹ́ èsì akùdé àgọ́ sí àrùn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni bí wọn kò bá tètè ṣe ìdámọ̀ àti ìtọ́jú rẹ̀ kankan, ó lè yọrí sí ìpayà sẹ́púsì, ìkùnà àwọn àgọ́ ẹ̀yà ara àti ikú. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ìṣirò gbòógì àjàgà àrùn sẹ́púsì káríayé ṣòro láti mọ̀, ìròyìn àtẹ̀jáde sáyẹ́ńsì àìpẹ́ kan jábọ̀ pé àwọn ipa sẹ́púsì nípa lórí bí mílíọ̀nù 49 ènìyàn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó fa àwọn ikú mílíọ̀nù 11 káríayé lọ́dọọdún. Ní ibùdó àwùjọ, sẹ́púsì sáábà máa ń wà gẹ́gẹ́ bí àdínkù ojúṣe ara tit ó wọ́pọ̀ àti àwọn àrùn tí wọ́n ṣe é dènà. Sẹ́púsì tún máa ń wáyé láti ara àwọn àrùn tí ènìyàn kó ní àwọn ibùdó ìtọ́jú ìlera, èyí tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọ̀kan nínú àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tí ó lágbára jùlọ lásìkò ìpèsè ìtọ́jú bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ń nípa lórí àwọn ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ aláìsàn káàkiri àgbáyé. Bí sẹ́púsì bá ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò oyún, lásìkò tàbí lẹ́yìn ìbímọ, tàbí lẹ́yìn ìṣẹ́yún, èyí ni sẹ́púsì abiamọ. Sẹ́púsì láàárín àwọn ọmọ tuntun ni à ń pè ní sẹ́púsì ìkókó. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ó ṣe é dènà dáadáa, sẹ́púsì abiamọ àti ìkókó túbọ̀ jẹ́ okùnfà ikú gbòógì fún àwọn aláboyún àti àwọn ọmọ tuntun.
translations.reference: Sepsis Overview: Sepsis is a life-threatening condition that arises when the body’s response to infection causes injury to its own tissues and organs. It is frequently a final common pathway to death for many infectious diseases worldwide. It involves organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection and if not recognized early and managed promptly, it can lead to septic shock, multiple organ failure and death. Although a precise estimate of the global epidemiological burden of sepsis is difficult to ascertain, a recent scientific publication reported that sepsis affects an estimated 49 million people and causes 11 million deaths globally every year. In the community setting, sepsis often presents as the clinical deterioration of common and preventable infections. Sepsis also frequently results from infections acquired in health care settings, which are one of the most frequent adverse events during care delivery and affect hundreds of millions of patients worldwide every year. If sepsis develops during pregnancy, while or after giving birth, or after an abortion, it is called maternal sepsis. Sepsis in newborn babies is called neonatal sepsis. Despite being highly preventable, maternal and neonatal sepsis continues to be a major cause of death for pregnant women and newborn babies.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Septicemia Report: Septicemia is a serious illness that occurs when the body responds excessively to an infection that causes inflammation of the tissues and organs. It is a major cause of death for many diseases worldwide. It is characterized by a systemic inflammatory response syndrome, which is a life-threatening condition that can lead to organ dysfunction, sepsis, organ failure, and death if not treated promptly. Although the global burden of sepsis is difficult to quantify, recent scientific reports indicate that sepsis affects approximately 49 million people worldwide and causes approximately 11 million deaths annually. In the community, sepsis is often associated with high rates of morbidity and mortality and with conditions that predispose individuals to infection. Septicemia can also arise from infections that people acquire in healthcare settings, which are among the most preventable events during healthcare and affect vulnerable populations worldwide. Septicemia occurring during childhood, during or after delivery, or after surgery is known as postpartum sepsis. Septicemia among newborns is called neonatal sepsis. Although it may seem mild, postpartum and neonatal sepsis are a major cause of death for infants and newborns.**

**Key notes on the translation:**

*   I aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability in English.
*   I used terminology consistent with medical understanding of sepsis.
*   I tried to capture the nuances of the Yoruba language as best as possible within the constraints of English.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Àmì àìsàn: Sẹ́púsì jẹ́ pàjáwìrì ìṣègùn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó pè fún ìtọ́jú kankan láti dènà àwọn ìṣòro síwájú sí i tàbí ikú. Ó lè ṣe àfihàn àwọn onírúurú àmì ìkìlọ̀ àti àmì àìsàn nígbà mìíràn, díẹ̀ nínú èyí tí wọ́n lè fa ìdámọ̀ òdì pẹ̀lú àwọn ìṣòro mìíràn. Àrùn sẹ́púsì lè fa ibà, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè fa ìgbóná ara tí kò tó nǹkan, òtútù ara àti ẹ̀gbọ̀n rìrì. Ìnira lè wà láti máa mí tàbí ìmí líle, àti ọkàn tí ó ń lù kìkì, tàbí mímí tí kò lágbára tó àti àìrutó ẹ̀jẹ̀. Ó lè fa ìtọ̀ tí kò tó nǹkan, àti awọ ara dúdú tàbí awọ sísì, àti ipò ọkàn tí àyídà ti bá. Ní ìkádìí, ó lè fa ara ríro tàbí ìnira ńlá, èyí tí ó mú kí ó rọrùn láti sì í mú pẹ̀lú ọ̀fìnkìn-olóde tàbí májèlé inú oúnjẹ. Láàárín àwọn ọmọdé, sẹ́púsì lè farahàn nípasẹ̀ mímí tí ó yára púpọ̀, gìrì, ara sísì, àìlera tàbí ìṣòro jíjí tàbí níní ìmọ̀lára ìtutùjù fún ìfọwọ́kàn. Fún àwọn ọmọdé lábẹ́ ọdún 5, ó lè fa ìṣòro oúnjẹ jíjẹ, èébì tàbí àìletọ̀ léraléra. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ sẹ́púsì àti gbígbé ìgbésẹ̀ kánkán ṣe pàtàkì fún ìdámọ̀ àti àyẹ̀wò ní àsìkò. Ìtọ́jú: Ìdámọ̀ àwọn àmì àìsàn lásìkò àti ìfìdímúlẹ̀ ìṣàkóso ìṣègùn lásìkò jẹ́ àwọn èròjà pàtàkì láti mú àdínkù bá ewu àti ipa sẹ́púsì.
translations.reference: Symptoms: Sepsis is a medical emergency and requires immediate attention to prevent further complications or death. It may present different warning signs and symptoms at times, some of which may cause misidentification with other conditions. A septic infection may cause a fever, but may also cause low body temperature, cold extremities and shivering. There may be difficulty breathing or rapid breathing, as well as an increased heart rate, or conversely a weak pulse and low blood pressure. It may cause low urine output, as well as blue-tinted, mottled or abnormally pale skin, and an altered mental state. Finally, it may cause extreme body pain or discomfort, which makes it easy to confuse for influenza or food poisoning. In children, sepsis may present through very fast breathing, convulsions, pale skin, lethargy or difficulty waking up or feeling abnormally cold to the touch. For children under 5 years, it may cause difficulty feeding, repeated vomiting or a lack of urination. Suspecting sepsis and acting quickly is crucial for early recognition and diagnosis. Treatment: Early identification of symptoms and timely establishment of appropriate clinical management are critical elements to reducing the risk and impact of sepsis.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Disease Name:** Sepsis is a severe stage of infection that prevents the body from responding to the infection and can lead to shock or death. It can manifest with a variety of warning signs and symptoms of illness, some of which can cause organ failure along with other complications. Sepsis can cause fever, but it can also cause a slight chill, skin rash, and confusion. Difficulty breathing or shallow breathing, a racing heart, or weakness and decreased urine output may occur. It can cause slight pain and dark or pale skin, and a state of disorientation. In some cases, it can cause severe fatigue or exhaustion, making it easy to feel overwhelmed and accompanied by difficulty swallowing or eating. In children, sepsis can manifest through rapid breathing, vomiting, pale skin, lethargy, or severe pain or behavioral changes. For children under 5, it can cause severe food intolerance, diarrhea, or dehydration. Preventing sepsis and minimizing the progression of illness are crucial for diagnosis and treatment at the right time.

**Treatment:** Prompt diagnosis of disease symptoms and timely initiation of infection control measures are essential to reduce the risk and impact of sepsis.

translations.source: Ìṣàwárí àwọn àmì-ajọ̀fẹ́, gẹ́gẹ́ bí aṣẹ̀míró C inú ẹ̀dọ̀ àti purokasitoníìnì, lè kájúẹ̀ láti ṣe àyẹ̀wò àìsàn náà. Lẹ́yìn ìdámọ̀ lásìkò, àyẹ̀wò láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ ìdámọ̀ ajọ̀fẹ́ àrùn aṣokùnfà tí ó fa sẹ́púsì ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìtọ́jú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò àfojúsùn. Ìtọ́jú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò tí ó yẹ (funt àpẹẹrẹ, òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò bakitéríà àti òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò fọ́ńgáì) gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ fífún aláìsàn láàrin wákàtí kan ìdámọ̀ sẹ́púsì. Àìgbóògùn kòkòrò (AMR) lè mú ewu bá àkóso ìtọ́jú sẹ́púsì nítorí pé ìtọ́jú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò bakitéríà adálórí-ìrírí ni ó sáábà máa ń nílò. Fífa omi sára lásìkò ṣe pàtàkì ní ìpele ìsáájú bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè nílò àwọn òògùn amúsantẹ́ẹ́rẹ́ láti mú ìtẹ̀síwájú àti àkóso bá ìsànká inú ìṣù ara. Ìgbésẹ̀ méjì gbòógì ló wà kárí dènà sẹ́púsì: Ìdènà ìkóràn kòkòrò àìfojúrí àti àrùn Ìdènà àrùn tí ó ń di sẹ́púsì Láàárín àwùjọ, ìdènà sẹ́púsì ń rí ìrànlọ́wọ́ nípasẹ̀ ìlò àwọn iṣẹ́ ìmọ́tótó tí ó péye, ìlọsíwájú títún àyíká ṣe àti omi dídára àti wíwà, ìpèsè àǹfààní sí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, pàápàá jùlọ fún àwọn àwọn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ńlá, àti ìjẹunrekánú pípéye, tí ó fi mọ́ ìfọ́mọlọ́yàn fún àwọn ìkókó. Ìdènà ní àwọn ibùdó ìtọ́jú ìlera gbáralé àwọn ètò ìdènà àrùn àti àkóso (IAA), àwọn iṣẹ́ ìmọ́tótó tí ó péye, àti irinṣẹ́ mímọ́, tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa.
translations.reference: The detection of some biomarkers, such as C reactive protein and procalcitonin, can be effective in diagnosing the condition. After early recognition, diagnostics to help identify a causal pathogen of infection leading to sepsis are important to guide targeted antimicrobial treatment. The appropriate antimicrobial therapy (for example, antibiotics and antifungals) should be given within one hour of sepsis identification. Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) can jeopardize clinical management of sepsis because empirical antibiotic treatment is often required. Early fluid resuscitation is important in the initial phase and vasopressors may be required to improve and maintain tissue perfusion. There are two main steps to preventing sepsis: Prevention of microbial transmission and infection Prevention of an infection evolving into sepsis Within the community, sepsis prevention is assisted through the use of effective hygiene practices, improving sanitation and water quality and availability, providing access to vaccines, particularly for those at high risk, as well as appropriate nutrition, including breastfeeding for newborns. Prevention in health care facilities relies on infection prevention and control (IPC) programmes, effective hygiene practices, and clean, well-functioning equipment.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The management of infectious diseases, especially diseases like C in the hospital and precautions, can be complicated to assess the disease. After initial assessment, it is important to consider interventions for infectious disease control, especially those caused by fungi, to ensure proper drug management. Antimicrobial therapy (which should be given, for example, bacterial and fungal antimicrobial drugs) should be administered within the initial assessment period. Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) can complicate the management of sepsis because bacterial antimicrobial therapy is often required. Rapid fluid resuscitation is critical at the initial stage, and they may need supportive medications to advance and control the infection within the body. There are two main stages in sepsis: the stage of bacterial infection and the stage of sepsis disease. Within the community, sepsis is managed through the use of evidence-based interventions, continuous improvement, clean water and sanitation, emphasis on prevention, especially for those at high risk, and active surveillance, which provides information for forecasting. Sepsis management in healthcare facilities is supported by disease management and control programs (DMCP), evidence-based interventions, and effective communication."

translations.source: Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ Ìsọnísókí: Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ṣe pàtàkì fún gbogbo ìlera àti àlàáfíà gbogbogbò fún àwọn ènìyàn, tọkọtaya àti ẹbí, àti fún ìdàgbàsókè àwùjọ àti ọrọ̀ ajé ní àwọn àwùjọ àti orílẹ̀ èdè. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀, nígbà tí a bá wò ó ní àwòdájú, ó pè fún ìlànà dídára tí ó lọ́wọ̀ fún ìbáṣepọ̀ ìbálòpọ̀ àti takọtabo, àti ìṣeéṣe níní ìrírí ìbálòpọ̀ tí ó dùn tí ó sì láàbò, tí kò ní ipá, ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà àti ìwà ipá. Agbára tọkùnrin tobìnrin láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti àlàáfíà da lórí: àǹfààní sí alayst gbígbòòrò, dídára nípa ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìjákọ̀jábo; ìmọ̀ nípa àwọn ewu tí wọ́n lè kojú àti ìsíjúsí àwọn ìṣòro ńlá iṣẹ́ ìbálòpọ̀ aláìláàbò; agbára láti ní àǹfààní sí ìtọ́jú ìlera ìbálòpọ̀; gbígbé ní àyíká tí ń jẹ́rìí tí ó sì ń polongo ìlera ìbálòpọ̀. Àwọn ọ̀ràn ìlera ajẹmọ́-ìbálòpọ̀ pọ̀ ní onírúurú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ní ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìdánimọ̀ ìṣakọṣabo, àfihàn, ìbáṣepọ̀, àti ìgbádùn ìbálòpọ̀. Wọ́n tún ní àwọn àbájáde òdì tàbí ipò gẹ́gẹ́ bí:
translations.reference: Sexual health Overview: Sexual health is fundamental to the overall health and well-being of individuals, couples and families, and to the social and economic development of communities and countries. Sexual health, when viewed affirmatively, requires a positive and respectful approach to sexuality and sexual relationships, as well as the possibility of having pleasurable and safe sexual experiences, free of coercion, discrimination and violence. The ability of men and women to achieve sexual health and well-being depends on their: access to comprehensive, good-quality information about sex and sexuality; knowledge about the risks they may face and their vulnerability to adverse consequences of unprotected sexual activity; ability to access sexual health care; living in an environment that affirms and promotes sexual health. Sexual health-related issues are wide-ranging, and encompass sexual orientation and gender identity, sexual expression, relationships, and pleasure. They also include negative consequences or conditions such as:
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Comprehensive Health: The Importance of Sexual Health**

Comprehensive health is crucial for all aspects of health and well-being for individuals, families, and for the development of communities and economies within societies and nations. Comprehensive sexual health, when viewed holistically, calls for a specific approach that promotes safe sex and prevention, and addresses the challenges of enjoyable, safe, and fulfilling sexual experiences without harm, complications, or violence.

The key factors for men and women to achieve comprehensive sexual health and well-being are:

*   Access to comprehensive healthcare services
*   Knowledge about sexual health and reproductive issues; awareness of potential risks and prevention of major diseases related to sexual health
*   The ability to access sexual health services without stigma
*   The ability to advocate for sexual health
*   A strong commitment to promoting comprehensive sexual health.

Sexual health problems are diverse, and they include issues related to sexual health, identification of sexually transmitted infections, prevention, safe sex practices, and sexual satisfaction. They also include specific vulnerabilities or situations such as:

translations.source: àwọn àìsàn pẹ̀lú kòkòrò apasọ́jà ara (KASA), àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ (STIs) àti àwọn àrùn òpónà ìbísí (RTIs) àti àwọn àbájáde líle wọn (bí jẹjẹrẹ àti àìlèbímọ); oyún àìròtì àti ìṣẹ́yún; àìṣedéédé ìbálòpọ̀; ìwà ipá; àti àwọn àṣà tí ó léwu (gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìdábẹ́ fún obìnrin, IFO). Àwọn oríkì: WHO ti ń ṣiṣẹ́ ní agbègbè ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ láti ó kéré jù 1974, nígbà tí àwọn ìjíròrò àjọ alámọ̀já kan yọrí sí àtẹ̀jáde ìròyìn ìmọ̀ tí wọ́n pè ní "Education and treatment in Human sexuality" (WHO, 1975). Bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní 2000, Àjọ Ìsọ̀kan Ìlera America (PAHO) àti WHO ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn onírúurú ìmọ̀ràn alámọ̀já láti ṣe àtúnyẹ̀wò àwọn èdè ìperí àti ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn àṣàyàn ètò. Àbájáde àwọn ìpàdé wọ̀nyí ni Ìpàdé Ìmọ̀ràn 2002, níbi tí àwọn oríkì èdè ìperí ajẹmọ́ ìbálòpọ̀ tí wọ́n ń ṣàmúlò nísàlẹ̀ wọ̀nyí ti jẹ́ àgbékalẹ̀. Ìṣakọṣabo: Ìṣakọṣabo ni àwọn àbùdá ẹ̀dá tí ó ń tọ́ka ènìyàn gẹ́gẹ́ bí abo tàbí akọ. Nígbà tí ètò àwọn àbùdá ẹ̀dá wọ̀nyí lè wà nínú àgọ́ kan, nítorí pé àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní méjèèjì wà, wọ́n sáábà máa ń ṣe ìyàtọ̀ láàrin àwọn ẹ̀dá gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọkùnrin àti obìnrin.
translations.reference: infections with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), sexually transmitted infections (STIs) and reproductive tract infections (RTIs) and their adverse outcomes (such as cancer and infertility); unintended pregnancy and abortion; sexual dysfunction; sexual violence; and harmful practices (such as female genital mutilation, FGM). Definitions: WHO has been working in the area of sexual health since at least 1974, when the deliberations of an expert committee resulted in the publication of a technical report entitled “Education and treatment in human sexuality” (WHO, 1975). Starting in 2000, the Pan American Health Organization (PAHO) and WHO convened a number of expert consultations to review terminology and identify programme options. The culmination of these meetings was a Technical Consultation in 2002, where the working definitions of the key sexual health-related terms below were developed. Sex: Sex refers to the biological characteristics that define humans as female or male. While these sets of biological characteristics are not mutually exclusive, as there are individuals who possess both, they tend to differentiate humans as males and females.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Problems related to commercial sex workers (KASA), sexually transmitted infections (STIs) and respiratory tract infections (RTIs) and their consequences (such as HIV and infertility); drug abuse and addiction; unsafe sexual practices; drug abuse; and risky behaviors (especially for women, WHO).** The WHO has been working in the field of sexual health since at least 1974, when a group of experts produced an educational document called "Education and treatment in Human sexuality" (WHO, 1975). Starting in 2000, the Pan American Health Organization (PAHO) and WHO brought together various experts to review regional guidelines and develop program options. The outcome of these meetings was the 2002 Expert Consultation, where the regional guidelines for sexual health mentioned below were established.

**Terminology:** Terminology refers to the terms that refer to people, such as male or female. While the programs for these terms may exist within a community, there is a distinction made between the terms, such as male and female.

translations.source: Ní ìlò gbogbogbò nínú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn èdè, ọ̀rọ̀ ìperí ìṣakọṣabo ní wọ́n sáábà máa ń lò láti túmọ̀ sí "iṣẹ́ ìbálòpọ̀", ṣùgbọ́n fún iṣẹ́ ìmọ̀ nínú àwọn ìjíròrò ọ̀gangan ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ìjákọ̀jábo àti ìlera ìbálòpọ̀, oríkì òkè yìí dára jùlọ. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ Gẹ́gẹ́ bí oríkì tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ báyìí, ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ni:"...ipò ara, ìmọ̀lára, ipò ọkàn àti àlàáfíà ìbáraẹnilò ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú ìjákọ̀jábo; kì í ṣe àìsí àìsàn lásán, àìṣedéédé ìbálòpọ̀ àti àìlera. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ pè fún ìlànà dídára tí ó sì sé ìbọ̀wọ̀ fún ìjákọ̀jábo àti àwọn ìbáṣepọ̀ ìbáraẹnilòpọ̀, àti ìṣeéṣe láti àwọn ìrírí ìgbádùn ìbálòpọ̀ aláàbò, tí kò ní ìfipámú, ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà àti ìwà ipá. Fún ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ láti jẹ ìṣeéṣe àti ìṣàkóso, àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ìbálòpọ̀ fún gbogbo àwọn ènìyàn gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ ìbọ̀wọ̀fún, ìdáàbòbò àti àmúṣe." (WHO, 2006a). Ìjákọ̀jábo: Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ kò ṣe é kì, lóye tàbí ṣàmúlò láìsí àgbéyẹ̀wò gbòòrò ìjákọ̀jábo, èyí tí ó tọ́ka àwọn ìwà àti àbájáde pàtàkì tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìlera ìbálòpọ̀. Oríkì ìjákọ̀jábo tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ ni:"...àárín abala jíjẹ ẹ̀dá ènìyàn títí ayé ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìṣakọṣabo, àwọn ìdánimọ̀ ìjákọ̀jábo àti ojúṣe, àlàyé ìbálòpọ̀, ìmáradìde, ìgbádùn, ìbáṣepọ̀ àti ìbísí. Ìjákọ̀jábo máa ń wáyé bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó máa ń jẹ́ ìṣàfihàn nínú àwọn èrò, ìrònú, ìfẹ́ ọkàn, ìgbàgbọ́, ìwà, iyì, ìṣeṣí, ìṣe, ojúṣe àti àjọṣepọ̀. Nígbà tí ìjákọ̀jábo let ní gbogbo àwọn ìwọ̀n wọ̀nyí, kì í ṣe gbogbo wọn ni wọ́n ṣe é ní ìrírí tàbí ṣe àfihàn. Ìjákọ̀jábo máa jẹ́ okùnfà ìbáṣepọ̀ àwọn ìdí ajẹmẹ́dàá, ìròrí, àwùjọ, ọrọ̀ ajé, òṣèlú, àṣà, òfin, ìtàn, ẹ̀sìn àti ajẹmẹ́mìí.
translations.reference: In general use in many languages, the term sex is often used to mean “sexual activity”, but for technical purposes in the context of sexuality and sexual health discussions, the above definition is preferred. Sexual health According to the current working definition, sexual health is:“…a state of physical, emotional, mental and social well-being in relation to sexuality; it is not merely the absence of disease, dysfunction or infirmity. Sexual health requires a positive and respectful approach to sexuality and sexual relationships, as well as the possibility of having pleasurable and safe sexual experiences, free of coercion, discrimination and violence. For sexual health to be attained and maintained, the sexual rights of all persons must be respected, protected and fulfilled.” (WHO, 2006a) Sexuality: Sexual health cannot be defined, understood or made operational without a broad consideration of sexuality, which underlies important behaviours and outcomes related to sexual health. The working definition of sexuality is:“…a central aspect of being human throughout life encompasses sex, gender identities and roles, sexual orientation, eroticism, pleasure, intimacy and reproduction. Sexuality is experienced and expressed in thoughts, fantasies, desires, beliefs, attitudes, values, behaviours, practices, roles and relationships. While sexuality can include all of these dimensions, not all of them are always experienced or expressed. Sexuality is influenced by the interaction of biological, psychological, social, economic, political, cultural, legal, historical, religious and spiritual factors.” (WHO, 2006a)
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"In general discussions within many languages, the term 'social disease' is often used to translate "social issues," but for work involving scientific research in the areas of complex events of sexual transmission and social diseases, this term is more appropriate. Severe Social Disease, as the term currently in use, defines as: "...physical condition, mental health, psychological condition and social vulnerability in interaction with complex sexual behavior; it is not a sexually transmitted infection, social exclusion, or illness. Social disease refers to a specific process that is detrimental to complex sexual behavior and social vulnerability relationships, and to the inability to experience positive social well-being, without growth, joy, and integrity. For social disease to be prevented and controlled, the rights of social well-being for all people must be respected, guaranteed and promoted." (WHO, 2006a).

Complex Sexual Behavior: Social disease does not exist, nor is it created without considering complex sexual behavior, which points to the characteristics and consequences associated with social disease. The definition of complex sexual behavior in use is: "...the core of human existence from birth until death is associated with sexuality, sexual identity and expression, sexual relationships, intimacy, pleasure, connection and desire. Complex sexual behavior arises in this way and it manifests in thoughts, emotions, love, beliefs, values, morality, action, purpose, identity and relationships. When complex sexual behavior possesses all of these elements, not all of them are experienced or revealed. Complex sexual behavior is a fundamental relationship of ancestral origins, history, culture, economic issues, politics, law, stories, religion and intergenerational issues."

**Important Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Terminology:** The document uses specific terminology related to "social disease" and "complex sexual behavior" that doesn't have direct, simple equivalents in English. I've tried to convey the meaning as accurately as possible, sometimes using longer phrases to ensure clarity.
*   **Cultural Context:** The concepts discussed are deeply rooted in Yoruba culture and understanding of social and health issues. Some nuances might be difficult to fully capture in a translation without further explanation.
*   **WHO Reference:** The reference to the World Health Organization (WHO) suggests this document is likely related to public health or social welfare policies.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ẹ̀tọ́ Ìbáṣepọ̀: Ìfẹnukò káàkiri ń wáyé pé ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ kò lè ṣe é mú ṣẹ àti ṣe àkóso láìsí ìbọ̀wọ̀ fún, ìdáàbòbò bo, àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn pàtàkì. Oríkì tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún ẹ̀tọ́ ìbáṣepọ̀ tí ó wà nísàlẹ̀ yìí jẹ́ ìdásí ìtàkùrọ̀sọ lórí ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìlera ìbálòpọ̀. Àmúlò àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn sí ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìlera ní àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ìbáṣepọ̀. Ẹ̀tọ́ ìbáṣepọ̀ dáàbò bo gbogbo àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ènìyàn láti ṣe àmúṣe àti àfihàn ìjákọ̀jábo wọn àti láti gbádùn ìlera ìbálòpọ̀, pẹ̀lú àkíyèsí tí ó yẹ fún ẹ̀tọ́ àwọn ẹlòmíràn tí ó sì jẹ́ nínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìhun ìdáàbòbò tako ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà." (WHO, 2006a, tí wọ́n ṣe ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n ní 2010) "Ìmúṣẹ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ so mọ́ bí àwọn ènìyàn ṣe ń bọ̀wọ̀ fún, dáàbò bo àti ṣe ìmúṣẹ ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn. Ẹ̀tọ́ ìbáṣepọ̀ so mọ́ àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn tí wọ́n ti dá mọ̀ nínú àkọsílẹ̀ àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn lágbàáyé àti ní agbègbè àti àwọn ìwé àdéhùn mìíràn àti nínú àwọn òfin orílẹ̀ èdè. Àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ pàtàkì sí ìmúṣẹ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ni: Àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ pàtàkì sí ìmúṣẹ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ni: Àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ pàtàkì sí ìmúṣẹ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ni: Ẹ̀tọ́ sí ìdọ́gba àti àìsí ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà
translations.reference: Sexual rights: There is a growing consensus that sexual health cannot be achieved and maintained without respect for, and protection of, certain human rights. The working definition of sexual rights given below is a contribution to the continuing dialogue on human rights related to sexual health. The application of existing human rights to sexuality and sexual health constitute sexual rights. Sexual rights protect all people's rights to fulfil and express their sexuality and enjoy sexual health, with due regard for the rights of others and within a framework of protection against discrimination." (WHO, 2006a, updated 2010)“The fulfilment of sexual health is tied to the extent to which human rights are respected, protected and fulfilled. Sexual rights embrace certain human rights that are already recognized in international and regional human rights documents and other consensus documents and in national laws. Rights critical to the realization of sexual health include: Rights critical to the realization of sexual health include: Rights critical to the realization of sexual health include: the rights to equality and non-discrimination
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Right to Health: The prevailing understanding is that disease control and management cannot be achieved without consideration for, respect for, fundamental human rights.** The principle guiding the right to health as outlined here is the duty to inform on human rights issues related to disease. The integration of human rights into disease and health constitutes the right to health. The right to health obligates all human rights to promote and protect health and to enjoy health, with due regard for the rights of others and within a framework of public health, as extensively as possible. (WHO, 2006a, updated in 2010). Health promotion regarding disease informs people about how to access, respect and promote human rights. The right to health reinforces human rights that have been established in international human rights declarations and regional and national conventions and in the laws of individual countries. Key rights to disease prevention include: Key rights to disease prevention include: Key rights to disease prevention include: The right to life and freedom from discrimination.

translations.source: ẹ̀tọ́ láti ní òmìnira kúrò nínú ìdíyàjẹ tàbí ìwà ìkà, àìláàánú tàbí ìwà ìtẹ́ńbẹ́lú tàbí ìfìyàjẹni ẹ̀tọ́ sí àṣírí ẹ̀tọ́ sí òṣùwọ̀n ìlera tí ó ga jùlọ (tí ó fi mọ́ ìlera ìbálòpọ̀) àti ààbò àwùjọ. ẹ̀tọ́ láti gbéyàwó àti láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ẹbí àti láti wọnú ìgbéyàwó pẹ̀lú ìfọwọ́sí àwọn tọkọtaya ojú ọ̀nà ní ìrọwọ́rọsẹ̀ àti ní kíkún, àti fún ìdọ́gba nínú àti fún ìtúká ìgbéyàwó. ẹ̀tọ́ pinnu iye àwọn ọmọ àti àlàfo láàárín àwọn ọmọ ẹni. ẹ̀tọ́ sí ìròyìn, àti ẹ̀kọ́ ẹ̀tọ́ sí òmìnira ioinnut àti èrò, àti ẹ̀tọ́ ojútùú pípé fún àwọn ìrúfin àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn pàtàkì. Iṣẹ́ ojúṣe àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn pè fún gbogbo ènìyàn bọ̀wọ̀ fún àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ẹlòmíràn. Àwọn ọ̀ràn: "Àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn pèsè ìhun àgbékalẹ̀ káríayé láàrin èyí tí àìní ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìlera ìbísí àti àwọn ìfẹ́ gbogbo àwọn ènìyàn lè jẹ́ àkásí." (GRUSKIN, 2005).
translations.reference: the right to be free from torture or to cruel, inhumane or degrading treatment or punishment the right to privacy the rights to the highest attainable standard of health (including sexual health) and social security the right to marry and to found a family and enter into marriage with the free and full consent of the intending spouses, and to equality in and at the dissolution of marriage the right to decide the number and spacing of one's children the rights to information, as well as education the rights to freedom of opinion and expression, and the right to an effective remedy for violations of fundamental rights. The responsible exercise of human rights requires that all persons respect the rights of others. Issues: “Human rights provide an international legal framework within which the sexual and reproductive health needs and aspirations of all people can be considered.” (GRUSKIN, 2005)

"The right to freedom from torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment; the right to health, including reproductive health, and the highest attainable standard of health (compatible with the general condition of resources) and social security. The principles of human rights apply to all persons equally, regardless of other rights. translations.source: Ní àwọn ọdún lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ yìí, àwọn irinṣẹ́ ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn káríayé ti ń gbèrú nínú lílò láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àti láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá àṣẹ òfin láti ọwọ́ àwọn ènìyàn àti gbogbo àwùjọ kí àwọn ìjọba orílẹ̀ èdè lè fọwọ́sọ̀yà lórí ìbọ̀wọ̀fún, ààbò àti ìmúṣẹ ẹ̀tọ́ ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìlera ìbísí wọn (Cook et al., 2003). Àwọn oríkì tí wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́ àti ìhun àmúṣiṣẹ́ fún ìṣètò tí wọ́n ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ níhìn-ín fìdí kalẹ̀ lórí àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn tí wọ́n ṣe ìdámọ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé tí ó sì pèsè ìlànà adálórí-ẹ̀tọ́ láti ṣètò nínú ìlera ìbálòpọ̀. Àwọn èròjà àgbékalẹ̀ pàtàkì ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ni wọ́n wà nísàlẹ̀ yìí:. Àwọn èròjà àgbékalẹ̀ pàtàkì ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ Nígbà tí a bá wò ó lódidi àti lọ́nà tí ó dára: Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ dá lórí àlàáfíà, kì í ṣe àìsí àìsàn nìkan. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìbọ̀wọ̀fún, ààbò àti òmìnira kúrò lára ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà àti ìwà ipá. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ dá lórí ìmúṣẹ àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn kan. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ṣe pàtàkì títí ìgbésí ayé ẹ̀dá, kì í ṣe fún àwọn tí wọ́n wà ní àwọn ọdún ìbísí wọn, ṣùgbọ́n fún àwọn ọ̀dọ́ àti àgbàlagbà. Ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ ń jẹ́ ìṣàfihàn nípa ìjákọ̀jábo onírúurú àti àwọn ẹ̀yà ìṣàfihàn ìbálòpọ̀.
translations.reference: In recent years, international human rights instruments have been increasingly used to support and advance legal claims by individuals and whole communities so that national governments will guarantee the respect, protection and fulfilment of their sexual and reproductive health rights (Cook et al., 2003). The working definitions and framework for programming presented here are grounded in internationally recognized human rights and offer a rights-based approach to programming in sexual health. The key conceptual elements of sexual health are listed below. Key conceptual elements of sexual health When viewed holistically and positively: Sexual health is about well-being, not merely the absence of disease. Sexual health involves respect, safety and freedom from discrimination and violence. Sexual health depends on the fulfilment of certain human rights. Sexual health is relevant throughout the individual’s lifespan, not only to those in the reproductive years, but also to both the young and the elderly. Sexual health is expressed through diverse sexualities and forms of sexual expression.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"In recent years, international human rights norms have expanded in their application to provide support and advance legal remedies from individuals and society to governments, enabling them to focus on the provision, availability, and accessibility of reproductive and health services (Cook et al., 2003). The principles and guidelines for testing that were established in writing have been applied to human rights related to the monitoring of reproductive health. The key implementation challenges for reproductive health are as follows: Key implementation challenges for reproductive health When viewed closely and in the best way: Reproductive health is based on well-being, it is not just the absence of disease. Reproductive health involves the provision, availability, and autonomy from violence and harmful practices. Reproductive health is based on the realization of human rights. Reproductive health is critical throughout the life cycle, not just for those in their reproductive years, but for children and the elderly. Reproductive health is a reflection of the complexity of gender and the various dimensions of gender inequality."

translations.reference: Sexual health is critically influenced by gender norms, roles, expectations and power dynamics. Sexual health needs to be understood within specific social, economic and political contexts.

translations.source: Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ (STIs) Ìsọnísókí: Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ (STIs) máa ń tàn ka lọ́pọ̀ nípasẹ̀ ìbálòpọ̀ aláìláàbò. Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ díẹ̀ lè jẹ́ ìkóràn lásìkò ìlóyún, ìbímọ àti ìfọ́mọlọ́yàn àti nípasẹ̀ ẹ̀jẹ̀ àìmọ́ tàbí àwọn èròjà ẹ̀jẹ̀. Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ ní ipa pàtàkì lórí ìlera. Bí ó bá wà láìsí ìtọ́jú, wọ́n lè yọrí sí àwọn ìṣòro ńlá tí ó fi mọ́ àìsàn ajẹmópòónà òye àti ọkàn, àìlèbímọ, oyún ìta ilé ọmọ, àbíkú, àti ewu Kòkòrò Apasọ́jà ara (KASA) púpọ̀. Wọ́n tún ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìdẹ́yẹsí, ìwà ipá abẹ́lé, àti ìnípa lórí àbùdá ìgbésí ayé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn Àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ kì í ní àwọn àmì àìsàn. Nígbà tí wọ́n bá wà àwọn àmì àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni nǹkan tí ó ń jáde lójú ara tàbí òpónà ìtọ̀, ọgbẹ́ ojú ara àti ìrora abo ìsàlẹ̀ inú. Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ tí wọ́n sì ṣe é wò ni tirikomónà, aṣojú àrùn ìbálòpọ̀, àtọ̀sí àti sífílì. Àìgbóògùn àwọn kòkòrò bakitéríà tí wọ́n ń pọ̀ sí i jẹ ewu ìgbòòrò àrùn àtọ̀sí tí kò sè é wòsàn.
translations.reference: Sexually transmitted infections (STIs) Overview: Sexually transmitted infections (STIs) are spread predominantly by unprotected sexual contact. Some STIs can also be transmitted during pregnancy, childbirth and breastfeeding and through infected blood or blood products. STIs have a profound impact on health. If untreated, they can lead to serious consequences including neurological and cardiovascular disease, infertility, ectopic pregnancy, stillbirths, and increased risk of Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV). They are also associated with stigma, domestic violence, and affects quality of life. The majority of STIs have no symptoms. When they are present common symptoms of STIs are vaginal or urethral discharge, genital ulcer and lower abdominal pain. The most common and curable STIs are trichomonas, chlamydia, gonorrhoea and syphilis. Rapidly increasing antimicrobial resistance is a growing threat for untreatable gonorrhoea.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

**Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) Information:** Sexually transmitted infections (STIs) are commonly spread through unprotected sexual intercourse. Some STIs can cause eye infections, infertility, and complications during pregnancy, and through blood or bodily fluids. STIs have a significant impact on health. If left untreated, they can lead to serious complications such as Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID), ectopic pregnancy, infant mortality, death, and a high risk of HIV/AIDS. They can also cause psychological distress, social stigma, and affect quality of life. Many STIs have no symptoms. When symptoms do occur, common STI symptoms include discharge from the genitals or urethra, eye infections, and lower abdominal pain. The most common STIs that are easily treated are gonorrhea, chlamydia, syphilis, and herpes. Bacterial infections that are resistant to antibiotics pose a risk of antibiotic-resistant syphilis.

translations.source: Àwọn kòkòrò àrùn fáírọ́ọ̀sì ìbálòpọ̀ tí ó fi mọ́ KASA, kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì àrùn herpes, kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì àrùn jẹ̀dọ̀jẹ̀dọ̀ B, kòkòrò HPV àti kòkòrò HTLV-1 kò ní tàbí ní àwọn àṣàyàn tí kò. Àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára wà fún àrùn jẹ̀dọ̀jẹ̀dọ̀ B láti dènà àrùn tí ó lè fa jẹjẹrẹ ẹ̀dọ̀ àti fún HPV láti dènà jẹjẹrẹ àbáwọlé ilé ọmọ. KASA, HSV àti HTLV-1 jẹ́ àrùn títí ayé:fún KASA àti HSV àwọn ìtọ́jú tí wọ́n lè ki kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà mọ́lẹ̀, ṣùgbọ́n ní báyìí kò sí ìwòsàn fún èyíkéyìí àwọn kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ wọ̀nyí. Àwọn ọ̀rá ìdáàbòbò tí wọ́n lò lọ́nà tí ó tọ́ àti nígbà gbogbo jẹ́ àwọn ìlànà tí wọ́n kojúwọ̀n láti dáàbò boni lọ́wọ́ àwọn àrùn ibalopo àti KASA. Ṣíṣe àyẹ̀wò pẹ̀lú àyẹ̀wò àsìkò fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ àti àwọn alábàáṣepọ̀ wọn pèsè àǹfààní tí ó dára jùlọ fún ìtọ́jú tí ó múnádóko àti fún ìdènà àwọn ìṣòro àti ìkóràn síwájú sí i. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ẹnìkan lè ní àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ láìní àwọn àmì àìsàn náà tí wọ́n fojú hàn. Nígbà tí ó bá wà, lára àwọn àwọn àmì àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni: nǹkan tí ó ń jáde lójú ara ní àbaadì, nǹkan tí ó ń jáde ní òpónà ìtọ̀, àwọn ọgbẹ́ àti kókó ojú ara, àti ìrora ní ìsàlẹ̀ inú. Àwọn àmì àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ kọ̀ọ̀kan ni: Àrùn àtọ̀sí àti aṣojú àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ Àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ wọ̀nyí ń fa ìṣòro ẹnu ilé ọmọ, ìṣòro òpónà ìtọ̀ láàrin àwọn ọkùnrin àti àfikún àwọn àrùn ojú ara, tí ó fi mọ́ ìfarahàn ní ẹnu fùrọ̀ àti ọ̀nà ọ̀fun.
translations.reference: Viral STIs including HIV, genital herpes simplex virus (HSV), viral hepatitis B, human papillomavirus (HPV) and human T-lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1) lack or have limited treatment options. Vaccines are available for hepatitis B to prevent infection that can lead to liver cancer and for HPV to prevent cervical cancer. HIV, HSV and HTLV-1 are lifelong infections: for HIV and HSV there are treatments that can suppress the virus, but currently there are no cures for any of these viral STIs. Condoms used correctly and consistently are effective methods to protect against STIs and HIV. Screening with early diagnosis of people with STIs and their sexual partners offers the best opportunity for effective treatment and for preventing complications and further transmission. Symptoms: A person can have an STI without having obvious symptoms of disease. When present, common symptoms of STIs include: abnormal vaginal discharge, urethral discharge, genital ulcers and lumps, and lower abdominal pain. Symptoms of specific STIs are: Gonorrhoea and chlamydial infection These STIs cause cervicitis in women, urethritis in men and extra-genital infections, including rectal and oropharyngeal manifestations.

Vaccines are available for Hepatitis B to prevent infection and for HPV to prevent cervical cancer. HIV, HSV, and HTLV-1 are lifelong infections: For HIV and HSV, there are treatments that can control the viral load, but currently, there is no cure for any of these viral STIs. Prevention methods used consistently and correctly are the strategies recommended to prevent STIs and HIV. Regular screening with a rapid test for people with STIs and their partners is the best approach for early detection and prevention of complications and future infections. Symptoms: A person can have an STI without showing any of the common symptoms. When they do occur, common symptoms of STIs include sores or blisters on the genitals, rectum, mouth, or throat, and abdominal pain. Specific STI symptoms include: Syphilis and complications of STIs. These STIs cause inflammation of the cervix in women, urethritis in men, and sores on the genitals, which can lead to mouth ulcers and sore throats."
