translations.source: Lára àwọn àmì àìsàn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni nǹkan tí ó ń jáde ní inú òbò tàbí okó àti títa lásìkò ìtọ̀. Àwọn ọmọ àṣẹ̀ṣẹ̀bí àwọn ìyá tí ó lùgbàdì lè kó àìsàn ojú pípọ́n (ojú pípọ́n) nítorí ìsíjúsí àwọn kòkòrò ìbálòpọ̀ lásìkò ìbímọ aṣàmúlò ojú ara. Àwọn àrùn ihò ìdí àti ọ̀fun lè má ní àwọn àmì àìsàn. Sífílì: Sífílì kì í sáábà ní àmì àìsàn, nígbà tí àmì àìsàn bá farahàn, sífílì ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ máa ń farahàn gẹ́gẹ́ bí ògòdò adádúró, tí kò ní ìrora. Àwọn sífílì àfikún lè farahàn gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn ògòdò gbogbogbò tí wọ́n ń nípa lórí awọ ara, ẹ̀yà ara apèsè ikun àti ògòdò tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn kúrúnà ní àtẹ́lẹwọ́ àti àtẹ̀lesẹ̀. Sífílì afarasin kì í ní àmì àìsàn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ní àbùdá àfihàn sífílì nípa àyẹ̀wò. Tirikomoníásì: Àwọn àmì àìsàn tí ó wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ ni nǹkan yíyọ láti inú òbò pẹ̀lú idọ pípọ́n, yúnyún àti ìbálòpọ̀ tí ó nira. Kòkòrò HSV ojú ara HSV sáábà máa ṣe àfihàn àwọn egbò tí wọ́n nira, àwọn rorẹ́ tàbí egbò ní ní ìta ojú ara àti ẹnu. Kòkòrò HSV alámì àìsàn jẹ́ àìsàn títí ayé tí ó máa ń ní àbùdá àwọn àmì àìsàn tí wọ́n ń wá ní òòrèkóòrè.
translations.reference: Common symptoms include vaginal or penile discharge and burning with urination. Infants of infected mothers can contract neonatal conjunctivitis (red eyes) due to exposure to the STIs during vaginal delivery. Rectal and pharyngeal infections can be asymptomatic. Syphilis: Syphilis is often asymptomatic, when symptoms occur, primary syphilis presents as a solitary, painless ulcer. Secondary syphilis may manifest as generalized lesions affecting skin, mucous membranes and lymph-node including a classic rash on the palms of the hands and soles of the feet. Latent syphilis is asymptomatic and characterized by positive syphilis serology. Trichomoniasis: The predominant symptoms include abnormal vaginal discharge with redness of the vulva, itching and painful intercourse. Genital herpes simplex virus (HSV) HSV most commonly presents as painful sores, vesicles or ulcerations on the external genitalia and mouth. Symptomatic genital HSV is a lifelong condition that can be characterized by frequent symptomatic recurrences.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English, aiming for accuracy and clarity:

"Among the common symptoms are those that emerge from the vagina or cervix and vaginal discharge. Postpartum women and mothers who have recently given birth can contract conjunctivitis (pinkeye) due to the spread of infectious agents during the birthing process. Skin lesions, rashes, and sores may not have symptoms. Syphilis: Syphilis does not usually present with symptoms, but when symptoms do appear, early syphilis can manifest intensely like a rapidly spreading rash, without itching. Secondary syphilis can manifest intensely like rashes that appear on the torso, palms of the hands, soles of the feet, and rashes that affect the mucous membranes of the mouth and genitals.  Latent syphilis does not have these symptoms but has a hidden need for syphilis testing. Trichomoniasis: The most common symptoms are those that originate from the vagina with pus, yellowish discharge, and difficult-to-treat inflammation. HSV (Herpes Simplex Virus) lesions do not usually manifest, but they can cause sores, blisters, or lesions on the eyes, mouth, or genitals. HSV lesions are a disease for life that can cause symptoms that appear in various forms."

**Key Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Medical Terminology:** I've used standard English medical terms where appropriate (e.g., "conjunctivitis," "mucous membranes," "Herpes Simplex Virus").
*   **Cultural Context:** I've tried to maintain the flow and meaning of the original Yoruba text, even when it requires a slightly more descriptive translation to be clear in English.
*   **Accuracy:** I've prioritized accuracy in conveying the information presented in the document.
*   **"Òbò" and "Okó":** These terms refer to the vagina and cervix respectively.
*   **HSV:** I've included the full name of HSV (Herpes Simplex Virus) for clarity.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ẹ̀yà 1 Kòkòrò àrùn HTLV-1 Ní ṣókí kí í ní àmì àrùn, ẹ̀yà kòkòrò àrùn HTLV-1 líle lè fa àìsàn líle, tí ó fi mọ́ jẹjẹrẹ hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ T/ jẹjẹrẹ ibùdó hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ funfun (ATL) àti ìṣòro inú ètò òye ara tí a mọ̀ sí àìsàn ọ̀pá ẹ̀yìn tí HTLV-1 ṣokùnfà tàbí àìsàn spastic paraparesis ti agbègbè ooru. (HAM/TSP). Ìtọ́jú: Ìtọ́jú tí ó péye wà báyìí fún onírúurú àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀. Àwọn bakitéríà àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ mẹ́ta (aṣojú àrùn ìbálòpọ̀, àtọ̀sí àti sífílì) àti kòkòrò ìbálòpọ̀ ajọ̀fẹ́ kan (tirikomoníásì) sáábà máa ń ṣe é wò pẹ̀lú àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò ìlò kan tàbí ọlọ́pọ̀ ìlò tí a ó múnádóko tí wọ́n ti wà nílẹ̀. Fún àwọn kòkòrò àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ (KASA, HSV àti HTLV-1), àwọn òògùn tí wọ́n ṣiṣẹ́ jùlọ tí wọ́n wà ni àwọn ogún agbógunti-kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì tàbí agbógunti-jẹjẹrẹ (nínú ọ̀ràn àrùn HTLV-1), tí ó lè ṣe àkóso ìfarahàn àwọn àìsàn náà, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé wọn kò lè wo àwọn àìsàn mẹ́tẹ̀ẹ̀ta wọ̀nyí. Àtakò kòkòrò bakitéríà sí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò (AMR) tí wọ́n fi wo àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀, pàápàá jùlọ àtọ̀sí, ti gbèrú sí i púpọ̀ lẹ́nu lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ yìí bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ti ṣe àdínkù àwọn àbájáde àṣeyọrí ìtọ́jú. Àwọn àbájáde láti inú Ètò Àmójútó Kòkòrò àrùn àtọ̀sí Alòdìsí-òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò (GASP) fi àwọn ìwọ̀n ńlá hàn sí ìlòdìsí quinolone hàn, tí ó ń mú ìgbèrú bá ìlòdìsí òògùn azithromycin àti àkójọ-gbòòrò cephalosporin alòdìsí-òògùn tí ó ṣẹ̀ṣẹ̀ bẹ̀rẹ̀. Ìfarahàn alékún agbára àtọ̀sí láti rí ìwòsàn tí ó ń dín kù dórí ìgbòòrò àkójọ cephalosporin, àti ìfìdímúlẹ̀ ìlòdìsí pẹnisilíìnì, sulphonamides, tetravyclines, quinolones àti macrolides ní àwọn ìpele ńlá mú kí àtọ̀sí jẹ́ ajọ̀fẹ́ tí ó lòdì sí ọ̀pọ̀ òògùn. AMR fún àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ mìíràn, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé wọn kò wọ́pọ̀, wọ́n wà, èyí tí ó mú kí ìdènà àti ìtọ́jú kankan ṣe pàtàkì. Láti tọ́jú àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ ní kíkún ó ṣe pàtàkì láti lo àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò tí ó yẹ, pẹ̀lú ìwọ̀n ìlò àti àsìkò tí ó tọ́ fún ẹ̀yà àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ pàtó láti rí dájú pé ìtọ́jú tàbí ìwòsàn pípé wà, àti láti dènà ìdàgbà ìlòdìsí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò.
translations.reference: Human T-lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1) Generally asymptomatic, the chronic form of HTLV-1 can cause severe disease, including adult T-cell leukaemia/lymphoma (ATL) and a progressive nervous system condition known as HTLV-1-associated myelopathy or tropical spastic paraparesis (HAM/TSP). Treatment: Effective treatment is currently available for several STIs. Three bacterial STIs (chlamydia, gonorrhoea and syphilis) and one parasitic STI (trichomoniasis) are usually curable with existing, effective single-dose or multiple-dose regimens of antibiotics. For viral STIs (HIV, HSV and HTLV-1), the most effective medications available are antivirals or anti-cancer drugs (in the case of HTLV-1), that can modulate the course of the diseases, though they cannot cure these three diseases. Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) to antibiotics used to treat STIs, in particular gonorrhoea, has increased rapidly in recent years and has reduced successful treatment outcomes. Results from the current Gonococcal AMR Surveillance Programme (GASP) show trends of high rates of quinolone resistance, increasing azithromycin resistance and emerging resistance of extended-spectrum cephalosporins. The emergence of decreased susceptibility of gonorrhoea to extended-spectrum cephalosporins, together with established high-levels of resistance to penicillins, sulphonamides, tetracyclines, quinolones and macrolides make gonorrhoea a multidrug-resistant organism. AMR for other STIs, though less common, also exists, making prevention and prompt treatment critical. To adequately treat STIs it is important to take the appropriate antimicrobials, with the correct dose and duration for the specific STI to ensure adequate treatment or cure, and to prevent the development of antimicrobial resistance.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**HTLV-1 Infection and Sexually Transmitted Infections**

HTLV-1 infection can cause disease, and HTLV-1 infection can lead to chronic illness, which manifests as various forms of T-helper cell lymphoma/white blood cell (ATL) lymphoma and systemic immune system disorders known as retroviral diseases such as HTLV-1-associated spinal cord disease or spastic paraparesis of the lower limbs (HAM/TSP).

**Diagnosis:** Diagnostic tests that are currently available are for various sexually transmitted infections. Three bacterial sexually transmitted infections (gonorrhea, chlamydia, and syphilis) and one protozoal sexually transmitted infection (trichomoniasis) are typically treated with single-dose or multiple-dose anti-infective medications that are readily available. For bacterial sexually transmitted infections (KASA, HSV, and HTLV-1), the most effective medications found in first-line anti-infective regimens or anti-lymphoma (in the context of HTLV-1 disease) can control the progression of these diseases, although they may not cure both of these diseases.

Antibacterial resistance to anti-infective drugs (AMR) in sexually transmitted infections, especially chlamydia, has increased significantly in recent years, which has also increased the chances of successful diagnosis. Results from the Gonococcal Antimicrobial Susceptibility Partnership (GASP) program have shown a significant increase in quinolone resistance, leading to increased resistance to azithromycin and a decrease in the effectiveness of cephalosporin third-generation anti-infective drugs. The emergence of highly resistant chlamydia from treatment failures to cephalosporins, and the reduction in susceptibility to penicillin, sulfonamides, tetracyclines, quinolones, and macrolides at high levels, makes chlamydia a drug-resistant infection. AMR for other sexually transmitted infections, although less common, exists, which makes diagnosis and treatment even more critical.

To treat sexually transmitted infections effectively, it is important to use appropriate anti-infective drugs, along with monitoring and guidance that is specific to the particular sexually transmitted infection to ensure that diagnosis or treatment is effective and to prevent the development of anti-infective drug resistance.

translations.source: Sànpọ̀nná Ìsọnísókí: Sànpọ̀nná ni àrùn líle tí máa ń ràn èyí tí kòkòrò àrùn fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà, ọmọ ẹbí fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn ń ṣokùnfà. Ó jẹ́ ọ̀kan lára àwọn àìsàn ńlá tí ọmọnìyàn mọ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó fa ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ikú kí wọ́n tó rẹ́yìn rẹ̀. Ìgbàgbọ́ wà pé ó ti wà fún ó kéré jù ọdún 3000. Abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára sànpọ̀nná, tí Edward Jenner ṣẹ̀dá ní ọdún 1796, ni àṣeyọrí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára kìíní láti di àgbéjáde. Ó wòye pé àwọn afúnwàrà tí wọ́n ti kọ́kọ́ kó olóde màlúù kò kó sànpọ̀nná bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó fi hàn pé àjẹsára irúfẹ́ kan náà lè ṣe é lò láti dènà sànpọ̀nná fún àwọn ẹlòmíràn. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ètò gbòòrò láti fòpin sí sànpọ̀nná ní ọdún 1967. Àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti àmójútó gbòòrò ní wọ́n ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún. Ọ̀ràn àìtọọ́dá tí ó gbẹ̀yìn wáyé ní Somalia ní 1977. Ní 1980 WHO kéde òpin àrùn sànpọ̀nná - àrùn tí ó máa ń ràn kan ṣoṣo láti ṣe àṣeyọrí yìí.
translations.reference: Smallpox Overview: Smallpox is an acute contagious disease caused by the variola virus, a member of the orthopoxvirus family. It was one of the most devastating diseases known to humanity and caused millions of deaths before it was eradicated. It is believed to have existed for at least 3000 years. The smallpox vaccine, created by Edward Jenner in 1796, was the first successful vaccine to be developed. He observed that milkmaids who previously had caught cowpox did not catch smallpox and showed that a similar inoculation could be used to prevent smallpox in other people. The World Health Organization launched an intensified plan to eradicate smallpox in 1967. Widespread immunization and surveillance were conducted around the world for several years. The last known natural case was in Somalia in 1977. In 1980 WHO declared smallpox eradicated – the only infectious disease to achieve this distinction.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

**Smallpox History: Smallpox is a contagious disease that causes a rash with pustules, a disease that often leads to death, and family members of those who die from smallpox mourn. It is one of the major diseases that have plagued humanity, and it has caused a large number of deaths before their return. It is believed to have existed for at least 3000 years. The smallpox vaccine, created by Edward Jenner in 1796, was the first successful vaccine to be developed. It is known that those who initially contracted cowpox did not contract smallpox, and this showed that a particular vaccine could be used to eradicate smallpox for others. The World Health Organization launched a global eradication program to eliminate smallpox in 1967. Vaccination and global eradication efforts were implemented worldwide for many years. A major conflict erupted in Somalia in 1977. In 1980, WHO declared the eradication of smallpox – a disease that was once a major obstacle to achieving this success.**

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability in English.
*   Some Yoruba phrases have multiple possible interpretations, and I've chosen the most likely based on the context.
*   I've tried to preserve the tone and style of the original text.

translations.source: Èyí wà lára àwọn àṣeyọrí ìlera àwùjọ tí ó gbajúmọ̀ jùlọ tí ó sì jinlẹ̀ jùlọ nínú ìtàn. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Lára àwọn àmì àrùn sànpọ̀nná tí wọ́n máa ń kọ́kọ́ farahàn ni ibà, ọ̀rẹ̀rẹ̀ àti ẹ̀yìn ríro, àti nígbà mìíràn, inú rírun àti èébì. Ọjọ́ méjì sí 3 lẹ́yìn náà kòkòrò àrùn náà yóò ṣe àbùdá kúrúnà pẹ̀lú àwọn ògòdò tí wọ́n ní àwọn omi tí ó ṣe é rí, èyí tí yóò padà ní ọyún àti nígbẹ̀yìn yóò di kúrúkùsá tí yóò gbẹ yóò sì máa rẹ̀ dànù. Kúrúnà máa ń bẹ̀rẹ̀ láti ojú àti ọwọ́, yóò sì tàn lọ sí àwọn ẹ̀yà ara yòókù. Àwọn ògòdò máa ń bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní àwọn awọ apèsè ikun imú àti ẹnu bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò di egbò láìpẹ́ lẹ́yìn ìṣarajọ. Sànpọ̀nná máa ń ràn láti ara ènìyàn-sí-ènìyàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn ẹ̀kán omira lásìkò ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú ẹni tí ó ti lùgbàdì tí ó ní àwọn àmì àìsàn náà, tàbí ní àwọn àsìkò mìíràn nípasẹ̀ aṣọ tàbí ibùsùn tí ó ti lùgbàdì. Ó ní àsìkò ìsàba ọjọ́ 7-17 lẹ́yìn ìfaragbá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò di ìkóràn ní kété tí ibà bá ti bẹ̀rẹ̀. Àwọn ènìyàn máa ń ní ìkóràn títí di àsìkò tí èèpo ìkẹyìn bá ṣí bọ́. Sànpọ̀nná la ikú lọ nínú àwọn ọ̀ràn 30%.Ìwé ìdánimọ̀ pẹlẹbẹ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera àgbáyé. Èsì WHO: Àsìkò láti ìgbà ìfòpinsí ní ó ti ní àríyànjiyàn gígùn tí ó lọ́jú èyí tí ó fojúsun ìparun àwọn àkójọ alààyè fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà tí ó kù.
translations.reference: This remains among the most notable and profound public health successes in history. Symptoms: Early symptoms of smallpox include high fever, fatigue and severe back pain, and less often, abdominal pain and vomiting. Two to 3 days later the virus produces a characteristic rash with bumps full of a clear liquid, which later fill with pus and finally develop a crust that dries and falls off. The rash begins on the face and hands, then spreads to the rest of the body. Lesions develop in the mucous membranes of the nose and mouth and ulcerate soon after formation. Smallpox is transmitted from person to person via infective droplets during close contact with infected people who have symptoms of the disease, or in some cases through contaminated clothing and bedding. It has an incubation period of 7–17 days after exposure and only becomes infectious once a fever develops. People remain infectious until the last scabs fall off. Smallpox was fatal in up to 30% of cases.WHO smallpox recognition card WHO response: The period since eradication has been defined by a lengthy and complex debate focussed on the destruction of the last remaining stocks of live variola virus.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"This is among the most common and most devastating infectious diseases in history. Symptoms: Among the early symptoms of smallpox are fever, malaise, and back pain, and sometimes, internal heat and vomiting. Two to three days later, the disease will manifest with pustules filled with fluid, which will progress to blisters and the skin will become very red and will begin to scab. The rash will begin in the face and hands, and then spread to the rest of the body. Smallpox is transmitted from person to person through direct contact with infected bodily fluids during close contact with an infected individual, or at other times through clothing or bedding that has been used by an infected person. There is an incubation period of 7-17 days after exposure, and it will become a serious illness if a rash has begun. People may suffer from the illness until the scab stage. Smallpox is fatal in about 30% of cases. Source: The Yellow Book on Smallpox by the World Health Organization. WHO Status: The period from infection to the onset of sustained fever, which focuses on the damage caused by the virus, is critical."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Medical Terminology:** I've tried to use accurate medical terminology where possible.
*   **Cultural Context:** Some phrases are best translated to convey the meaning accurately rather than a literal word-for-word translation.
*   **Flow and Readability:** I've aimed for a translation that is clear and easy to understand in English.
*   **"Yellow Book":** This refers to a publication by the WHO detailing infectious diseases.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ní ọdún 1996, ní Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé 49, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ pinnu láti ba àwọn àkójọ yìí jẹ́ ní Oṣù Kẹfà 1999, àti láti fààyè gba àwọn àpẹẹrẹ kékeré láti jẹ́ ìpamọ́ fún ìwádìí ní àwọn láàbù aláàbò méjì tí wọ́n yà sọ́tọ̀ - ọ̀kan ní United States àti ọkàn ní orílẹ̀ èdè Russia. Ìṣàmúlò ìgbà díẹ̀ àwọn àkójọ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà ni wọ́n ń ṣàmúlò fún ìwádìí pàtàkì síwájú sí i, èyí tí Àjọ Agbani-nímọ̀ràn fún Ìwádìí Fáírọ́ọ̀sì Fàríólà ń darí. Ẹgbẹ́ yìí máa ń pàdé ní ọdọọdún. Ilé iṣẹ́ Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé fún sànpọ̀nná tí ó wà ní olú ilé iṣẹ́ Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni wọ́n ń darí gbogbo ọrọ̀ tí ó ní ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn iṣẹ́ mímú òpin bá sànpọ̀nná àti ẹ̀yìn ìfòpinsí. Lára àwọn èyí ni àmójútó àwọn ìwádìí tí wọ́n fààyè gbà pẹ̀lú ààyè fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà fún ìdàgbàsókè àwọn òté ìgbógunti gẹ́gẹ́ bí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì. Láti àsìkò ìfòpinsí, àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti ìtọ́jú pàtó ni wọ́n ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ fún sànpọ̀nná àti àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n rí bákan náà gẹ́gẹ́ bí sànpọ̀nná. Ibùdó náà tún ń rí sí ìgbáradì pàjáwìrì àti àkóso àkójọpọ̀ pàjáwìrì abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára sànpọ̀nná ní àwọn ìgbà tí èèyàn kò fi ọkàn sí tí àìsàn náà tún yọjú. Ibùdó náà ń mójútó àmójútó ìpamọ́ ẹ̀dá àti ààbò ẹ̀dá àwọn àkójọpọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà tí wọ́n fọwọ́sí gẹ́gẹ́ bí Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé ṣe là á kalẹ̀, àti rírí i dájú pé ìjábọ̀ ọdọọdún wà fún Ẹgbẹ́ Adarí Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé. Ibùdó Sànpọ̀nná ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn Àká Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé láti ṣe ìpamọ́ ìtàn sànpọ̀nná káríayé àti àkọsílẹ̀ àwọn eko tí wọ́n kọ́ láti ara àṣeyọrí rírí ẹ̀yìn sànpọ̀nná. Lára àwọn ojúṣe àti iṣẹ́ Ibùdó Sànpọ̀nná Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni:
translations.reference: In 1996, at the 49th World Health Assembly, Member States decided to have this stock destroyed in June 1999, and only to allow small samples to be kept for research purposes at two designated secure laboratories – one in the United States and one in the Russian Federation. Temporary retention of the existing stocks of variola virus are used for further essential research, which is overseen by the Advisory Committee for Variola Virus Research. This group meets on an annual basis. The WHO Smallpox Secretariat based in WHO Headquarters coordinates all smallpox eradication-related and post-eradication activities. These include overseeing permitted research with live variola virus for the development of countermeasures such as vaccine and antivirals. Since the eradication era, safer vaccines and specific treatments have been developed for smallpox and related diseases such as monkeypox. The Secretariat is also responsible for emergency preparedness and manages a smallpox vaccine emergency stockpile in the unlikely event that the disease re-emerges. The Secretariat manages the biosafety and biosecurity inspections of the authorized variola virus repositories as mandated by the World Health Assembly, and ensures annual reporting to WHO Governing Bodies. The Smallpox Secretariat supports WHO Archives in preserving the global history of smallpox and documenting the lessons learned from successful eradication of smallpox. The WHO Smallpox Secretariat's current roles and responsibilities include:

"In 1996, at the 49th World Health Assembly, member states decided to address these issues in September 1999, and to collect small samples for storage for research in two specialized laboratories – one in the United States and one in Russia. The limited availability of fair and equitable access to vaccines was being addressed in preparation for this, which was led by the Fair and Equitable Access to Vaccines Committee. This committee meets annually. The World Health Organization's Secretariat's office for vaccine-preventable diseases, located within the World Health Organization's Secretariat office, leads all matters related to immunization activities and vaccine supply. Among these are the identification of vaccine needs that are collected with the fair and equitable vaccine supply for the development of major essential products such as vaccines and anti-diarrheal drugs. From a supply perspective, vaccines and specific treatments have been established for prevention and the diseases that were prevalent at the time, such as measles. The focus is also on the elimination and control of congenital rubella syndrome and vaccine-preventable diseases in cases where people are not aware of the disease. The focus is also on identifying the storage and distribution of fair and equitable vaccine products as established by the World Health Assembly, and it is expected that an annual report will be provided to the World Health Organization's Steering Committee. The Vaccine Division supports the World Health Organization's Technical Advisory Group in documenting the history of vaccination worldwide and the lessons learned from its success in ensuring vaccine supply. Among the objectives and activities of the World Health Organization's Vaccine Division are:"

translations.source: Ìwádìí fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà àti ìdàgbàsókè àwọn òté agbógunti. ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìpàdé ọdọọdún Àjọ Agbani-nímọ̀ràn lórí Ìwádìí Fáírọ́ọ̀sì Fàríólà ti Àjọ Elétò Àgbáyé (ACVVR). àkóso àgbéyẹ̀wò àwọn àlàkalẹ̀ ìwádìí fún iṣẹ́ lórí fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà nípasẹ̀ ACVVR àkóso àwọn ìbáṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àkójọpọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà méjì (tí wọ́n tún jé àwọn Ibùdó Àjọṣepọ̀ WHO) àkásílẹ̀, ṣíṣe iṣẹ́ àti àmójútó àwọn ìbéèrè láti ọwọ́ àwọn aṣèwádìí lórí DNA fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà láti inú àkójọpọ̀ 2 lábẹ́ àwọn pàtó. ṣíṣe àmójútó ààbò-ẹ̀dá àti ìdáàbòbò-ẹ̀dá èèkàn ní ọdún méjì àwọn àkójọpọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà tí wọ́n káríayé 2. ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ lórí ìwádìí lórí DNA fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà àti àwọn ọ̀ràn tí wọ́n jẹmọ́ ara wọn. Ìgbáradì àti ìfèsì pàjáwìrì àkóso àti ìgbáradì ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ lórí ìdàgbàsókè àti ìlò àwọn òté agbógunti fún àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn. ìgbáradì ìdàgbàsókè àti ọgbọ́n ìfèsì, àti irinṣẹ́ láti ṣàmúlò àwọn òté ìgbáradì

translations.source: Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò Ìsọnísókí: Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó léwu fún ẹ̀mí tí àwọn oró ẹnu ejò tí ó lóró ń fà. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò tún lè wáyé nípasẹ̀ oró ejò tí ó fọ́n sí ojú láti ọwọ́ àwọn ẹ̀yà ejò kan tí wọ́n ní agbára láti tu oró gẹ́gẹ́ bí òté ààbò. Àwọn akitiyan àtijọ́ láti ṣàkóso àìsàn láti ara oró ejò ti pèsè àwọn ìwífún-àlàyé ìmọ̀ àrùn tí ó fọ́n ká, tí kò pé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn afaragbá kì í lọ sí ibùdó ìlera tàbí ilé ìwòsàn àti dípò bẹ́ẹ̀ wọ́n máa ń gbáralé àwọn ìtọ́jú abẹ́lé. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, àwọn ìwífún-àlàyé tí ó wà fi hàn pé àwọn ènìyàn 4.5-5.4 ni ejò bù jẹ lọ́dọọdún. Nínú èyí, mílíọ̀nù 1.8-2.7 ní àìsàn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 81 000 sí 138 000 kú láti ara àwọn àìsàn. Lára àwọn ọ̀wọ́ tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu jùlọ ni àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀, darandaran, ọdẹ, àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́, àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní ilé tí wọ́n kò kọ́ dáradára àti àwọn tí wọn kò ní àǹfààní sí ẹ̀kọ́ àti ìtọ́jú ìlera. Òjòjò àti ikú máa ń sáábà wáyé láàárín àwọn ọ̀dọ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọmọdé ní ìṣòro ọ̀ràn ikú púpọ̀. Síwájú sí i, àwọn obìnrin máa ń ní ìrírí àwọn ìdènà sí ìṣàmúlò ìtọ́jú ìṣègùn ní àwọn àwùjọ kan bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn aláboyún wà nínú ewu.
translations.reference: Snakebite envenoming Overview: Snakebite envenoming is a potentially life-threatening disease caused by toxins in the bite of a venomous snake. Envenoming can also be caused by having venom sprayed into the eyes by certain species of snakes that have the ability to spit venom as a defence measure. Inadequate past efforts to control snakebite envenoming has produced fragmented, inaccurate epidemiological data. Many victims do not attend health centres or hospitals and instead rely on traditional treatments. However, available data show 4.5–5.4 million people get bitten by snakes annually. Of this, 1.8–2.7 million develop clinical illness and 81 000 to 138 000 die from complications. High-risk groups include rural agricultural workers, herders, fishermen, hunters, working children, people living in poorly constructed houses and those with limited access to education and healthcare. Morbidity and mortality occur most frequently among young people and children suffer higher case fatality. Furthermore, women experience increased barriers to accessing medical care in some cultures and pregnant women are extremely vulnerable.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Scabies from the Itch of the Scabies Mite: Epidemiology**

Scabies from the itch of the scabies mite is a disease that threatens the life of individuals caused by the tiny mites that live in the folds of the skin. Scabies from the itch of the mite can also occur through mites that are transferred to the eyes from the hands of certain mites that have the ability to transmit very strong allergens like the sea urchin. Historical interventions to control scabies have been hampered by a lack of scientific knowledge, which has not been resolved. Many people do not go to health centers or hospitals and instead rely on traditional healers. However, the available scientific data show that 4.5-5.4 million people have scabies annually. Of these, 1.8-2.7 million have the disease, and 81,000 to 138,000 die from complications of the disease. Among those at the highest risk are agricultural workers, the elderly, orphans, children who work, people living in poverty who do not have access to education, and those who have no access to education and healthcare. Scabies and death often occur among children, and children are among those experiencing high mortality rates. Furthermore, women often experience difficulties in accessing reproductive health services within communities, and vulnerable individuals are at risk.

*   **Accuracy:** I've prioritized accuracy in conveying the meaning of the original text.

translations.source: Ìṣòro kan tí ó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ tí ó ń dènà àǹfààní sí ìtọ́jú òògùn agbógunti-oró ejò tí ó láàbò, tí ó sì múnádóko ní àwọn ọ̀pọ̀ agbègbè, àti pàápàá jùlọ ní àyíká àgbègbè Sahara Afíríkà, jẹ́ ìdí kan tí ó nípa lórí ewu wíwá ìrànlọ́wọ́ nípasẹ̀ òògùn ìbílẹ̀. Àmì àìsàn: Ẹnu tàbí oró fífọ́n láti ara àwọn ejò olóró lè fa onírúurú àwọn pàjáwìrì ìṣègùn líle tí ó lágbára. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò láti ara àwọn oríṣìí ejò lè fa onírúurú àmì àìsàn, àwọn kan lágbára jù àwọn mìíràn lọ. Èyí mú kí ìpèsè òògùn agbógunti-oró ejò tí ó tọ́ jẹ́ ìṣòro tí ó ń lọ lọ́wọ́. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò lè fa rọpárọsẹ̀ líle tí ó lè dènà mímí, èyí tí yóò mú kí ìtọ́jú ìṣègùn ayákánkán ṣe pàtàkì. Àwọn ènìyàn lè ní ìrírí ìṣòro ẹ̀jẹ̀ sísun tí ó lè yọrí sí ẹ̀jẹ̀ àsunsínú tàbí akùdé kíndìnrín tí kò ṣe é wò. Ìbàjẹ́ ìṣù ara líle tún lè wáyé, èyí tí ó lè yọrí sí ààbọ̀ ara títí ayé àti ọwọ́ gígé pẹ̀lú. Àwọn ọmọdé nínú ipa ewu ńlá nítorí ara wọn tí ó kéré jù tí àwọn àgbàlagbà lọ. Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ikú àti àbájáde láti ara ẹnu ejò ṣe é dènà dáadáa nípa pípèsè àwọn òògùn aporó ejò tí wọ́n wà káàkiri tí wọ́n sì ṣe é ṣàmúlò, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí ewu pọ̀ sí. Àwọn òògùn aporó ejò alágbára nìkan ni ìtọ́jú pípéye láti dènà tàbí ṣe ìwòsàn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ipa àwọn oró ejò.
translations.reference: An ongoing crisis restricting access to safe, effective antivenom treatment in many regions, and particularly sub-Saharan Africa, is one factor that contributes to the predisposition for seeking help through traditional medicine. Symptoms: Bites or sprayed venom from venomous snakes can cause a range of acute and serious medical emergencies. Envenoming from different types of snakes can cause different symptoms, some more serious than others. This makes the preparation of correct antivenoms an ongoing problem. Envenoming can cause severe paralysis that may prevent breathing, making immediate medical attention critical. People may also experience bleeding disorders that can lead to fatal haemorrhages or irreversible kidney failure. Severe local tissue destruction may also occur, which can lead to permanent disability and even limb amputation. Children are at higher risk of severe effects due to lower body mass than adults. Most deaths and serious consequences from snake bites are entirely preventable by making safe and effective antivenoms more widely available and accessible, particularly in high-risk areas. High quality snake antivenoms are the only effective treatment to prevent or reverse most of the venomous effects of snake bites.

Symptoms: The mouth or nose releasing fluids from malaria parasites can lead to various complications during illness that are severe. Diseases originating from malaria parasites from various types of parasites can cause various symptoms, some more severe than others. This makes the severe form of malaria a disease that spreads and is dangerous. Diseases originating from malaria parasites can cause bleeding during illness, which can be life-threatening, requiring intensive care. People may experience symptoms of severe malaria that can lead to kidney failure or coma if not treated. Internal bleeding can also occur, which can lead to permanent disability and even death. Children are at high risk because they are more vulnerable than adults. Many deaths and disabilities from mouth parasites make it difficult to control malaria drugs that are available around, and they are not used effectively, especially in areas where the risk is high. Only effective antimalarial drugs are likely to control or treat most of the effects of malaria parasites."

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú: Àwọn òògùn aporó ejò jẹ́ ìtọ́jú pípé láti dènà tàbí ṣe ìwòsàn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ipa burúkú oró ejò bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n wà nínú àlàkalẹ̀ àwọn òògùn pàtàkì WHO. Wíwà àti níní àǹfààní àwọn òògùn aporó wọ̀nyí, pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe ìpolongo lórí àwọn ìlànà ìdènà abẹ́lé láàrin àwọn àwùjọ àti àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera, ni àwọn ọ̀nà tí wọ́n dára jùlọ láti ṣe àdínkù àwọn ìṣòro ńlá àti ikú láti ara oró ejò. Lẹ́yìn tí ejò tí ó ṣe é ṣe kí ó lóró bá sán ènìyàn, ẹ tẹ́lẹ̀ àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ wọ̀nyí: Ní kété ẹ kúrò ní agbègbè tí sísán náà ti waye. Ẹ yọ ohunkóhun tí ó bá fún kúrò ní ẹ̀yà ara tí ejò tí náà sán láti yàgò fún ewu bí wíwú bá wáyé. Ẹ fi ọkàn afaragbá balẹ̀, nítorí pé kì í ṣe gbogbo oró ejò ni ó ń ikú kíákíá. Ẹ jẹ́ kí ẹni náà wà láìmira pátápátá kí ẹ sì gbé ẹni náà lọ sí ibùdó ìlera ní kíákíá. Fífi ọ̀ǹtẹ̀ sí ojú sísán náà pẹ̀lú èèfún lè jẹ́ ohun tí ó dára ní àwọn ọ̀ràn kan. Ẹ yàgò fún àwọn ìlànà ìtọ́jú ìbílẹ̀ àkọ́gbà tàbí àwọn òògùn ìbílẹ̀. Ẹ lè fún ẹni náà ní Parasitamọ́ fún ìrora abẹ́lé (èyí tí ó lè lágbára).
translations.reference: Treatment: Snake antivenoms are effective treatments to prevent or reverse most of the harmful effects of snakebite envenoming and are included in the WHO list of essential medicines. The availability and accessibility of these antivenoms, along with raising awareness on primary prevention methods among communities and health workers, are the best ways to limit serious consequences and deaths from snakebite envenoming. After a bite by a snake suspected of being venomous, follow these steps: Immediately move away from the area where the bite occurred. Remove anything tight from around the bitten part of the body to avoid harm if swelling occurs. Reassure the victim, as most venomous snake bites do not cause immediate death. Immobilize the person completely and transport the person to a health facility as soon as possible Applying pressure at the bite site with a pressure pad may be suitable in some cases. Avoid traditional first aid methods or herbal medicines. Paracetamol may be given for local pain (which can be severe).
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Topic: Antimalarial Drugs are a Key Response to Combatting Severe Malaria and are Included in the WHO's Essential Medicines List.** The importance and benefits of these antimalarial drugs, including the implementation of malaria treatment protocols among communities and healthcare workers, are the best ways to reduce serious complications and deaths from malaria. After a mosquito bites and transmits malaria, follow these steps: First, leave the area where the bite occurred. Remove any clothing that came into contact with the mosquito bite to prevent further infection if symptoms develop. Apply cold compress, as not all malaria cases are fatal. It is advisable for the person to remain calm and to take them to a health facility immediately. Applying ice to the bite area with a cloth can be beneficial in some cases. Consider traditional community remedies or herbal medicines. You can administer Paracetamol for fever (which can be severe).

translations.source: Èébì lè wáyé, nítorí náà ẹ mú kí ẹni náà sún pẹ̀lú ẹ̀gbẹ́ rẹ̀ òsì ní ipò ìmúláradá. Ẹ mójútó káà ìmí àti ìmí rẹ̀ kí ẹ sì gbáradì láti jí i bí ó bá ṣe pàtàkì.
translations.reference: Vomiting may occur, so place the person on their left side in the recovery position. Closely monitor airway and breathing and be ready to resuscitate if necessary
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"Trouble may arise, therefore, ensure that the person is accompanied by their family in a supportive role. Carefully consider the matter and its implications so you can advise accordingly as needed."

**Explanation of some of the choices made in the translation:**

*   **"Èébì lè wáyé"**: This translates to "Trouble may arise" or "Problems may occur."
*   **"nítorí náà ẹ mú kí"**: This means "therefore, ensure that" or "for that reason, make sure that."
*   **"ẹni náà sún pẹ̀lú ẹ̀gbẹ́ rẹ̀"**: This translates to "the person is accompanied by their family."
*   **"òsì ní ipò ìmúláradá"**: This means "in a supportive role" or "as a source of support."
*   **"Ẹ mójútó káà ìmí àti ìmí rẹ̀"**: This translates to "Carefully consider the matter and its implications."
*   **"kí ẹ sì gbáradì láti jí i bí ó bá ṣe pàtàkì"**: This means "so you can advise accordingly as needed."



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe ìkóràn Ìsọnísókí: Àwọn àìsàn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn wà lára àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ káàkiri àgbáyé tí wọ́n sì ń nípa lórí àwọn àwùjọ tí wọ́n tòṣì jùlọ tí wọ́n kò sì ní àǹfààní jùlọ. Wọ́n máa ń ràn nípa àwọn ẹyin tí ó wà nínú ìgbẹ́ ènìyàn èyí tí ó ń sọ ilẹ̀ di àìmọ́ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí kò ti sí ìmọ́tótó. Àwọn ẹ̀yà gbòógì tí wọ́n máa ń mú ènìyàn ni aràn roboto (Ascaris lumbricoides), aràn kòbókò (Trichuris trichuria) àti kán-ànyàn (Necator americanus àti Ancylostoma duodenale). Aràn inú máa ń pèsè àwọn onírúurú àmì àìsàn tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìṣòro inú bí (ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, inú rírun), ìrora àti àìlera gbogbogbò. Kán-ànyàn ń fa àdánù ẹ̀jẹ̀ inú líle èyí tí ó lè fa àìlẹ́jẹ̀tó. Àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn máa ń ràn nípa àwọn ẹyin tí wọ́n máa ń jáde láti ara ìgbẹ́ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n jẹ́ afaragbá. Àwọn àgbà aràn máa ń gbé nínú ìfun níbi tí wọ́n ti máa ń pèsè àwọn ẹgbẹẹgbẹ̀rún ẹyin lójoojúmọ́. Ní àwọn agbègbè tí wọn kò ní ìmọ́tótó tó tó, àwọn ẹyin wọ̀nyí maat ń sọ ilẹ̀ di àìmọ́. Èyí lè ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àwọn onírúurú ọ̀nà: àwọn ẹyin tí wọ́n lẹ̀ mọ́ ẹ̀fọ́ tí ènìyàn jẹ nígbà tí wọn kò bá ṣe, fọ̀ tàbí bó ẹ̀fọ́ náà dáadáa.;
translations.reference: Soil-transmitted helminthiases Overview: Soil-transmitted helminth (STH) infections are among the most common infections worldwide and affect the poorest and most deprived communities. They are transmitted by eggs present in human faeces which in turn contaminate soil in areas where sanitation is poor. The main species that infect people are the roundworm (Ascaris lumbricoides), the whipworm (Trichuris trichiura) and the hookworms (Necator americanus and Ancylostoma duodenale). Intestinal worms produce a wide range of symptoms including intestinal manifestations (diarrhoea, abdominal pain), general malaise and weakness. Hookworms cause chronic intestinal blood loss that result in anaemia. Soil-transmitted helminths are transmitted by eggs that are passed in the faeces of infected people. Adult worms live in the intestine where they produce thousands of eggs each day. In areas that lack adequate sanitation, these eggs contaminate the soil. This can happen in several ways: eggs that are attached to vegetables are ingested when the vegetables are not carefully cooked, washed or peeled;
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Diseases the Country Causes of Helminthiasis (Worm Infections):** Worm infections are among the most prevalent diseases worldwide, particularly affecting the most vulnerable populations who lack adequate sanitation. They are caused by eggs found in the human environment, making areas without proper sanitation highly contaminated. The major worms that cause these infections are roundworm (Ascaris lumbricoides), whipworm (Trichuris trichuria), and hookworm (Necator americanus and Ancylostoma duodenale). Intestinal worms cause various symptoms of illness, leading to problems such as diarrhea, abdominal pain, malnutrition, and general weakness. Hookworm causes anemia due to blood loss, which can lead to iron deficiency. Worm infections are caused by eggs that are released from the intestines of people who eat unwashed vegetables. Adult worms live in the soil, releasing thousands of eggs daily. In areas with inadequate sanitation, these eggs contaminate the environment. This can happen in various ways: eggs can contaminate vegetables that people eat when they are not properly washed, or when the vegetables are of poor quality.

translations.source: wọ́n máa ń jẹ ẹyin láti ara àwọn orísun omi tí kò mọ́; àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n ń ṣeré ní orí ilẹ̀ tí kò mọ́ máa ń kó ẹyin be sì ni wọ́n yóò fi àwọn ọwọ́ wọn sí ẹnu wọn láìfọ wọ́n. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Àìsàn máa ń ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú iye àwọn aràn tí àgọ̀ gbà. Àwọn ènìyàn tí àìsàn wọn jẹ́ àwọn aràn tí kò pọ̀ (aràn níwọ̀n) kì í sáábà jìyà láti ara àìsàn yìí. Àwọn àìsàn líle lè fa onírúurú àwọn àmì àìsàn tó fi mọ́ àwọn ìfarahàn nínú ìfun (ìgbẹ́ gbuuru àti inú rírun), àìjẹunrekánú, ìrora àti àìlera gbogbogbò, àti àìdàgbàbóṣeyẹ̀ àti àìgbèrú. Àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n le lè fa ìdíwọ́ inú tí ènìyàn gbọ́dọ̀ tọ́jú pẹ̀lú iṣẹ́ abẹ. S. stercolaris lè fa àìsàn awọ ara àti òpónà ọ̀fun bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìjẹunkánú láàrin àwọn ọmọdé. Ní ti àdínkù àjẹsára ẹni tí ó gbàlejò wọn, ajọ̀fẹ́ náà lè fa òòrèkóòrè àìsàn/àpẹẹrẹ àpínká tí ó lágbára bí wọn kò bá tètè tọ́jú wọn bí ó ṣe tọ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó máa léwu bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ìtọ́jú wà. Ní 2001, àwọn aṣojú ní Àpérò Àgbáyé fẹnukò papọ̀ lórí ọ̀nà àbáyọ (WHA54. 19) tí ó ń rọ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ní àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn láti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ní kojú aràn, pàápàá jùlọ sikosomíásì àti àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn. Ọgbọ́n láti ṣàkóso àwọn àìsàn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn ni láti ṣàkóso ewu àìsàn nípasẹ̀ ìtọ́jú òòrèkóòrè àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ní àwọn agbègbè àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn.
translations.reference: eggs are ingested from contaminated water sources; eggs are ingested by children who play in the contaminated soil and then put their hands in their mouths without washing them. Symptoms: Morbidity is related to the number of worms harboured. People with infections of light intensity (few worms) usually do not suffer from the infection. Heavier infections can cause a range of symptoms including intestinal manifestations (diarrhoea and abdominal pain), malnutrition, general malaise and weakness, and impaired growth and physical development. Infections of very high intensity can cause intestinal obstruction that should be treated surgically. S. stercoralis may cause dermatological and gastro-intestinal morbidity and is also known to be associated with chronic malnutrition in children. In case of reduced host immunity, the parasite can cause the hyperinfection/dissemination syndrome that is invariably fatal if not promptly and properly cured and is often fatal despite the treatment. In 2001, delegates at the World Health Assembly unanimously endorsed a resolution (WHA54.19) urging endemic countries to start seriously tackling worms, specifically schistosomiasis and soil-transmitted helminths. The strategy for control of soil-transmitted helminth infections is to control morbidity through the periodic treatment of at-risk people living in endemic areas.

Symptoms of illness: Illnesses are often accompanied by the amount of worms the body contains. People whose illnesses are worms that do not grow (intestinal worms) rarely suffer from this illness. Certain illnesses can cause a variety of symptoms that manifest in the body (black stool and bloody diarrhea), loss of appetite, weakness and general illness, and stunted growth and reduced intelligence. Illnesses that can cause internal bleeding that a person must treat with iron therapy. S. stercolaris can cause skin lesions and anal itching, and it is also associated with loss of appetite in children. In addition, ascariasis, if it is heavy, can cause complications/severe manifestations if they are not treated properly; it can be fatal even though treatment exists. In 2001, representatives at the World Health Assembly unanimously agreed on a resolution (WHA54.19) urging countries with endemic diseases to begin controlling worms, especially schistosomiasis and diseases the country is experiencing an epidemic of. The key to controlling endemic diseases is to control the risk of illness through the treatment of people at risk in endemic areas."

translations.source: Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ni: àwọn ọmọ jéléósinmi àwọn ọmọ ọjọ́ orí wọn lọ ilé ẹ̀kọ́ àwọn obìnrin tí ọjọ́ orí wọn ti tó bímọ (tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn aláboyún ní ìpele kejì Tai ìkẹta oyún àti obìnrin tí wọ́n ń fún ọmọ lọ́yàn) Àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn ń ṣe àwọn iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n léwu bí àwọn olùkórè tíì tàbí awakùsà. Ìtọ́jú Àjọ WHO dábàá àwọn òògùn - albendazole (400mg) àti mebendazole (500mg) - kájúẹ̀, kò wọ́n bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n rọrùn láti lo láti ọwọ́ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọn kì í ṣe òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera (b.a àwọn olùkọ́). Wọ́n ti là ìdánwò ààbò gbòòrò kọjá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ènìyàn ti lò wọ́n pẹ̀lú ipa ẹ̀yìn níwọ̀n àti èyí tí kò tó nǹkan. Àti albendazole àti mebendazole ni wọ́n máa ń fún àwọn àjọ ìlera ìjọba orílẹ̀ èdè nípasẹ̀ Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ní gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ní àrùn fún ìtọ́jú gbogbo àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n wà ní ọjọ́ orí láti lọ sí ilé ẹ̀kọ́. Ifamẹkitíìnì fún àkóso S. stercolaris ni ìrètí wà pé yóò wà ní iye tí kò jara lọ láti ọdún 2021.
translations.reference: People at risk are: preschool children school-age children women of reproductive age (including pregnant women in the second and third trimesters and breastfeeding women) adults in certain high-risk occupations such as tea-pickers or miners. TreatmentThe WHO recommended medicines –albendazole (400 mg) and mebendazole (500 mg) – are effective, inexpensive and easy to administer by non-medical personnel (e.g. teachers). They have been through extensive safety testing and have been used in millions of people with few and minor side-effects. Both albendazole and mebendazole are donated to national ministries of health through WHO in all endemic countries for the treatment of all children of school age. Ivermectin for the control of S. stercoralis is expected to be available at affordable price from 2021.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The people at risk include: children of school age, girls whose age allows them to attend school, women who provide care for children (including those who act as guardians at the secondary level, third grade, and women who care for orphans), and elders who perform tasks that put them at risk, such as teachers or assistants. The WHO advisory body recommends drugs – albendazole (400mg) and mebendazole (500mg) – which are readily available and can be administered by people who are not healthcare professionals (e.g., teachers). Extensive testing has been conducted, and the impact on the general population has been minimal. Albendazole and mebendazole are provided to health authorities by the World Health Organization through the Global Fund for Health in all countries that have the disease for the treatment of all children of school age. The cost of controlling *S. stercolaris* is expected to be relatively low from 2021 onwards."

translations.source: Àbíkú Ìsọnísókí: Ọmọ tí ó kú lẹ́yìn ọ̀sẹ̀ méjìdínlọ́gbọ̀n oyún, ṣùgbọ́n síwájú tàbí lẹ́yìn ìbí, ni a mọ̀ sí àbíkú. Ó tó mílíọ̀nù 2 àwọn àbíkú ni ó wà lọ́dọọdún - ọ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan ní ìṣẹ́jú àáyá 16 kan. Ó lé ní ìdá 40% gbogbo àwọn àbíkú ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àsìkò ìbímọ - àdánù tí ó ṣe é dènà pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú dídára tí ó sì pọ́nnilé lásìkò ìbímọ tí ó fi mọ́ ètò àmójútó àti àǹfààní àsìkò sí ìtọ́jú aláboyún pàjáwìrì nígbà tí ó nílò. Níní ìrírí àbíkú lásìkò ìlóyún tàbí ìbímọ jẹ́ àjálù tí wọ́n kò ṣe àmójútó tí ó tó nínú àwọn ètò, ìlànà àti ìrànwọ́ àgbáyé. Àwọn ìdíyelé wà, pàápàá jùlọ fún àwọn obìnrin àti ẹbí wọn, bí ìbànújẹ́ alábiamọ, ìṣòro owó àti àwọn àbájáde ọrọ̀ ajé, àti ìdẹ́yẹsí àti èèwọ̀ pẹ̀lú. Ní 2014, Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé fọwọ́sí Ètò Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan, èyí tí a ti rí àfojúsùn àgbáyé 12 tàbí àwọn àbíkú onípele kẹta (pípẹ́) díẹ̀ nínú àpapọ̀ ìbí 1, 000 ní orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan ní 2030. Ní 2021, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi àti alábọ́dé èrè 139 gbòógì ti fẹnukò lórí àfojúsùn yìí, ṣùgbọ́n àwọn orílẹ̀ 56 kò ní ṣe àṣeyọrí àfojúsùn Ètò Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan ní 2030 bí àwọn ìgbìyànjú síwájú sí i kò bá jẹ́ ṣíṣe. Bí àwọn àṣà ìwòyí bá tẹ̀síwájú, àwọn ìkókó mílíọ̀nù 15.9 yóò jẹ́ àbíkú; ó sún mọ́ ìdajì àwọn wọ̀nyí (mílíọ̀nù 7.7, 48%) yóò ṣẹlẹ̀ ní agbègbè ẹkùn Sahara Afíríkà. Okùnfà: Pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú ìlera dídára káàkiri àsìkò ìlóyún àti ìbímọ, ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn àbíkú ṣe é dènà. Àwọn okùnfà àbíkú gbòógì ni àwọn ìṣòro oyún àti ìbímọ, oyún ọjọ́ pípẹ́, àìsàn ìyálọ́mọ bí ibà maléríà, sífílì àti KASA, àwọn ìṣòro ìyálọ́mọ pàápàá jùlọ ẹ̀jẹ̀ ríru, àti àtọ̀gbẹ, àti gbèdéke ìwọ̀n títóbi (nígbà tí ọmọ tí wọ́n kò tíì bí kò bá lè dàgbà bí ó ṣe tọ́ nítorí náà ó kéré jù bí ó ṣe yẹ lọ). Àwọn ìṣòro àsìkò ìbí ni wọ́n ṣokùnfà kò tó ìdá 10% àwọn àbíkú ni wọ́n jábọ̀ káàkiri àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ibùdó elérè pínnísín kò ní àkọsílẹ̀ nítorí àwọn gbèdéke inú àwọn àyẹ̀wò.
translations.reference: Stillbirth Overview: A baby who dies after 28 weeks of pregnancy, but before or during birth, is classified as a stillbirth.There are nearly 2 million stillbirths every year – one every 16 seconds. Over 40% of all stillbirths occur during labour – a loss that could be avoided with improved quality and respectful care during childbirth including routine monitoring and timely access to emergency obstetric care when required. Experiencing a stillbirth during pregnancy or childbirth is a tragedy insufficiently addressed in global agendas, policies and funded programmes. There are psychological costs, especially to women and their families, such as maternal depression, financial consequences and economic repercussions, as well as stigma and taboo.In 2014, the World Health Assembly endorsed the Every Newborn Action Plan (ENAP), which includes a global target of 12 or fewer third trimester (late) stillbirths per 1,000 total births in every country by 2030. In 2021, 139 mainly high-income and upper middle-income countries had met this target, but 56 countries will not each the ENAP target by 2030 if further efforts are not made. If current trends continue, 15.9 million babies will be stillborn; nearly half of these (7.7 million, 48%) will occur in sub-Saharan Africa. Causes: With quality health care throughout pregnancy and childbirth, most stillbirths are preventable. The major causes of stillbirth include pregnancy and childbirth-related complications, prolonged pregnancy, maternal infections such as malaria, syphilis and HIV, maternal conditions especially hypertension, and diabetes, and fetal growth restriction (when an unborn baby is unable to achieve its growth potential and therefore smaller than it should be). Congenital abnormalities are responsible for less than 10% of stillbirths reported nationally in high-income countries and the exact proportion in low-income settings is unknown due to limitations in diagnostics.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Abiku Mortality: A Child who dies within the first two weeks of life, or before or shortly after birth, is known as an Abiku.** Approximately two million Abiku are born annually – each with a lifespan of about 16 days. 40% of all Abiku deaths occur during the perinatal period – a rate exacerbated by poor quality healthcare and a lack of postnatal care and newborn screening programs, especially when these are needed to address severe complications. In cases of Abiku deaths during infancy or the perinatal period, they are often not included in national health programs, policies, and planning. The causes are present, particularly for women and their families, including postpartum hemorrhage, financial difficulties and related stressors, and stigma and misconceptions. In 2014, the Global Health Initiative endorsed the Every Newborn Initiative, aiming to reduce the third-day (postnatal) mortality rate to less than 20 per 1,000 live births in each country by 2030. In 2021, 139 high-burden and fragile countries committed to this goal, but 56 countries are unlikely to achieve the Every Newborn Initiative target by 2030 without accelerated progress. As current trends continue, 15.9 million children will die; 7.7 million (48%) of these deaths will occur in sub-Saharan Africa. Causes: Many Abiku deaths are caused by poor quality healthcare throughout infancy and the perinatal period. The main causes of Abiku deaths are childhood illnesses and birth complications, prolonged labor, childhood illnesses such as malaria, sickle cell disease, HIV/AIDS, childhood illnesses especially diarrhea, prematurity, and obstructed labor (when a child who has not been born cannot grow as they should, so they are smaller than they should be). Perinatal complications account for less than 10% of Abiku deaths in high-burden countries, and remote communities often lack data due to deficiencies in vital registration systems.
