translations.source: Àwọn ìdí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ọjọ́ orí ìyá àti fífa nǹkan náà lè ṣe àgbéga ewu àìsàn ìyálọ́mọ àti àbíkú. Àwọn àbíkú lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ ètò ìfètòsọ́mọbíbí láti dènà oyún àìmọ̀dí, ìlera àti ìjẹunrekánú gidi síwájú àti lásìkò ìlóyún, dídára àti ìbọ̀wọ̀fún ìtọ́jú àsìkò ìlóyún àti ìbímọ tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn alámọ̀já òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera tí wọ́n tó dórí àwọn agbẹ̀bí. Ìtọ́jú sífílì lásìkò ìlóyún lè dènà ìṣirò àwọn àbíkú 200 000, nígbà tí àmójútó ìwọ̀n ìmí olẹ̀ àti àmójútó ìbímọ àti ìdásí àsìkò nígbà tí wọ́n nílò ṣe pàtàkì láti dènà àwọn àbíkú 832 000 lásìkò ìbí àti àdínkù àwọn ikú lẹ́yìn ìbí. Wọ́n ní àjọṣepọ̀ wà láàrin àbíkú pẹ̀lú àwọn aṣokùnfà àwùjọ àti ọrọ̀ ajé líle lórí ìlera. Ìdènà àti ìtọ́jú afèsì nílò láti jẹ́ ṣíṣe àmúlò káàkiri ìtẹ̀síwájú ìtọ́jú ìlera ìyálọ́mọ, àti jù bẹ́ẹ̀ lọ. Lára èyí ni ìtọ́jú alápọ̀n-ọ́nlé àti alátìlẹ́yìn lásìkò ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ikú. Fífi etí sílẹ̀ sí ìrírí àti ohùn àwọn obìnrin àti àwùjọ wọn ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún kíkojú àwọn ọ̀ràn ìdẹ́yẹsí tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àbíkú. Èsì WHO: Ohun púpọ̀ ní láti di ṣíṣe sí i láti ṣàmúlò ìdènà àbíkú láàrin ètò àgbáyé àti orílẹ̀ èdè fún ìtọ́jú ìlera ńlá fún àwọn obìnrin, ọ̀dọ́mọdé, àti ìkókó. Nípasẹ̀ ọgbọ́n Àgbáyé fún Ìlera àwọn Obìnrin, Ìkókó àti Ọ̀dọ́mọdé 2016-2030 àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn àbá Ètò Iṣẹ́ Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan (2014), Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ń ṣiṣẹ́ láti fòpin sí àwọn àbíkú tí wọ́n ṣe é dènà, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ara akitiyan láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera ìyálọ́mọ, ọmọ tuntun, ọmọdé àti ọ̀dọ́mọdé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àbíkú kì í gba ìwé ẹ̀rí ìbí bẹ́ẹ̀ sit ni wọn kì í forúkọ wọn sílẹ̀.
translations.reference: Factors related to age of the mother and practicing smoking can also increase the risk of maternal disease and stillbirth. Stillbirths can be prevented through family planning to avoid unwanted pregnancies, good health and nutrition prior to and during pregnancy, quality and respectful antenatal and childbirth care including adequate skilled health personnel including midwives. Syphilis treatment in pregnancy could also prevent an estimated 200 000 stillbirths, while fetal heart rate monitoring and labour surveillance and prompt interventions when needed are crucial for preventing 832 000 intrapartum stillbirths and reducing neonatal deaths. Stillbirths are strongly linked to adverse social and economic determinants of health. Prevention and responsive care need to be integrated across the continuum of maternal health care, and beyond. This includes respectful and supportive care in the event of a death. Listening to the experiences and voices of women and their communities is essential to help address issues of stigma associated with stillbirth. WHO response: More needs to be done to integrate stillbirth prevention within global and national agendas for high quality health care for women, adolescents, and babies. Through the Global Strategy for Women's, Children's and Adolescents Health 2016–2030 and implementing the recommendations of the Every Newborn Action Plan (2014), WHO is working to end preventable stillbirths, as part of efforts to improve maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health. Most stillbirths do not receive a birth certificate and are not registered.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The factors associated with maternal mortality and stillbirth can increase the risk of child mortality and death. Deaths can be reduced through comprehensive reproductive health programs to address infertility, illness, and genuine lack of family planning before and during pregnancy, accurate and timely identification of pregnancy loss and childbirth, and training healthcare workers, including community health workers. Screening for pregnancy loss can reduce an estimated 200,000 deaths, while interventions for maternal mortality and stillbirth and newborn deaths, when needed, can prevent an estimated 832,000 deaths during pregnancy and postnatal deaths. There is a partnership between deaths with social and economic factors related to health. Prevention and screening are needed to implement comprehensive maternal health care assessments and, even more so. This includes screening for early pregnancy and postpartum deaths. Addressing the needs and vulnerabilities of women and their communities is crucial to reducing the burden of preventable deaths. WHO Challenge: A lot needs to be done to implement death reduction within global and national programs for large-scale health assessments for women, children, and adolescents. Through the Global Strategy for Women’s, Children’s and Adolescents’ Health 2016-2030 and the implementation of Every New Baby Action Plan (2014), the World Health Organization is working to address deaths that can be reduced, particularly as a key priority to advance maternal, newborn, child, and adolescent health. Many deaths do not receive birth certificates, so they are not recorded."

translations.source: Mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ètò ìjábọ̀ àwọn ìbí àti ikú ẹ̀yìn ìbímọ jẹ́ ọ̀rọ̀ ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn àti àmúyẹ fún mímú àdínkù bá àwọn àbíkú. Ní àfikún, ó nílò kí wọ́n mú ìrólágbára bá àwọn ètò ìròyìn ojoojúmọ́ láti sọ iye àti láti jábọ̀ àwọn àbíkú pérépéré fún àmójútó àti ìlọsíwájú ìtọ́jú ìlera. WHO pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti mú ìlọsíwájú ìwífun-àlàyé wọn lórí àwọn àbíkú àti àwọn ikú ìkókó. WHO tún ní ìtọ́nisọ́nà láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣàyẹ̀wò àti láti wádìí ikú ẹ̀dá kọ̀ọ̀kan kí wọ́n lè dábàá kí wọ́n sì ṣàmúlò àwọn ọ̀nà àbáyọ láti dènà irúfẹ́ àwọn nǹkan bẹ́ẹ̀ ní ọjọ́ iwájú àti ìpínsọ́wọ̀ọ́ ètò fún àwọn ikú àbíkú (ICD-PM). WHO àti UNICEF ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ìpè fún iṣẹ́ àjọṣe láti fòpin sí àwọn àbíkú tí wọ́n ṣe é dènà nípa: mímú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìpolongo àti àdínkù ìdẹ́yẹsí ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn obìnrin àti mọ̀lẹ́bí tí ọ̀fọ́ sẹ̀ ìrólágbára àwọn ètò ìlera láti pèsè ìtọ́jú pípéye àmúlò àwọn àfojúsùn àbíkú ní orílẹ̀ èdè àti lábẹ́lé mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìdíwọ̀n àwọn àbíkú láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ẹ̀rí àti ìmọ̀.
translations.reference: Improving systems for reporting births and neonatal deaths is a matter of human rights and a prerequisite for reducing stillbirths. In addition, there is a need to strengthen routine health information systems to accurately capture and report stillbirths for monitoring and improving the quality of care. WHO provides tools to help countries improve their data on both stillbirths and neonatal deaths. WHO also has guidance to help countries review and investigate individual deaths so they can recommend and implement solutions to prevent similar ones in the future and a standard classification system for perinatal deaths (ICD-PM). WHO and UNICEF are supporting a call for collective action to end preventable stillbirths by: increasing awareness and reducing stigma supporting bereaved women and families strengthening health systems to provide high-quality care nationalizing and localizing stillbirth targets improving measurement of stillbirths to enhance evidence and knowledge.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The advancement of vital registration systems for births and deaths, including the registration of stillbirths, is a crucial matter and a guarantee for the advancement of additional registration of the deceased. Furthermore, it is necessary to provide strong support for daily news programs to report figures and to register deceased individuals accurately for monitoring and the advancement of public health. WHO provides guidance to countries to support the advancement of their reporting on deaths and unexpected deaths. WHO also has a responsibility to support countries in reviewing and validating each cause of death so that they can establish reliable data and develop strategies to prevent such occurrences in the future and the program for classifying deaths (ICD-PM). WHO and UNICEF are advocating for resources for operational work to reach the deceased who they have identified through: advancing the policy and additional implementation support for women and caregivers who have the authority to support health programs to ensure comprehensive monitoring of death certificates in countries and to advance the recording of deaths to improve health and knowledge."

translations.source: Àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ìsọnísókí: Ó tó bílíọ́nù méjì àwọn ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé ni wọn kò ní àǹfààní sí àwọn òògùn, abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, irinṣẹ́ ìṣègùn tí wọ́n ṣe pàtàkì tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn irinṣẹ́ àyẹ̀wò àìsàn, àti àwọn ohun èlò ìlera mìíràn, èyí tí ó ń ṣẹ̀dá àlàfo tí ó máa ń sáábà jẹ́ dídí láti ọwọ́ àwọn ohun èlò ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ìṣòro yìí ń dàgbà sí i bí ẹ̀wọ́n ìpèsè káàkiri àgbáyé ṣe ń gbòòrò sí i, tí ó túmọ̀ sí pé àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n pèsè ní orílẹ̀ èdè kan lè jẹ́ dídì ní orílẹ̀ èdè kejì tí wọn yóò sì máa pín wọn káàkiri àwọn odi ìlú láti polówó tàbí tà fún swkmt oníbàárà ní ìkẹta. Ìdàgbàsókè okòwò orí ẹ̀rọ tún ń nípa lórí àṣà yìí nípa mímú kí ó rọrùn láti ra àwọn òògùn lórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára, láti ọwọ́ àwọn orísun tí wọ́n kò ní àṣẹ ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà. WHO ti ṣe ìdámọ̀ ọ̀ràn yìí ọ̀kan nínú àwọn ìṣòro ìlera kíákíá fún ọdún mẹ́wàá tí ó ń bọ̀ nítorí pé ó lé ní ọ̀kan nínú mẹ́wàá àwọn òògùn ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè ni wọ́n ṣírò pé wọ́n jẹ́ ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Kò sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí kò tíì faragbá nínú ọ̀ràn yìí, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni WHO gba àwọn ìròyìn òògùn, abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti irinṣẹ́ àyẹ̀wò àìsàn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ ní gbogbo àwọn ẹkùn àgbáyé. Àti àwọn òògùn abéròjàjórúkọ àti àwọn òògùn àtọọ́dá ni wọ́n ṣe é gbélẹ̀rọ, láti orí orí àwọn ohun èlò olówó gọbọi fún jẹjẹrẹ dórí àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n kò wọ́n rárá fún ìtọ́jú ìrora. Àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé fẹnukò lórí ọgbọ́n gbòòrò káríayé tí ó fojúsun ìdènà, àyẹ̀wò àti ìfèsì láti tẹ̀síwájú lórí àṣeyọrí níní àǹfààní púpọ̀ sí ohùn èlò ìṣègùn dídára, aláàbò, tí ó múnádóko tí ó sì dára. Ipa: Àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ ṣẹ̀dá ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìṣòro fún ati awon ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń lo àwọn òògùn náà àti fún àwọn ẹbí, àwùjọ gbòòrò, ètò ìtọ́jú ilera àti ẹ̀wọ́n ìpèsè. Nígbà tí àwọn òògùn kò bá ṣiṣẹ́ bí ó ṣe yẹ kí wọ́n ṣiṣẹ́, wọ́n lè kùnà láti ṣèwòsàn tàbí dènà àìsàn, ń dá kún ìlòdìsí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò, ìfiṣòfò àwọn àlùmọ́ọ́nì iyebíye tí yóò sì yọrí sí àdánù ìgboyà nínú lílo àwọn òògùn àti àìfọkàntán àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera.
translations.reference: Substandard and falsified medical products Overview: Up to two billion people around the world lack access to necessary medicines, vaccines, medical devices including in vitro diagnostics, and other health products, which creates a vacuum that is too often filled by substandard and falsified products. This problem is growing as global supply chains become more complex, meaning products manufactured in one country may be packaged in a second country and distributed across borders to be marketed or sold to consumers in a third. The growth of e-commerce also contributes to this trend by making it easier to purchase medicines online, often from unauthorized sources. WHO has identified this issue one of the urgent health challenges for the next decade given that more than one in ten medicines in low- and middle-income countries are estimated to be substandard or falsified. No country remains untouched from this issue, and WHO has received reports of substandard or falsified medical medicines, vaccines and in vitro diagnostics from all regions of the world. Both generic and innovator medicines can be falsified, ranging from very expensive products for cancer to very inexpensive products for treatment of pain. WHO Member States have agreed on a comprehensive global strategy focused on prevention, detection and response to move towards achieving increased access to quality, safe, effective and quality medical products. Impact: Substandard and falsified medical products create many problems for both the individual taking the medicine and for families, the larger society, health care system and supply chain. When medicines do not work the way they should, they can fail to treat or prevent disease, contribute to antimicrobial resistance, waste precious resources and lead to a loss of confidence in medicines and distrust of healthcare providers.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Counterfeit and Substandard Medicines and Health Products.**

**Background:** Approximately two billion people worldwide lack access to essential medicines, safe and effective medical products, diagnostic tests, and other health products, creating a vulnerability that can be exploited by counterfeit and substandard health products. This problem is exacerbated by the increasing prevalence of fake medicines around the world, meaning that medicines manufactured in one country can be falsely labeled in another and distributed across cities to be sold at a profit or to unscrupulous vendors. The rapid development of technology also contributes to this issue, making it easier to purchase medicines online from sources that are often unregulated. WHO has identified this as one of the most serious public health threats for the next decade, as it is estimated that one in ten medicines in low- and middle-income countries are falsely identified as being essential and substandard. No country has been spared from this problem, and WHO collects information on medicines, medical products, and diagnostic tests that are essential and substandard globally. Counterfeit and falsified medicines, as well as substandard medical products, range from those offered for free to those that are not even intended for therapeutic use. Member states of the World Health Assembly are focused on innovation, testing, and strategies to advance success in ensuring greater access to quality, safe, effective, and affordable essential health products.

**Impact:** Counterfeit and substandard medicines and health products pose significant risks to individuals using these products, as well as to families, communities, health systems, and national economies. When medicines do not work as they should, they can fail to treat or cure illnesses, leading to antimicrobial resistance, undermining public confidence in the value of medicines, and ultimately resulting in loss of life due to improper medicine use and the failure of healthcare workers.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Èyí lè ní ipa ajẹmọ́ àwùjọ òun ọrọ̀ ajé òdì, níní ipa lórí àdánù tí ó ní í ṣe nínú ìpèsè àti ṣíṣe àfikún àwọn owónàá fún ènìyàn náà àti ètò ìlera orílẹ̀ èdè. Ó ṣe é ṣe kí wọ́n kàn sí àwọn aláìsàn ní àwọn ipò níbi tí ìdènà àǹfààní ti wà sí àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn dídára àti aláàbò, ìdarí tí kò dára àti ipa ìmọ̀ tí kò lágbára. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ọ̀ràn yìí ń nípa lórí gbogbo orílẹ̀ èdè, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè níbi tí wọ́n kò ti ètò ìdarí aláìlágbára tàbí tí kò sí ti ń rú àjàgà púpọ̀ àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ní ayé tí ó ti lajú sí i, kò sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí ó ní àwọn èròjà tí ó tó àti agbára láti gbógun ti ọ̀ràn náà ní àdáwà. Nípasẹ̀ àwọn ètò àti ìgbésẹ̀ àgbáyé bí Ètò Àkóso àti Àmójútó Káríayé ẹgbẹ́ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé pẹ̀lú ọ̀ràn yìí ní àdáwà. Àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti dènà, ṣàyẹ̀wò àti fèsì sí àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ.
translations.reference: This can have a negative socioeconomic impact, contributing to related loss in productivity and adding expenses to the individual and national health system. They are most likely to reach patients in situations where there is constrained access to quality and safe medical products, poor governance and weak technical capacity. Although this issue impacts all countries, countries where there are weak or non-existent regulatory systems bear the greatest burden of substandard and falsified medical products. In an increasingly globalized world, no one country has sufficient resources and capacity to deal with this issue in isolation. Through global systems and processes such as the WHO Global Surveillance and Monitoring System and WHO Member State Mechanism, Member States are working together to prevent, detect and respond to substandard and falsified medical products.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"This can have a significant impact on community health, particularly on financial resources, its effect on reducing mortality and implementing and expanding programs for people and national health systems. It requires them to respond to patients in situations where access to specific and advanced medical equipment is limited and inadequate, poor management, and weak knowledge. While this issue affects all countries, those countries without strong management systems or that do not have sufficient access to advanced medical equipment and technology are particularly vulnerable. In the world we live in, there is no country with the resources and capacity to completely overcome this challenge. Through global initiatives and programs such as the Governance and Accountability Action Plan, the World Health Organization is addressing this challenge. Member countries are working together to ensure, evaluate, and improve access to advanced and innovative medical equipment."

translations.source: Ìdènà ìgbẹ́míaraẹni Ìsọnísókí: Ó lé ní àwọn ènìyàn 700 000 ni wọ́n kú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lọ́dọọdún. Fún ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, ìṣirò àwọn ìgbìyànjú láti gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni tí ó ju 20 ló wà. Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lè wáyé ní èyíkéyìí ìpele ìgbésí ayé àti ní gbogbo ẹkùn àgbáyé. Ní 2019, ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni asíwájú okùnfà ikú kẹrin láàrin àwọn ọmọ ọdún 15-29 káàkiri àgbáyé, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 77% gbogbo àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti àti elérè gọbọi. Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ẹ́ tó 20% àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lágbàáyé ṣẹlẹ̀ láti ara mímu májèlé òògùn apako. Nígbà tí àjọṣepọ̀ láàrin ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìṣòro ìlera orí (pàápàá jùlọ, ìbànújẹ́ ọkàn àti ìṣòro ọtí mímu) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni tẹ́lẹ̀ fẹsẹ̀ múlẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò ìṣòro. Àwọn okùnfà ewu fún ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni ìrírí àdánù, ìdáwà, ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà, ìyapa ibáraẹniṣepọ̀, ìṣòro owó, ìrora ńlá àti àìsàn, ìwà ipá, àti ìjà tàbí àwọn pàjáwìrì ajẹmọ́ ọmọnìyàn mìíràn. Ìdẹ́yẹsí tí ó yí ìṣòro ilerat orí ká àti ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni túmọ̀ sí pé ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ronú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kò wá ìrànlọ́wọ́. Àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìgbìyànjú láti gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni ní agbára tí ó ń nípa lórí ẹbí, ọ̀rẹ́, alábàáṣiṣẹ́pọ̀, àwùjọ àti ìlú.
translations.reference: Suicide prevention Overview: More than 700 000 people die by suicide every year. For each suicide, there are an estimated more than 20 suicide attempts. Suicide can occur at any stage of life and in all regions of the world. In 2019, suicide was the fourth leading cause of death among 15–29-year-olds globally, and 77% of all suicides happened in low- and middle-income countries. Almost 20% of global suicides were due to pesticide self-poisoning. While the link between suicide and mental health conditions (in particular, depression and alcohol use disorders) and a previous suicide attempt is well established in high-income countries, many suicides happen impulsively in moments of crisis. Further risk factors for suicide include experience of loss, loneliness, discrimination, a relationship break-up, financial problems, chronic pain and illness, violence, abuse, and conflict or other humanitarian emergencies. Stigma around mental health conditions and suicide means many people thinking about suicide do not seek help. Suicides and suicide attempts have a ripple effect that impacts on families, friends, colleagues, communities and societies.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Suicide Statistics: It is estimated that 700,000 people die annually from suicide. For every suicide, there are more than 20 people struggling with suicidal thoughts. Suicide can occur at any stage of life and in every corner of the world. In 2019, suicide was the fourth leading cause of death among 15-29 year olds worldwide, and 77% of all suicides occur in low- and middle-income countries. Approximately 20% of suicides worldwide occur due to military-grade pesticide poisoning. While there is a correlation between suicide and mental health disorders (most commonly depression and anxiety disorders) and people struggling with suicidal thoughts often seek help in high-income countries, many suicides occur during times of crisis. Risk factors for suicide include trauma, abuse, mistreatment, social isolation, financial problems, serious illness, behavioral problems, and violence or other external factors. The stigma surrounding mental health disorders and suicide means that many people who are concerned about suicide do not seek help. Suicide and suicidal thoughts have a significant impact on families, friends, colleagues, communities, and cities.**

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability in English.
*   Some phrases required a bit of interpretation to convey the intended meaning clearly.
*   I've tried to preserve the tone and seriousness of the original text.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: àwọn òtítọ́ àti iye káàkiri àgbáyé Ìròyìn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni káàkiri àgbáyé fún 2019 GBÉ AYÉ: ṣíṣe àmúlò ìtọ́nisọ́nà funt ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè Ìdènà: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ nǹkan lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe ní àwọn ìpele ẹ̀dá kọ̀ọ̀kan, àwùjọ àti orílẹ̀ èdè láti dènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni. Ìlànà GBÉ AYÉ àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé dábàá àwọn ìpèsè mẹ́rin pàtàkì tí wọ́n múnádóko, adálórí-ẹ̀rí èyí tí ó gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ fífi sí ara ìtọ́jú orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan dórí ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: fífi gbèdéke sí àǹfààní sí àwọn ọ̀nà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni níní àjọsọ pẹ̀lú ilé-iṣẹ́ ìròyìn fún ìròyìn tí ó tọ̀nà lórí ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ìgbèrú àwọn ìmọ̀-ọ́nṣe ìgbésí ayé àwùjọ ajẹmọ́ ìmọ̀lára láàrin àwọn ọ̀dọ́mọdé ìdámọ̀ àsìkò, ìgbéléwọ̀n, àkóso àti ìtẹ̀lé ẹnikẹ́ni tí ó ní ìpalára nípa àwọn ìwà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni. Àwọn òpó ìpìlẹ̀ ìdásí náà ni àlàyé ipò, àjọṣepọ̀ ọlọ́pọ̀ ẹ̀ka, ṣíṣe ìpolongo, ìṣẹ̀dá agbára, ṣíṣe ètò ìṣúná, àmójútó, àkóso àti ìgbéléwọ̀n.
translations.reference: Suicide: facts and figures globally Suicide worldwide in 2019 report LIVE LIFE: an implementation guide for suicide prevention in countries Prevention: Much can be done at individual, community and national levels to prevent suicide and suicide attempts. WHO’s LIVE LIFE approach recommends four effective, evidence-based key interventions which should be included in every national response to suicide: limit access to the means of suicide interact with the media for responsible reporting of suicide foster socio-emotional life skills in adolescents early identify, assess, manage and follow up anyone who is affected by suicidal behaviours. The foundational pillars of the interventions are situation analysis, multisectoral collaboration, awareness raising, capacity building, financing, surveillance, monitoring and evaluation.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Sustainable Development Goals: Facts and Figures Worldwide**

**Global Report: Implementing the Sustainable Development Goals in Countries**

**Introduction:** Many things can be done at each level of development, community, and country to achieve sustainable development and personal well-being. The Global Report by the World Health Organization outlines four key priorities that must be tailored to each country's specific context to achieve sustainable development: strengthening the link between health and sustainable development practices within communities with media outlets for reporting on health-related issues; expanding the knowledge of life-saving professionals; promoting health education among children; preventing violence, control and tracking of anyone with information about sustainable development practices. The various steps involved include situation analysis, multi-sectoral collaboration, policy implementation, capacity building, program implementation, monitoring, management and evaluation.

translations.source: Àwọn akitiyan ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni pè fún àkóso àti àjọṣepọ̀ láàrin àwọn ọ̀pọ̀ ẹ̀ka, tí ó fi mọ́ ìlera, ẹ̀kọ́, iṣẹ́, iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀, okòwò, ìdájọ́, òfin, òṣèlú àti iṣẹ́ ìròyìn. Ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kì í sáábà jẹ́ wíwá ojútùú sí nítorí ìdẹ́yẹsí àti àìsí ìpolongo pé ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ ńlá. Àwọn àwùjọ náà ń kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni. Irinṣẹ́ àmúlò ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àwùjọ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ń ṣe ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwọn àwùjọ láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn iṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn ọ̀gangan ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ abẹ́lé. Èsì WHO: Ìpòǹgbẹ láti ṣiṣẹ́ dènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni wọ́n ti ṣe ìdámọ̀ tí wọ́n sì ti sọ di pàtàkì ní àwọn ìpele gíga jùlọ. Àdínkù ìwọ̀n ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè jẹ́ ìtọ́ka nínú àwọn Àfojúsùn ti UN (ìtọ́ka fún ìlera orí kan ṣoṣo), Ètò Gbogbogbò Iṣẹ́ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé àti Ètò Iṣẹ́ Ìlera orí Gbòòrò 2013-2030. Ìròyìn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àgbáyé kìíní ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé, Ìdènà Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: ìmọ̀ abágbàmu káríayé, tí wọ́n tẹ̀ jáde ní 2014, tí ó ní àfojúsùn láti mú ìgbéga bá ìpolongo pàtàkì ìlera àwùjọ ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti àwọn ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni àti sísọ ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni di pàtàkì ìlera àwùjọ. Wọ́n gba àwọn ìjọba ní ìyànjú láti ṣàmúlò àgbékalẹ̀ GBÉ AYÉ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ti àwọn ìdásí pàtàkì pípé àti adálórí-ẹ̀rí fún ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti láti mú ìdàgbàsókè tàbí ìrólágbára bá àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àgbáyé gbígbòòrò nínú ìlànà ìlera àwùjọ ọlọ́pọ̀ ẹ̀ka. Ìdàrí àti àkóso àgbáyé lórí ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni wọ́n túbọ̀ ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún nípa àtẹ̀jáde àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni orílẹ̀ èdè: ìlọsíwájú, àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti ìtọ́ka. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ ìwífún àlàyé àti ìṣirò hebt fún ìkéde àti ṣíṣe ìpinnu, àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé tẹ Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni jákèjádò àgbáyé ní 2019: Àwọn Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé.
translations.reference: Suicide prevention efforts require coordination and collaboration among multiple sectors, including health, education, labour, agriculture, business, justice, law, defence, politics and the media. Suicide prevention is often not adequately addressed because of stigma and lack of awareness that suicide is a major public health problem. Communities also play a crucial role in preventing suicide. The WHO community engagement toolkit guides communities in identifying priorities and implementing activities according to local contexts. WHO response: The urgency to act to prevent suicides has been recognized and prioritized at the highest levels. The reduction of suicide rates in countries is an indicator in the UN Sustainable Development Goals (the only indicator for mental health), WHO’s General Programme of Work and WHO’s Comprehensive Mental Health Action Plan 2013–2030. The first WHO world suicide report, Preventing suicide: a global imperative, published in 2014, aimed to increase awareness of the public health significance of suicide and suicide attempts and make suicide prevention a public health priority. Governments are encouraged to implement WHO’s LIVE LIFE package of effective and evidence-based key interventions for suicide prevention, and to develop or strengthen comprehensive national suicide prevention strategies in a multisectoral public health approach. National leadership and coordination on suicide prevention are further supported by the publication of National suicide prevention strategies: progress, examples and indicators. Recognizing that data and statistics are necessary for advocacy and decision-making, WHO published Suicide worldwide in 2019: Global Health Estimates.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Actions on neglected tropical diseases focus on control and collaboration among various sectors, including health, education, work, agriculture, livestock, justice, law, social welfare, and journalism. Neglected tropical diseases should not be considered a minor issue due to the underestimation and lack of recognition that they are a major public health problem. Communities play a crucial role in neglected tropical disease control. The framework for action on social determinants of health by the World Health Assembly provides guidance to communities to address key issues and implement comprehensive interventions such as vector control programs. WHO's priority: Establishing a platform to work on neglected tropical disease control has been established and prioritized at the highest levels. The increased attention to neglected tropical diseases in countries is reflected in the UN Resolutions (a focus on one health specifically), the Global Action Plan for the World Health Assembly, and the Global Health Sector Strategy 2013-2030. The global neglected tropical disease report by the World Health Assembly, Neglected Tropical Diseases: Progress and Challenges, released in 2014, has a focus on boosting the prioritization of public health neglected tropical diseases and personal protective measures and making neglected tropical diseases a public health priority. It encourages governments to implement the World Health Assembly's global framework of key interventions and leadership for neglected tropical disease control and to strengthen the global neglected tropical disease control community within a multi-sectoral health approach. Global governance on neglected tropical disease control has increasingly emphasized support for the development of national neglected tropical disease programs: advancement, models, and indicators. To facilitate evidence-based decision-making and implementation, the World Health Assembly released the Global Report on Neglected Tropical Diseases in 2019: Global Health Trends."

translations.source: Àfojúsùn àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni ayé tí ikú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kò ti síwájú okùnfà ikú àti níbi tí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbìyànjú tàbí ronú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ti lè rí ìrọ̀rùn láti béèrè fún ìrànlọ́wọ́ èyí tí wọ́n ń pèsè nínú ipá àti ọ̀wọ̀ fún gbogbo ènìyàn.
translations.reference: WHO’s vision is a world where death by suicide is no longer a leading cause of death and where people who are attempting or thinking about suicide feel comfortable seeking help which is provided capably and respectfully to all.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"The World Health Organization's public health advisory states that the global pandemic has not progressed, and that people who are experiencing symptoms or are concerned about the pandemic can seek help, which is being provided within the arm and hand for everyone."

**Explanation of some nuances:**

*   **Àfojúsùn àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé:** World Health Organization's public health advisory
*   **ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni:** Pandemic (This is a relatively modern Yoruba word, adopted from English)
*   **gbìyànjú tàbí ronú:** experiencing symptoms or are concerned
*   **ìrànlọ́wọ́:** help/assistance
*   **pèsè nínú ipá àti ọ̀wọ̀:** being provided within the arm and hand (This is a figurative expression meaning readily available and accessible)
*   **fún gbogbo ènìyàn:** for everyone



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése Ìsọnísókí: Ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése ni ọ̀rọ̀ ìperí gbòòrò láti ṣàpèjúwe àwọn ìlànà, iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àti ìdókòwò tí ó ń pèsè àwọn àǹfààní lónìí láì kọlu ìlera àyíká, àwùjọ àti ti ara ẹni lọ́jọ́ iwájú. Àwọn ìlànà wọ̀nyí ni wọ́n sáábà máa ń ṣàpèjúwe gẹ́gẹ́ bí àìléwu nítorí pé wọ́n fojúsùn mímú àdínkù bá ipa ìdàgbàsókè lórí àyíká. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn àǹfààní ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése ń jẹ́ rírí káàkiri àwọn onírúurú ẹ̀ka ìlera àti àlàáfíà ọmọnìyàn, tí ó fi mọ́ àdínkù abayíkájẹ́- àti àìsàn adálórí àyíká, àwọn àbájáde ìlera tí ó lọ síwájú àti àdínkù ìṣòro. Bí ìṣòro àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ ṣe ń ṣe pàtàkì sí i, àwọn ọgbọ́n àmúṣiṣẹ́ fún àdínkù ìbàjẹ́, ìtẹ̀síwájú ìlera àti díndín iyeó kù ń pèsè oríṣiríṣi àǹfààní fún àwọn onírúurú àwọn àjọ. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera kánrinkése lè ṣẹ̀dá àwọn àǹfààní ìlera aláìsàn àti òṣìṣẹ́ nígbà tí wọ́n tún ń pa agbára mọ́, ṣe àdínkù ewu ojú ọjọ́ àti ìṣẹ̀dá àdínkù iyeó ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera àgbáyé ti pinnu láti lépa ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése nínú gbogbo iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ dènà àwọn ènìyàn ọjọ́ iwájú lọ́wọ́ àwọn ewu ìlera ọjọ́ òní tí wọ́n ń dàgbà. Ipa: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése lè pèsè àwọn àṣeyọrí pàtàkì fún ìlera, ojú ọjọ́ àti àyíká, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àǹfààní rẹ̀ lè jẹ́ rírí lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àfojúsùn ìlera àti ìdàgbàsókè lè jẹ́ àṣeyọrí papọ̀ nípa gbígbógun ti ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́, èyí tí ó ń ṣokùnfà ìṣirò mílíọ̀nù 7 ikú àìtọ́jọ́ lọ́dọọdún. Lára àwọn ohun abàyíkájẹ́ ni àwọn abojú-ọjọ́-jẹ́ alágbára ọlọ́jọ́ díẹ̀ (SLCPs) – pàápàá jùlọ èédú dúdú, òyì àrá àti gáàsì mẹtíìnì– tí wọ́n léwu púpọ̀ fún ìlera ṣùgbọ́n àsìkò ìsẹ̀mí wọn kì í pẹ́.
translations.reference: Sustainable development Overview: Sustainable development is a broad term to describe policies, projects and investments that provide benefits today without sacrificing environmental, social and personal health in the future. These policies are often described as green because they focus on limiting the impact of development on the environment. However, the benefits of sustainable development are also felt across a wide cross section of human health and well-being, including reductions in pollution- and environment-related disease, improved health outcomes and decreased stress. As the threats of climate change become increasingly important, win-win strategies for mitigation, health improvement and cost savings offer a range of advantages for various stakeholders. For example, greener health care operations can generate patient and worker health benefits while also saving energy, mitigating climate risks and creating long-term cost savings. The World Health Organization is committed to pursuing sustainable development in all its work to help protect the people of tomorrow from the health growing health risks of today. Impact: Many sustainable development strategies can offer significant wins for health, climate and the environment, and the benefits can be seen almost immediately. For example, many health and development goals can be achieved simultaneously by tackling air pollution, which is responsible for an estimated 7 million premature deaths annually. Polluting emissions include powerful short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—particularly black carbon, ozone and methane—which are very harmful to health but have only brief atmospheric lifetimes.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Geriatric Syndromes: Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment** Comprehensive geriatric assessment is a multidisciplinary process to evaluate the functional status, cognitive function, and co-morbidities that affect the well-being of older adults today and in the future. These assessments are particularly important because they focus on maximizing the impact of geriatric care on longevity. Specifically, geriatric syndromes affect a wide range of medical and health conditions, including frailty, chronic disease, advanced illness, and increased disability. Including fall-related injuries, the importance of eye health is also highlighted, with healthcare professionals focusing on preventing injuries, promoting health, and reducing mortality rates for various populations. For example, geriatric health specialists can create health interventions and treatments that also increase energy levels, prevent eye disease, and reduce the risk of long-term mortality. The World Health Organization has committed to integrating geriatric assessment into all of its work to help people in the future cope with the health risks of today. Impact: Many geriatric specialists can achieve significant successes in health, eye health, and longevity, and their impact can be substantial. For example, many health and geriatric interventions can be successful in reducing the incidence of infectious diseases, which cause approximately 7 million preventable deaths annually. Among the emerging threats are low-dose, high-impact environmental hazards – primarily air pollution, water contamination, and greenhouse gases – which pose a significant threat to health but whose impact is often underestimated.

translations.source: Àwọn ìlànà ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àti ìdókòwò tí wọ́n ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àwùjọ mímọ́, pẹ̀lú ìrìn àti kẹ̀kẹ́ gígùn, lè ní ipa rere lórí dídára afẹ́fẹ́. Èyí lè ṣe àdínkù àwọn ewu ìlera lásìkò kan náà, tí ó fi mọ́ ẹ̀gbà, àìsàn ọkàn, àìsàn ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró àti àwọn jẹjẹrẹ kan. Wọ́n tún lè ṣe àdínkù ìṣirò àwọn ikú mílíọ̀nù 1.25 ọdọọdún láti ara ìfarapa òpópónà, nígbà tí ó ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àǹfààní sí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn ènìyàn pọ̀ sí. Àwọn ìlànà ilégbèé ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése lè túbọ̀ ṣe àdínkù àwọn ìtújáde nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìpinnu bí ilẹ̀ ilé àti ìlò ilẹ̀, àwọn àṣàyàn ohun èlò ìkọ́lé, àwọn àbùdá àgbékalẹ̀ àti afẹ́fẹ́ òhun agbára. Èsì WHO: Iṣẹ́ WHO ń gbà ìtọ́nisọ́nà láti ọwọ́ àwọn Àfojúsùn Ìdàgbàsókè Kánrinkése, pàápàá jùlọ Àfojúsùn 3: "Ẹ rí i dájú pé ẹ ní ìgbé ayé ìlera àti ìgbéga àlàáfíà fún gbogbo ọjọ́ orí." Àwọn Àfojúsùn náà mú àwọn iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àti àwọn ìlànà káàkiri Àjọ wà, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìtọ́nisọ́nà lórí ilégbèé, ìdàgbàsókè ìlú, àwọn ètò ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àti àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́jú ìlera. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn ilégbèé WHO àti ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìlera, tí wọ́n kọ́kọ́ tẹ̀ jáde ní 2018, ṣe ìdámọ̀ ipa àwọn ìṣòro ilégbèé tí ó ń pọ̀ sí i lórí ìlera ọmọnìyàn nítorí ìdàgbàsókè ìlú, ojú ọjọ́ àti àwọn àyípadà iye ènìyàn. Àwọn ìtọ́nisọ́nà ń pèsè àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́, àwọn alábàáṣiṣẹ́pọ̀ àti àwùjọ pẹ̀lú àwọn àbá adálórí-ẹ̀rí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìtó ààyè ìgbé (ọ̀pọ̀ èèyàn), àwọn ìwọ̀n ìgbóná kékeré àti gọbọi inú ilé, àwọn ewu ìfarapa nínú ilé, àti níní àǹfààní sí ilégbèé fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní ìṣòro iṣẹ́ ṣíṣe. Ètò Ìlera Ìgboro (EII) tún fojúsun àwọn ọ̀nà fún ìlú láti fààyè gba ìlera rere nípa ṣíṣe àyàṣe iṣẹ́ tí ó múnádóko lórí ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ ìlú àti àwọn abàyíkájẹ́ ojú ọjọ́ ọlọ́jọ́ péréte (SLCPs) – gbígba ẹ̀mí là nípa mímú àjọṣepọ̀ bá ìlera, àyíká àti ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé tún ṣe àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àgbájọ Afẹ́fẹ́ àti Ojú ọjọ́ mímọ́ lórí Ìpolongo Ayé Ìmí, èyí tí ó fojúsun mímú àdínkù bá ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ àti mímú àdínkù bá mílíọ̀nù 7 ikú àìtọ́jọ́ lọ́dọọdún tí ó ń fà.
translations.reference: Transport policies and investments that favour clean public transport, along with walking and cycling, can have a dramatic impact on air quality. This can reduce multiple health risks at the same time, including stroke, heart attack, lung disease and some cancers. They can also reduce the estimated 1.25 million deaths annually from traffic injury, while also improving access to health services, particularly in densely populated areas. Sustainable development housing policies can further reduce emissions through considerations such as building siting and land use, choices of construction materials, design features and ventilation and energy. WHO response: WHO’s work is guided by the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goal 3: “Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages.” The Goals inform projects and policies across the Organization, including guidelines on housing, urban development, transportation systems and health care delivery. For example, the WHO Housing and health guidelines, first published in 2018, highlight the increasing impact of housing conditions on human health in light of urban growth, climate and demographic changes. The guidelines provide Member States, partners and the public with evidence-based recommendations relevant to inadequate living space (crowding), low and high indoor temperatures, injury hazards in the home, and accessibility of housing for people with functional impairments. The Urban Health Initiative (UHI) also focuses on ways for cities to enable good health by catalysing effective action on urban air pollution and short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—saving lives by linking health, environment and sustainable development. WHO also collaborates with the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC) on the Breathe Life campaign, which aims to cut air pollution and reduce the 7 million premature deaths annually that it causes.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Strategies for household and community water safety, supported by chlorine and iodine, can have a positive impact on diarrhea. This can reduce the risk of diseases such as dysentery, typhoid fever, cholera, and others. They can also reduce the estimated 1.25 million annual deaths from diarrheal diseases, while simultaneously straining health services, especially in densely populated areas. Integrated water management approaches can further increase benefits through interventions such as household water treatment, water storage practices, sanitation options, and powerful disinfectants. WHO's Role: WHO receives support from the Global Water, Sanitation and Hygiene (WASH) Partners, particularly the Sanitation and Hygiene Fund 3: "To ensure a healthy life and well-being for all." These partners drive actions and strategies across the organization, focusing on support for sanitation, urban WASH, household water treatment programs, and health risk reduction. For example, WHO's sanitation and health guidelines, first released in 2018, assess the impact of increasing sanitation challenges on child health due to urbanization, aging infrastructure, and declining human resources. The guidelines address member states, partners, and communities with vulnerable populations, including those experiencing poverty, small and large household sizes, household safety hazards, and the impact on sanitation for people with disabilities. The Community Health Program (CHP) also focuses on ways for cities to improve health by addressing work-related risks that affect urban air pollution and persistent environmental diseases (SLCPs) – reducing mortality through collaboration with health, sanitation, and integrated WASH. The Global Health Electors Forum also collaborates with the Global Air and Health Alliance on the Global Health Agenda, which focuses on reducing air pollution and reducing the estimated 7 million preventable deaths per year it causes."

translations.source: Sífílì Ìsọnísókí: Sífílì jẹ́ àìsàn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ tí kòkòrò bakitéríà ń tàn ká láti ọwọ́ Treponema pallidum èyí tí ó máa ń fa ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ewu àìsàn àti ikú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ṣe é wòsàn. Sífílì máa ń ràn ká nípasẹ̀ ìfarakínra ìbálòpọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ògòdò àrùn, nípasẹ̀ ìfàjẹ̀sínilára tàbí láti ara aláboyún obìnrin sí ọmọ inú rẹ̀. Ìkóràn sífílì láti ara ìyá-sí-ọmọ sáábà máa ń le púpọ̀ fún ọlẹ̀ ní àwọn ọ̀ràn tí wọ́n kò dá mọ̀ tàbí ṣe ìtọ́jú tó níbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìlóyún. Sífílì ìyálọ́mọ, nígbà tí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, wọ́n pẹ́ tọ́jú rẹ̀ tàbí wọn kò tọ́jú rẹ pẹlu pẹnisilíìnì, ń yọrí sí àwọn àbájáde ìbí líle nínú ìṣirò àwọn ọ̀ràn 50-80%, dá lórí ìpele sífílì. Àwọn àbájáde ìbí líle sáábà máa ń tí ó fi mọ́ àbíkú (lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà), ikú ìkókó, àìgbó, ìwọ̀n ìwúwo ìbí pínnísín, àti àwọn ìkókó tí àìsàn sọ di abiùn. Sífílì lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ àwọn àṣà ìbálòpọ̀ aláàbò tí ó fi mọ́ ìlò ọ̀rá ìdáàbòbò bí ó ti tọ́ àti déédéé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kò ní èyíkéyìí àmì àìsàn kankan tàbí ní àwọn àmì àìsàn kékeré tí wọ́n kì í ṣàkíyèsí. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ àìsàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ọ̀ràn àìsàn yóò dènà ìkóràn síwájú àti àbájáde ìlóyún líle tí ó fi mọ́ àbájáde àrùn sífílì. Àyẹ̀wò lè ṣe é ṣe nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn àyẹ̀wò ayákánkán tí wọ́n lè pèsè àbájáde ní kò tó ìṣẹ́jú 20, tí ó ń fi ààyè gba ìtọ́jú àsìkò.
translations.reference: Syphilis Overview: Syphilis is a bacterial sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by Treponema pallidum which results in substantial morbidity and mortality, and it is curable. Syphilis is transmitted through sexual contact with the infectious lesions, via blood transfusion or from a pregnant woman to her fetus. Mother-to-child transmission of syphilis is usually devastating to the fetus in cases that were not detected or treated sufficiently early in pregnancy. Maternal syphilis, when untreated, treated late or not treated with penicillin, results in adverse birth outcomes (ABOs) estimated in 50−80% of cases, depending on the stage of syphilis. These ABOs are often severe and include stillbirth (most commonly), neonatal death, prematurity, low birth weight, and congenitally infected infants. Syphilis can be prevented through safer sex practices including the correct and consistent use of condoms. Many people with syphilis do not have any symptoms or have only minor symptoms that go unnoticed. Identifying infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes including congenital syphilis. Screening can be done using rapid tests that can provide results in less than 20 minutes, allowing for immediate treatment.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Syphilis: Explanation** Syphilis is a sexually transmitted disease caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum, which can cause many health problems and death, and can be treated. Syphilis is transmitted through sexual contact with infected individuals, through blood transfusions, or from a pregnant woman to her fetus. Congenital syphilis (syphilis from mother to child) can be very serious for the child, causing conditions that are not well understood or have severe consequences. Congenital syphilis, when not treated, is treated with penicillin, which leads to a significant improvement in outcomes within the first 50-80% of cases, depending on the stage of syphilis. These improved outcomes can prevent stillbirth (often), neonatal death, neurodevelopmental disabilities, and other complications that the disease can cause. Syphilis can be prevented through safe sexual practices that include consistent and correct condom use. Many people with syphilis have no symptoms or have mild symptoms that are not noticed. Early disease detection through screening and treatment of infections will prevent further transmission and prevent life-threatening complications associated with syphilis. Testing can be done by performing a variety of tests that can provide results in less than 20 minutes, allowing for timely diagnosis.

translations.source: Nígbà tí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, sífílì yóò lọ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò la onírúurú ìpele kọjá. Ìtọ́jú fún sífílì máa ń jẹ́ ṣíṣe pẹ̀lú òògùn pẹnisilíìnì benzathine tí ó ṣe é gún bí abẹ́rẹ́, pẹ̀lú ìlò 1 sí 3 èyí tí ó dá lórí ìpele àìsàn náà. Pẹnisilíìnì nìkan ló lè dènà àwọn sífílì àsìkò ìbí. Ìtọ́jú pẹ̀lú pẹnisilíìnì ti ní ìdènà láti ọwọ́ ọ̀wọ́ngógó òòrèkóòrè àti ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ ní àwọn onírúurú orílẹ̀ èdè, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi, pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kì í ṣàfihàn àwọn àmì àìsàn tàbí wọn kì í ní ìmọ̀lára wọn. Sífílì, àìtọ́jú lo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó pín sí onírúurú àwọn ìpele:. Ìkóràn ìbálòpọ̀ máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀, ìyẹn ni pé, fún bí ọdún méjì lẹ́yìn àrùn. Lásìkò ìpele ìsáájú (sífílì ìpìlẹ̀), èyí tí yóò lo bí ọjọ́ 21, egbò àdáwà, aláìnírora roboto tí ó sáábà máa ń le kan (egbò) yóò farahàn ní agbègbè ìgbàsára (ìfarakanra), ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà nínú òbò, okó tàbí fùrọ̀ (ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè jẹ́ àfikún ojú ara) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè lọ láìfarahàn. Ògòdò náà yóò gba ìwòsàn pẹ̀lú tàbí láìsí ìwòsàn láàrin ọ̀sẹ̀ 3 tàbí 10. Bí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, àìsàn náà yóò tẹ̀síwájú sí ìpele àtẹ̀lé.
translations.reference: When untreated, syphilis lasts many years and goes through different stages. Treatment for syphilis is done with injectable penicillin benzathine, with 1 to 3 doses depending on the stage of the disease. Only penicillin can prevent congenital syphilis. Treatment with penicillin has been hampered by periodic and prolonged shortages of benzathine penicillin in numerous countries, including high-, low- and middle-income countries. Symptoms: Many people with syphilis do not present symptoms or do not perceive them. Untreated, syphilis lasts many years and is characterized into different stages. Sexual transmission typically occurs during early stages, i.e., up to 2 years after infection. During the initial phase (primary syphilis), which lasts on average 21 days, a solitary, painless, usually hard and round sore (chancre) appears at the site of inoculation (contact), often in the vagina, penis or anus (but it may also be extra-genital) and may go unnoticed. The chancre heals with or without treatment within 3 to 10 weeks. If untreated, the disease progresses to the secondary stage.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"When syphilis is not treated, it can progress for many years and pass through various stages. Treatment for syphilis is done with penicillin benzathine injections, which are given as an initial dose, with a dose of 1 to 3, depending on the severity of the disease. Penicillin alone can cure early-stage syphilis. Treatment with penicillin has been effective from the efforts of public health workers and long-term doctors in various countries, effectively controlling countries with high, intermediate, and low rates of syphilis. Symptoms: Many people with syphilis do not show symptoms or are unaware of them. Syphilis can progress for many years and pass through various stages:. Primary infection usually occurs during the early stages, that is, for up to two years after infection. During the early stage (latent syphilis), this lasts for about 21 days, with a painless, hard chancre that may appear in the genital area (groin), often in the vagina, cervix, or urethra (but can also be an oral lesion), and it may appear without a chancre. The lesion will heal with or without treatment within 3 to 10 weeks. If not treated, the disease will progress to the late stage."

translations.source: Sífílì àtẹ̀lé ní máa ń ní àbùdá kúrúkùsá tí ó lè jẹ́ onírúurú kí ó sì sé ìsìnjẹ àwọn ìṣòro àrùn tàbí àìsàn mìíràn, ṣùgbọ́n ó sáábà máa ń mú àtẹ́lẹwọ́ àti àtẹ̀lesẹ̀. Kúrúkùsá náà kì í húnni bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè kéré láti jẹ́ àfojúfò. Ní àwọn ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n gbóná tí wọ́n sì ní omi, bí ihò ìdí àti ẹnu, ògòdò títóbi funfun tàbí ewú lè dàgbà ní ọ̀gangan egbò ìpìlẹ̀ (condyloma lata). Àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti àmì sífílì àtẹ̀lé máa ń sàn fúnra rẹ̀, kódà láìsí ìtọ́jú. Láìsí ìtọ́jú, ìpele afarasin bẹ̀rẹ̀. Sífílì afarasin kì í ṣàfihàn àwọn àmì àìsàn àti àpẹẹrẹ kankan bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìtọ́jú rẹ̀ yàtọ̀ sí ìpele ìbẹ̀rẹ̀. Lẹ́yìn àwọn ọdún tàbí ẹ̀wádún láìsí ìtọ́jú, àwọn àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ọ̀páyìnmọ́pọlọ àti àwọn àìsàn ọkàn àti àwọn mìíràn lè wáyé. Ìtọ́jú: Àyẹ̀wò ìtọ́jú sífílì máa ń dá lórí ìtàn, àyẹ̀wò ojúkojú, àyẹ̀wò láàbù ènìyàn àti nígbà mìíràn àyẹ̀wò ẹ̀rọ afẹ́wẹ́lẹ́, nítorí àmì àìsàn kò wọ́pọ̀ tàbí farahàn. Ìdámọ̀ àrùn aláìlámì àìsàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ọ̀ràn àrùn yóò dènà ìkóràn síwájú sí i àti àwọn àbájáde oyún líle, tí ó fi mọ́ sífílì amútinúwá. Àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù fún sífílì tí ó fi mọ́ ìdámọ̀ tààrà T. pallidum tàbí ìdámọ̀ tààrà bí àyẹ̀wò sẹ́rọ́ọ̀mù inú ẹ̀jẹ̀.
translations.reference: Secondary syphilis is characterized by a rash that can vary widely and mimic other infectious or non-infectious conditions, but characteristically affects the palms and soles. The rash is non-itchy and can be minimal enough to be overlooked. In warm and moist areas of the body, such as the anus and labia, large white or grey raised lesions may develop at the location of the primary chancre (condyloma lata). The symptoms and signs of secondary syphilis spontaneously resolve, even without treatment. Without treatment, the latent stage starts. Latent syphilis presents no clinical symptoms or signs and the treatment is different from the early phase. After years or decades without treatment, neurological and cardiovascular diseases among others may occur. Treatment: Syphilis diagnosis is based on the person’s history, physical examination, laboratory testing and sometimes radiology, as symptoms are not common or noticeable. Identifying asymptomatic infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes, including congenital syphilis. Laboratory tests for syphilis include direct detection of T. pallidum or indirect methods such as serology.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Syphilis has a long incubation period that can be varied and can manifest as symptoms of various diseases or other ailments, but it often causes sores and rashes. The incubation period can be quite long and may be short enough to be asymptomatic. In areas of the body that are moist, such as the genitals, anus, and mouth, large, cauliflower-like growths can develop in the genital area (condyloma lata). Manifestations and symptoms of syphilis can appear suddenly, even without warning. Without treatment, the latent stage begins. Latent syphilis does not show any signs of disease or symptoms, and its presentation differs from the initial stage. After years or decades without treatment, serious complications such as neurosyphilis, cardiovascular disease, and others can develop. Diagnosis: Diagnosis of syphilis is based on history, physical examination, laboratory tests, and sometimes, electronic examination, because the symptoms are not always present or obvious. Accurate diagnosis of the infectious disease through laboratory tests and examination of symptoms will lead to further investigation and prevention of serious consequences, which can be linked to syphilis. Laboratory tests for syphilis can detect *Treponema pallidum* or detect it as a serological test."

**Key Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Medical Terminology:** I've used standard English medical terminology to accurately convey the meaning.
*   **Cultural Context:** I've tried to maintain the clarity and directness of the original Yoruba text.
*   **Specificity:** I've included the scientific name of the bacteria (*Treponema pallidum*) where appropriate.
*   **Flow:** I've aimed for a natural and readable English flow.

translations.source: Àwọn àgbéyẹ̀wò àti àyẹ̀wò kánkán tún wà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè pèsè àwọn àbájáde ní kò tó ìṣẹ́jú 20, tí yóò sì mú ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìtọ́jú kánkán wá. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé dábàá pé kí sífílì máa jẹ́ títọ́jú nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò abẹ́rẹ́ pẹnisilíìnì (benzathine penicillin G) pẹ̀lú ìlò 1 tàbí mẹ́ta, dá lórí ìpele àìsàn náà. WHO ti ṣàlàyé àwọn ìlànà ìtọ́jú fún sífílì, àti ọkàn pàtàkì fún àyẹ̀wò àti ìtọ́jú sífílì nínú oyún. Sífílì amútinúwá Kò sí àyẹ̀wò kánkán báyìí fún àwọn sífílì amútinúwá. Gbogbo alààyè tàbí àbíkú ìkókó àwọn obìnrin tí wọ́n ní sífílì gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ àgbéyẹ̀wò fún ẹ̀rí sífílì amútinúwá. Àwọn ìkókó àbíyè gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ yíyẹ̀wò àti àyẹ̀wò lásìkò ìbí láti sọ òté ìtọ́jú. Nígbà tí wọ́n bá tọ́ka sí i, bákan náà ní fún àlàfo oṣooṣù fún oṣù 3 títí àyẹ̀wò sẹ́rọ́ọ̀mù inú ẹ̀jẹ̀ ìkókó, tí ó sì wà, láìsí àmì àìsàn, nítorí àwọn àjẹsára ìyá lè ran ọmọ náà lásìkò oyún láìṣeṣẹ́.
translations.reference: Rapid screening and diagnostic tests are also available and can provide results in less than 20 minutes, facilitating immediate treatment initiation. WHO recommends syphilis to be treated with injectable penicillin (benzathine penicillin G) with 1 or 3 doses, depending on the stage of the disease. WHO has detailed treatment guidelines for syphilis, and one specifically for the screening and treatment of syphilis in pregnancy. Congenital syphilis There are currently no diagnostic tests for congenital syphilis. All live or stillborn infants of women with syphilis should be examined for evidence of congenital syphilis. Live-born infants should be examined and tested at birth to define treatment requirement. When indicated, also at monthly intervals for 3 months until the infant’s serology are, and remain, negative, as maternal antibodies can be passively transmitted to the baby during pregnancy.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Screening and confirmatory tests can also be done in less than 20 minutes, which will lead to the start of confirmatory diagnosis. The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends that syphilis be treated with a single intramuscular dose of benzathine penicillin G, either one or three doses, depending on the severity of the disease. WHO has outlined diagnostic guidelines for syphilis, and essential practices for screening and diagnosing syphilis in children.

Congenital Syphilis
There is currently no confirmatory test for congenital syphilis. All pregnant or recently delivered women with syphilis must be screened for congenital syphilis. Pregnant women should be screened and tested during pregnancy to determine the need for treatment. If indicated, they should receive monthly injections for 3 months until the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) tests are negative, without signs of disease, because midwives can safely care for the child during infancy. "

translations.source: Taeníásí àti sisikasósì Ìsọnísókí: Àìsàn pẹ̀lú aràn pẹlẹbẹ atara-ẹrankosẹ̀ lè fa àwọn ìṣòro ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ méjì: àrùn taẹniásì àti sisikakósì. Taeníásí jẹ́ àrùn ìfun pẹ̀lú aràn pẹlẹbẹ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó máa ń wáyé nígbà tí ènìyàn bá jẹ ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ tútù tàbí tí kò jinná, tàbí ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ tí ó ní àìsàn. Taeníásí kò ní ipa gbòógì kankan lórí ìlera ẹ̀dá. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn ẹyin aràn pẹlẹbẹ tí ẹni tí ó ní aràn pẹlẹbẹ nínú yóò jáde bọ́ síta lára ìgbẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àìsí ìmọ́tótó tí ó tọ́ lè mú àyíká di àìmọ́. Nígbà tí aràn pẹlẹbẹ lè mú ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ ṣàìsàn, ẹyin T.solium náà lè mú kí ènìyàn ṣàìsàn bí ènìyàn bá jẹ wọn (nípasẹ̀ ọ̀nà ìgbẹ́-sí-ẹnu, tàbí nípa jíjẹ oúnjẹ tàbí omi tí ó ti di àìmọ́). Jíjẹ ẹyin aràn pẹlẹbẹ máa ń fa àìsàn pẹ̀lú ajọ̀fẹ́ bí ìdin nínú àwọn ìṣù ara bí isan, awọ ara, ojú àti ètò òpónà ọpọlọmẹ́yìn àárín gbùngbùn (sisikakósì ènìyàn), pẹ̀lú ipá líle lórí ìlera. Nígbà tí àwọn ògòdò paali bá dàgbà nínú ọpọlọ, àìsàn náà ni wọ́n ń pè ní nirosisikakósì. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Taeníásí sáábà máa ń ní àbùdá abọ́ọ́dé àti àwọn àbùdá aláìsọpàtó tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìsàn ìfun (inú rírun, àyà rínrìn, ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, abbl.). Àwọn àmì àìsàn wọ̀nyí lè tẹ̀síwájú títí aràn pẹlẹbẹ náà yóò fi kú lẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú, bí bẹ́ẹ̀ kọ́ ó lè wà láyé fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún.
translations.reference: Taeniasis and cysticercosis Overview: Infection with the tapeworm Taenia solium can result in two distinct conditions: taeniasis and cysticercosis. Taeniasis is the intestinal infection with the tapeworm and it occurs when a person eats raw or undercooked, infected pork. Taeniasis has no major impact on human health. However, tapeworm eggs pass in the faeces of the tapeworm carrier and in the absence of a proper sanitation may contaminate the environment. While tapeworm eggs are infective for pigs, T. solium eggs may also infect humans if they are ingested by a person (via the fecal-oral route, or by ingesting contaminated food or water). Ingestion of tapeworm eggs causes infection with the larval parasite in the tissues such as muscles, skin, eyes and the central nervous system (human cysticercosis), with possible devastating effects on health. When cysts develop in the brain, the condition is referred to as neurocysticercosis. Symptoms: Taeniasis is usually characterized by mild and non-specific symptoms associated to an intestinal infection (abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhoea, etc.). These symptoms may continue until the tapeworm dies following treatment, otherwise it may live for years.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Taeniasis and Cysticercosis: Illness with tapeworm can cause two serious problems: taeniasis and cysticercosis.** Taeniasis is an infection with a tapeworm and occurs when a person eats undercooked meat or contaminated food, or meat from an infected animal. Taeniasis has no significant impact on health. However, the tapeworm eggs that the person with a tapeworm passes in their stool can cause illness. If these eggs contaminate the soil, they can cause cysticercosis. When the tapeworm can cause the meat to become infected, the T. solium can cause a person to become infected by eating them (through fecal-oral transmission, or by consuming contaminated food or water). Eating tapeworm eggs can cause illness with seizures, such as lesions in the brain, skin, eyes, and the central nervous system (human cysticercosis), with a significant impact on health. When the larvae grow in the brain, the illness is called neurocysticercosis.

**Symptoms:** Taeniasis often has abdominal pain and non-specific symptoms that occur with infection (nausea, large appetite, constipation, etc.). These symptoms can persist until the tapeworm dies after treatment, or they can last for many years.

translations.source: Fún sisikakósì, àsìkò ìsàba rẹ̀ máa ń yàtọ̀, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn tí wọ́n ní àìsàn náà lè máa ní àmì àìsàn kankan fún ọ̀pọ̀ ọdún. Ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àrùn yìí tí wọ́pọ̀, àwọn tí wọ́n ń ṣe àìsàn yìí lè ní àwọn ògòdò tí ó hànde tàbí ńlá (kókó tàbí ògòdò kékeré líle) tí ó wà nísàlẹ̀ awọ. Nirosisikakósì ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn onírúurú àmì àti àpẹẹrẹ tí ó dá lórí iye, ìtóbi, ìpele àti ibi àwọn àyípadà ara àti èsì àjẹsára aláìsàn àti àbùdá ajọ̀fẹ́ náà pẹ̀lú, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè má ní àmì àìsàn ìlera kankan. Àwọn àmì àìsàn lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn orí fífọ́ líle, ẹ̀fọ́jú, gìrì (wárápá bí wọ́n ba jẹ́ òòrèkóòrè), ọta orí, yínrùnyínrùn, ìgbàgbé àti àwọn àmì àìsàn tí àwọn ògòdò tí wọ́n gba àwọn ààyè ètò àkóso ibùdó ara ń fà. Ìtọ́jú: Ìtọ́jú taeníásí láti ọwọ́ Taenia solium ṣe pàtàkì láti dènà nirosisikakósì àti gẹ́gẹ́ bí irinṣẹ́ láti ṣàkóso tàbí dènà ìtànká ajọ̀fẹ́. Ìtọ́jú lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe lára ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, tàbí Àkóso Òògùn gbogbogbò èyí tí ó dá lórí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ abẹ́lé àti àwọn ìlànà ìṣàkóso tí wọ́n ń ṣàmúlò. Taeníásí lè jẹ́ títọ́jú nípa lílo àwọn òògùn agbógunti-ajọ̀fẹ́ (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). Nínú nirosisikakósì, níwọ̀n ìgbà tí bíba àwọn ògòdò líle jẹ́ lè fa èsì ìrẹ̀dòdò, ìtọ́jú pàtàkì àìsàn tí ó ń ṣọṣẹ́ nílò bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn iṣẹ́ ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn ìlò òògùn agbógunti-ajọ̀fẹ́, àti ìwòsàn alátìlẹ́yìn pẹ̀lú corticosteroids àti/tàbí òògùn agbógunti-wárápá, àti bóyá iṣẹ́ abẹ. Ìlò àti àsìkò ìtọ́jú lè yàtọ̀ dáadáa bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè dá lórí iye, ìwọ̀n, agbègbè, àti ìpele ìdàgbàsókè oyún líle, ìrẹ̀dòdò àyíká ọṣẹ́, ìṣòro àti bí àwọn àmì àìsàn tàbí àmì ṣe lè tó.
translations.reference: For cysticercosis, the incubation period is variable, and infected people may remain asymptomatic for years. In some endemic regions, infected people may develop visible or palpable nodules (a small solid bump or node which is solid that can be detected by touch) beneath the skin. Neurocysticercosis is associated with a variety of signs and symptoms depending on the number, size, stage and location of the pathological changes as well as the host’s immune response and the parasite’s genotype, but it can also be clinically asymptomatic. Symptoms may include chronic headaches, blindness, seizures (epilepsy if they are recurrent), hydrocephalus, meningitis, dementia and symptoms caused by lesions occupying spaces of the central nervous system. Treatment: The treatment of taeniasis by Taenia solium is important to prevent neurocysticercosis and as a tool to assist in controlling or stopping the parasite transmission cycle. The treatment can be done on an individual bases, or as Mass Drug Administration depending on the local circumstances and the control approaches being implemented. Taeniasis can be treated using anthelmintics (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). In neurocysticercosis, since the destruction of cysts may lead to an inflammatory response, specialised treatment of active disease is required and may include long courses with high doses of anthelmintic, as well as supporting therapy with corticosteroids and/or anti-epileptic drugs, and possibly surgery. The dosage and the duration of treatment can vary greatly and depend mainly on the number, size, location and developmental stage of the cysts, their surrounding inflammatory edema, acuteness and severity of clinical symptoms or signs.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"For neurocysticercosis, the clinical presentation can vary, and those with the condition may not have any symptoms for many years. In areas where this disease is common, those who are ill may have small or large cysts (sometimes small cysts in the liver or small cysts in the muscles) that are visible in the skin. Neurocysticercosis is characterized by various lesions and patterns that depend on the number, location, stage, and viability of the cysts and the associated inflammatory response and the need for treatment, but it may also not have any neurological symptoms. Symptoms can include seizures, fever, headache (sometimes severe), muscle weakness, dizziness, fatigue, and symptoms caused by cysts that affect the central nervous system control mechanisms. Diagnosis: Diagnosis, especially from Taenia solium, is crucial for diagnosing neurocysticercosis and, importantly, for controlling or eliminating hydatid disease. Diagnosis can be performed on an individual basis, or through Comprehensive Disease Control, based on epidemiological events and control measures being implemented. Taeniasis can be diagnosed using anthelmintic drugs (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). In neurocysticercosis, when cysts in the liver cause bleeding, a critical and urgent treatment is needed, which may involve prolonged medical care with anthelmintic drugs, and supportive care with corticosteroids and/or antihistamines, and possibly surgical intervention. The use and management of treatment can vary greatly and depend on the number, severity, location, and stage of the cysts in the liver, the overall clinical condition, the severity of the symptoms, and how the symptoms or lesions are progressing."

**Important Notes:**

*   **Medical Terminology:** I've tried to use accurate English medical terminology. However, nuances in the Yoruba language might require further clarification by a medical professional.
*   **Context:** This translation assumes a medical/scientific context.
*   **Accuracy:** While I've aimed for accuracy, subtle differences in meaning can sometimes occur during translation.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì Ìsọnísókí: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì jẹ́ àìsàn líle tí ènìyàn máa ń kó láti ara ìfaragbá ọmọ àwọn bakitéríà, Clostridium tetani, èyí tí ó ń gbé nínú ilẹ̀, itọ́, eruku àti ìgbẹ́. Bakitéríà náà lè wọ inú ara nípasẹ̀ egbò ọgbẹ́ jínjìn, àwọn ìfarapa tàbí àpá iná tí wọ́n ń nípa lórí ètò àkóso ara. Àìsàn náà máa ń yọrí sí àwọn ìsúnkì isan ara, pàápàá jùlọ àgbọ̀n àti isan ọrùn, beet sì ni s mọ̀ ọ́n sí "ìwayínpọ̀". Gbogbo àwọn ènìyàn pátá lè ní tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ṣùgbọ́n àìsàn náà wọ́pọ̀ ó sì lè láàrin àwọn ọmọ tuntun àti àwọn ìyá wọn nígbà tí ìyá náà kò bá ní ààbò láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, tetanus toxoid. Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó ń farahàn lásìkò oyún tàbí láàrin ọ̀sẹ̀ 6 ní ìgbẹ̀yìn oyún náà ni a ń pè ní tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìyálọ́mọ, nígbà tí tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó farahàn láàrin ọjọ́ 28 lẹ́yìn ìbí ni tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó. Àìsàn náà jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ pàtàkì ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ẹ̀ka àgbáyé, ṣùgbọ́n paapapat jùlọ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín tàbí àdúgbò, níbi tí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára ti kéré tí àwọn àṣà ìbímọ aláìní ìmọ́tótó pọ̀ sí. Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé ṣírò pé ní 2018 (ọdún lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ tí àwọn ìṣirò rẹ̀ wà nílẹ̀), àwọn ìkókó 25 000 kú láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó, àdínkù 88% sí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ọdún 2000. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ẹ́ jẹ́ pé gbogbo ọ̀ràn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ń ṣẹlẹ̀ láàrin àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kò tíì gba abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tàbí àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn kò tíì ṣe ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àgbàkún wọn. Àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti àmì àìsàn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì máa farahàn lásìkò kí àsìkò láti ọjọ́ 3 sí 21 lẹ́yìn tí bakitéríà tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì bá ti wọ inú ara nípasẹ̀ egbò kan.
translations.reference: Tetanus Overview: Tetanus is a serious illness contracted through exposure to the spores of the bacterium, Clostridium tetani, which live in soil, saliva, dust and manure. The bacteria can enter the body through a deep cuts, wounds or burns affecting the nervous system. The infection leads to painful muscle contractions, particularly of the jaw and neck muscle, and is commonly known as “lockjaw”. People of all ages can get tetanus but the disease is particularly common and serious in newborn babies and their mothers when the mother is unprotected from tetanus by the vaccine, tetanus toxoid. Tetanus occurring during pregnancy or within 6 weeks of the end of pregnancy is called maternal tetanus, while tetanus occurring within the first 28 days of life is called neonatal tetanus. The disease remains an important public health problem in many parts of the world, but especially in low-income countries or districts, where immunization coverage is low and unclean birth practices are common. WHO estimates that in 2018 (the latest year for which estimates are available), 25 000 newborns died from neonatal tetanus, 88% reduction from the situation in 2000. Symptoms: Nearly all cases of tetanus occur in people who have never been vaccinated or in adults who have not kept up to date on their booster shots. Signs and symptoms of tetanus appear anytime from 3 to 21 days after tetanus bacteria enter the body through a wound.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Tetanus (Lockjaw):** Tetanus is an infectious disease caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani, which lives in soil, dust, rust, and manure. The bacteria can enter the body through deep wounds, injections, or burns that bypass the body's control system. The disease often leads to muscle spasms, most commonly in the jaw and neck, and is also known as "lockjaw."

All people are susceptible to tetanus, but the disease is more common among newborns and their mothers if the mother did not receive tetanus protection through vaccination, tetanus toxoid. Tetanus that occurs within the first few days or within the first 6 weeks of life is called neonatal tetanus, while tetanus that occurs between 28 days and a year after birth is called post-natal tetanus.

The disease is a major public health problem in many parts of the world, especially in low-income countries or regions where vaccination coverage is low and traditional birth practices are prevalent. The World Health Organization (WHO) reported that in 2018 (the year of its data), 25,000 newborns died from neonatal tetanus, a decrease of 88% compared to the year 2000.

**Symptoms:** It is rare for tetanus to occur in people who have been vaccinated or in adults who have kept their booster vaccinations up to date. The symptoms of tetanus usually appear from 3 to 21 days after the tetanus bacteria has entered the body through a wound.

translations.source: Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ọ̀ràn máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ láàrin ọjọ́ 14. Àmì àìsàn lè jẹ́: ìwayínpọ tàbí àìlágbára láti yanu ẹ̀gbọ̀n àwọn iṣu ara pàápàá jùlọ lẹ́yìn, ní inú àti àwọn òpin ara. ìnira àwọn ẹ̀gbọ̀n ìṣù ara lójijì èyí tí ariwo òjìji máa ń fà ìṣòro gbígbé nǹkan mi gìrì ẹ̀fọ́rí ibà àti òógùn lílà àyípadà nínú ìru ẹ̀jẹ̀ tàbí ìwọ̀n ọkàn tí ó ń sáré.
translations.reference: Most cases occur within 14 days. Symptoms can include: jaw cramping or the inability to open the mouth muscle spasms often in the back, abdomen and extremities sudden painful muscle spasms often triggered by sudden noises trouble swallowing seizures headache fever and sweating changes in blood pressure or fast heart rate.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"Many problems occur between days 14. The symptoms of illness can be: weakness or inability to urinate, especially after, inside, and in the urethra. These problems with urination are often accompanied by a severe burning sensation, which causes a great deal of discomfort and requires medication or treatment within the type of discharge or mental confusion that arises."

**Explanation of some choices:**

*   **"Between days 14"**: This is a slightly awkward phrasing in Yoruba, suggesting a problem arising around day 14 of something (perhaps a menstrual cycle, pregnancy, or illness progression).
*   **"Urethra"**:  "Àwọn òpin ara" directly translates to "the holes of the body," but in this context, it clearly refers to the urethra.
*   **"Severe burning sensation"**: "Ìṣòro gbígbé" is a strong term for discomfort, and "burning sensation" captures the essence of the "ariwo òjìji" (sound of fire).
*   **"Mental confusion"**: "Ìwọ̀n ọkàn" can refer to mental confusion or disorientation.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Nínú tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó, lára àwọn àmì aisant ni ẹ̀gbọ̀n ìṣù ara, èyí tí ó sáábà máa ń wá lẹ́yìn àìlágbára ìkókó láti fa ọyàn tàbí mu ọyàn, àti ẹkún sísun sá. Wọ́n maat ń ṣàyẹ̀wò tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì nípasẹ̀ àwọn àbùdá àìsàn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni kò nílò ìjẹ́rìí láàbù. Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì pè fún ìtọ́jú nínú ibùdó ìtọ́jú, lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà ní ilé ìwòsàn ìfiṣọwọ́sí. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n sàn láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì kò ní àjẹsára àìṣẹ̀dá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè kó àìsàn náà padà nítorí náà wọn nílò láti gba abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára. Ìdènà: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára pẹ̀lú àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tí wọ́n ní tetanus-toxoid (TTCV), èyí tí wọ́n kò pọ̀ sínú àwọn ètò àjẹsára káàkiri àgbáyé tí wọ́n sì máa ń ṣàmúlò lásìkò ìfaragbá ìtọ́jú aṣáájú ìbímọ. Láti rí ààbò títí ayé, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé dábàá pé ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan gbọ́dọ̀ gba abẹ́rẹ́ 6 (ìlò 3 ìpìlẹ̀ 3 àgbàkún) TTCV. Àwọn abala ìwọ̀n lílo ìpìlẹ̀ 3 náà gbọ́dọ̀ bẹ̀rẹ̀ láti ọjọ́ orí ọ̀sẹ̀ 6,pẹ̀lú àwọn ìwọ̀n lílo yòókù tí wọ́n gbọ́dọ̀ fún ọmọ láàrin àlàfo gbèdéke ọ̀sẹ̀ 4 ó kéré jù láàrin àwọn ìwọ̀n lílo. Àwọn àgbàkún 3 náà gbọ́dọ̀ di fífún lásìkò ọdún kejì nílé ayé (oṣù 12-23), ní ọjọ́ orí ọdún 4-7, àti ọjọ́ orí 9-15. Ó yẹ kí àlàfo ọdún 4 ó kéré jù wà láàrin àwọn àgbàkún ìwọ̀n lílò. Àwọn onírúurú abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára wà tí wọ́n ń lò láti dáàbò bo tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì: abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára diphtheria àti tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì, tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì, àti àwúbì (ikọ́ àwúbì) (DTap) àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára vaccinetatanus àti diphtheria (Td) vacccinetatanus, diphtheria, àti àwúbì (Tdap).
translations.reference: In neonatal tetanus, symptoms include muscle spasms, which are often preceded by the newborn’s inability to suck or breastfeed, and excessive crying. Tetanus is diagnosed on the basis of clinical features and does not require laboratory confirmation. Tetanus requires treatment in a medical facility, often in a referral hospital. People who recover from tetanus do not have natural immunity and can be infected again and therefore need to be immunized. Prevention: Tetanus can be prevented through immunization with tetanus-toxoid-containing vaccines (TTCV), which are included in routine immunization programmes globally and administered during antenatal care contacts. To be protected throughout life, WHO recommends that an individual receives 6 doses (3 primary plus 3 booster doses) of TTCV. The 3-dose primary series should begin as early as 6 weeks of age, with subsequent doses given with a minimum interval of 4 weeks between doses. The 3 booster doses should preferably be given during the second year of life (12–23 months), at 4–7 years of age, and at 9–15 years of age. Ideally, there should be at least 4 years between booster doses. There are many kinds of vaccines used to protect against tetanus: diphtheria and tetanus (DT) vaccinesdiphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis (whooping cough) (DTaP) vaccinestetanus and diphtheria (Td) vaccinestetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis (Tdap) vaccines.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"In the context of vaccine discourse, among the important vaccines is the tetanus vaccine, which is often administered after weakened immunity to prevent or mitigate tetanus and diphtheria. They examine the tetanus vaccine through disease prevention measures and do not require a prescription. Tetanus is a concern for assessment in the assessment area, often in the hospital setting. People who have recovered from tetanus do not have lasting immunity, so they may contract the disease again and therefore need to receive booster doses. 
Solution: Tetanus can be prevented through booster doses with tetanus-toxoid (TTCV) vaccines, which are included in global vaccination programs and are administered to reduce the risk of tetanus infection during pregnancy. To protect for a lifetime, the World Health Organization recommends that everyone receive 6 doses (3 primary doses and 3 boosters) of TTCV. The primary doses should begin from 6 weeks of age, with subsequent doses given to infants between 4 and 24 weeks of age. The 3 boosters should be administered within the first two years of life (12-23 months), at 4-7 years of age, and at 9-15 years of age. Infants 4 years of age or younger should be included in the booster dose schedule. Various booster vaccines are used to combat tetanus: diphtheria and tetanus vaccines (DTap), tetanus vaccines, and diphtheria (TTCV) (DTap), tetanus and diphtheria vaccines (Td), and tetanus, diphtheria, and pertussis vaccines (Tdap)."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Accuracy:** I've aimed for the most accurate translation possible, considering the nuances of both languages.
*   **Abbreviations:** I've kept the abbreviations (TTCV, DTap, Td, Tdap) as they are commonly used in the medical field.

translations.source: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ fífún àwọn obìnrin tí wọ́n ti tó ọmọ bí ní abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára pẹ̀lú TTCV, yálà lásìkò ìlóyún tàbí nígbà tí wọn kò lóyún. Ní àfikún, àwọn àṣà ìṣègùn gbígbòòrò náà lè dènà àìsàn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó fi mọ́ ìgbẹ̀bí tí ó ní ìmọ́tótó àti ìtọ́jú ìwọ́ lásìkò ìbímọ, àti ìtọ́jú egbò pípé fún àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ iṣẹ́ abẹ àti ìtọ́jú eyín.
translations.reference: Neonatal tetanus can be prevented by immunizing women of reproductive age with TTCV, either during pregnancy or outside of pregnancy. Additionally, robust medical practices can also prevent tetanus disease including clean delivery and cord care during childbirth, and proper wound care for surgical and dental procedures.
