translations.source: Ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà Ìsọnísókí: Ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà (TBE) jẹ́ okùnfà àwọn kòkòrò àrùn fáírọ́ọ̀sì líle tí ibùdó ètò ara ní ẹ̀ka ìlà oòrùn, àárín gbùngbùn, àríwá àti pàápàá jùlọ ìwọ̀ oòrùn àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè Yúróòpù, àti ní àríwá China, Mongolia, àti Orílẹ̀ èdè Russia. Ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà jẹ́ mọ̀lẹ́bí Flaviviridae. Ó sún mọ́ 10 000–12 000 àwọn ọ̀ràn ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà tí wọ́n jábọ̀ ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan ṣùgbọ́n iye yìí ni wọ́n ní ìgbàgbọ́ pé ó kéré púpọ̀ sí gbogbo iye àwọn ọ̀ràn àìsàn náà gan-an. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àrùn láti ara kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ń wáyé láti ara àwọn eégbọn tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì àìsàn náà, tí wọ́n maat ń lẹ̀ mọ́ ara fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọjọ́. Ní ìgbà mìíràn, àrùn lè wáyé láti ara jíjẹ wàrà tí wọn kò sè èyí tí wọ́n gbà láti ara àwọn ewúrẹ́, àgùntàn tàbí màlúù tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì àìsàn náà. Àwọn ènìyàn máa ń farakó àwọn eégbọn lásìkò àwọn iṣẹ́ ìta gbangba ní àwọn agbègbè igbó títí dé ibi gíga bí mítà 2000. Kò sí ìkóràn ènìyàn-sí-ènìyàn tààrà. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àrùn máa ń wà láìsí àmì àìsàn. Nínú ọ̀ràn àìsàn alábùdá, àsìkò ìsàba ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà máa ń tó ọjọ́ 2-28 (ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà ọjọ́ 7-14) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ọjọ́ 1-8 àwọn àmì àìsàn òtútù yóò tẹ̀lé, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀rẹ̀rẹ̀, ẹ̀fọ́rí àti ara wíwo, tí ó sáábà maat ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú ibà ≥ 38°C.
translations.reference: Tick-borne encephalitis Overview: Tick-borne encephalitis (TBE) is an important cause of viral infections of the central nervous system in eastern, central, northern and increasingly western European countries, and in northern China, Mongolia, and the Russian Federation. Tick-borne encephalitis virus is a member of the family Flaviviridae. Approximately 10 000–12 000 clinical cases of tick-borne encephalitis are reported each year, but this figure is believed to be significantly lower than the actual total number of clinical cases. The vast majority of infections with the virus result from infected ticks, which often remain firmly attached to the skin for days. On rare occasions, infection can result from consumption of unpasteurized milk from infect goats, sheep or cows. People come in contact with the ticks during outdoor activities in forested areas up to an altitude of about 2000 meters. There is no direct person-to-person transmission. Symptoms: Most infections remain asymptomatic. In case of clinical illness, the incubation period for tick-borne encephalitis lasts 2–28 days (most commonly 7–14 days) and is followed by 1–8 days of general cold symptoms, such as fatigue, headache and general malaise, usually combined with fever of ≥38 °C.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

**Overview of Tick-borne Encephalitis:** Tick-borne encephalitis (TBE) is an infection of the nervous system caused by viruses that occur in the eastern, central, northern and, most importantly, southeastern regions of European countries, and in northern China, Mongolia, and the Russian Federation. Tick-borne encephalitis is a member of the Flaviviridae family. It affects approximately 10,000–12,000 people annually, although this number is believed to be significantly underestimated compared to the total number of infections. Many of the diseases from the tick-borne flaviviruses emerge from ticks that have already transmitted the disease, often without the person realizing it for several days. In other cases, the disease can emerge from the consumption of raw milk that has been contaminated by ticks that have transmitted the disease. People often encounter ticks during outdoor activities in forested areas, up to altitudes of 2000 meters. There is no human-to-human transmission.

**Symptoms:** Many infections occur without symptoms. In the typical course of the disease, the incubation period for tick-borne encephalitis is usually 2-28 days (often 7-14 days), followed by 1-8 days of characteristic symptoms, such as fever, headache, and stiff neck, which may be accompanied by a temperature ≥ 38°C.

translations.source: Lẹ́yìn àìsí àmì àìsàn láàrin àwọn ọjọ́ 1-20 bí 15% àwọn aláàárẹ̀ ń ní ìrírí ìpele kejì àìsàn náà tí ó ń ní àbùdá ibà tí ó ju 40° C àti àwọn àpẹẹrẹ ìdásí ètò ibùdó ara, bí yínrùnyínrùn (b.a. ibà, ẹ̀fọ́rí, àti ọrùn wíwàkì), ọpọlọ wíwú (b. a. òòyì ojú, ìrújú, àti ìdíwọ́ ọpọlọ), ìrora ọ̀pá ẹ̀yìn tàbí ìrora olóbìrípo. Ọpọlọ wíwú tí ń dàgbà lásìkò ìpele kejì lè fa rọpárọsẹ̀, àpá ayérayé tàbí ikú. Bí 1% àwọn ọ̀ràn pẹ̀lú àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ètò ara lè kú; wọn tí ròyìn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìwọ̀n ikú láti ilẹ̀ Russia, eyit tí ó lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú oríṣìí ẹ̀yà fáírọ́ọ̀sì mìíràn. Ọwọ́ líle àìsàn náà máa ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ọjọ́ orí aláàárẹ̀, ṣùgbọ́n wọ́n ti ròyìn àwọn ikú káàkiri gbogbo àwọn ọ̀wọ́ ọjọ́ orí. Kò sí ìtọ́jú pàtàkì fún ọpọlọ wíwú tí eégbọn ń ṣokùnfà. Ìdáàbòbò: Àwọn ènìyàn lè dáàbò bo ara wọn láti ọwọ́ eégbọn nípa wíwọ aṣọ tí ó tọ́, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ṣòkòtò gígùn àti bàtà amusẹ̀tán, nígbà tí wọ́n bá ń ṣeré tàbí pàgọ́ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tàbí agbègbè tí wọn léwu. Gbogbo ara gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ mímójútó lójoojúmọ́ kí àwọn eégbọn sì jẹ́ mímú kúrò lójú ọjọ́. Jíjẹ àwọn ẹ̀yà ara ẹran tí wọn kò sè gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ yíyàgò fún ní àwọn àgbègbè náà. Àjẹsára ń pèsè ìdáàbòbò tí ó múnádóko jùlọ. Lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ báyìí, àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tí wọ́n gbajúmọ̀ 4 tí wọ́n péye ló wà:
translations.reference: After an asymptomatic interval of 1–20 days up to 15% of patients experience a second phase of the disease characterized by fever frequently exceeding 40 °C and signs of central nervous system involvement, such as meningitis (e.g. fever, headache, and a stiff neck), encephalitis (e.g., drowsiness, confusion, and sensory disturbances), myelitis or radiculitis. Encephalitis developing during this second phase may result in paralysis, permanent sequelae or death. About 1% of cases with neurological pathologies may die; higher fatality rates have been reported from the Russian federation, which may be related to a different virus subtype. Severity of illness increases with age of the patient, but fatalities have been reported from all age groups. There is no specific treatment for tick-borne encephalitis. Prevention: People can protect themselves from ticks by wearing appropriate clothing, including long trousers and closed footwear, when hiking or camping in countries or areas at risk. The whole body should be inspected daily and attached ticks removed as soon as possible. The consumption of unpasteurized dairy products should also be avoided in those areas. Immunization offers the most effective protection. Currently, there are 4 widely used vaccines of assured quality:

translations.source: FSME-Imum àti Encepur, tí wọ́n ṣe ní Australia àti Germany, tí wọ́n sì dá lórí ẹ̀yà fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ti ilẹ̀ Yúróòpù; àti TBE- Moscow àti EnceVir, tí wọ́n ṣe ní ilẹ̀ Russia tí ó sì dá lórí àwọn ẹ̀yà jìnnà réré Ìlà oòrùn. Àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára 4 ni wọ́n gbà pé wọ́n ní ààbò tí wọn sì múnádóko. Ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn àìsàn náà tí wọ́pọ̀, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé dábàá pé kí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára wà fún gbogbo ènìyàn, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ọmọdé. Eégbọn tún ń tan Borreliosis (àìsàn Lyme), tí ó jẹ́ àrùn bakitéríà. Abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára TBE kò kájú gbígbógun ti àìsàn yìí, èyí tí ó ṣe é wò pẹ̀lú àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò.
translations.reference: FSME-Immun and Encepur, manufactured in Austria and Germany respectively, and based on European strains of the virus; and TBE-Moscow and EnceVir, manufactured in the Russian Federation and based on Far-Eastern strains. The 4 vaccines are considered to be safe and effective. In areas where the disease is highly endemic, WHO recommends that vaccination be offered to all age groups, including children. Ticks also transmit Borreliosis (Lyme disease), which is a bacterial infection. TBE vaccination is not effective against this disease, which however is treatable with antimicrobials.

translations.source: Ewé tábà Ìsọnísókí: Nikotíìnì tí ó wà nínú ewé tábà máa ń fà ìkúndùn púpọ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìlò ewé tábà jẹ́ ewu ńlá pàtàkì fún àwọn àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ọkàn àti òpónà ìmí, ó lé ní 20 onírúurú ẹ̀yà àti oríṣìí ẹ̀yà jẹjẹrẹ, àti àwọn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìṣòro ìlera tí wọ́n gbèrò mìíràn. Ní ọdọọdún, ó lé ní mílíọ̀nù 8 ènìyàn ń kú láti ara lílo ewé tábà. Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ikú tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìlò ewé tábà ní wọ́n ń wáyé ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè, tí wọ́n jẹ́ àfojúsùn ìkọlù ipa àti ọjà àwọn ilé iṣẹ́ tábà. Tábà tún lè léwu fún àwọn tí wọn kì í fa sìgá. Ìsíjúsí èéfín àmukù náà tún ti fa ọ̀ràn ní àwọn àbájáde ìlera, tí ó ń fa mílíọ̀nù 1.2 àwọn ikú lọ́dọọdún. Ó tó ìdajì gbogbo àwọn ọmọdé ni wọ́n ń mí àmísínú afẹ́fẹ́ tí èéfín tábà ti bàjẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọmọdé 65 000 ń kú lọ́dọọdún nítorí àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú èèfín àmukù. Fífa sìgá lásìkò ìlóyún lè yọrí sí àwọn ìṣòro ìlera ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ fún àwọn ìkókó. Àwọn èròjà tábà gbígbóná (HTPs) ní tábà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n máa ń síjú àwọn àwọn aṣàmúlò sí ìbújáde tí ó léwu, púpọ̀ àwọn èyí tí wọ́n ń fa jẹjẹrẹ tí wọ́n sì léwu fún ìlera. Àwọn ètò àgbékalẹ̀ nikotíìnì oníná (ENDS) àti àwọn ètò àgbékalẹ̀ tí kì í nikotíìnì oníná (ENNDS), tí àwọn ènìyàn mọ̀ sí sìgá oníná, kò ní tábà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ní tàbí kí ó má ní nikotíìnì, ṣùgbọ́n wọ́n léwu fún ìlera bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó dájú pé wọn kò ní ààbò.
translations.reference: Tobacco Overview: Nicotine contained in tobacco is highly addictive and tobacco use is a major risk factor for cardiovascular and respiratory diseases, over 20 different types or subtypes of cancer, and many other debilitating health conditions. Every year, more than 8 million people die from tobacco use. Most tobacco-related deaths occur in low- and middle-income countries, which are often targets of intensive tobacco industry interference and marketing. Tobacco can also be deadly for non-smokers. Second-hand smoke exposure has also been implicated in adverse health outcomes, causing 1.2 million deaths annually. Nearly half of all children breathe air polluted by tobacco smoke and 65 000 children die each year due to illnesses related to second-hand smoke. Smoking while pregnant can lead to several life-long health conditions for babies. Heated tobacco products (HTPs) contain tobacco and expose users to toxic emissions, many of which cause cancer and are harmful to health. Electronic nicotine delivery systems (ENDS) and electronic non-nicotine delivery systems (ENNDS), commonly known as e-cigarettes, do not contain tobacco and may or may not contain nicotine, but are harmful to health and undoubtedly unsafe.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Tobacco Warning: Nicotine contained in tobacco causes significant addiction, and the use of tobacco is a major risk factor for heart disease and respiratory illnesses, with 20 different types and varieties, and various other health problems. Annually, 8 million people die from tobacco use. Many of the deaths associated with tobacco use occur in low-income and developing countries, which are vulnerable to the influence of advertising and the marketing of tobacco companies. Tobacco can also be harmful to those who do not smoke. Secondhand smoke also causes problems in healthcare facilities, leading to 1.2 million deaths per year. It affects all children, as they inhale air polluted by tobacco smoke, and 65,000 children die each year due to illnesses caused by secondhand smoke. Quitting smoking during pregnancy can lead to long-term health problems for the child. Heated tobacco products (HTPs) in tobacco also release harmful substances into the environment, many of which are carcinogenic and harmful to health. Nicotine-containing electronic delivery systems (ENDS) and non-nicotine-containing electronic delivery systems (ENNDS), which people know as e-cigarettes, do not contain tobacco, but they may or may not contain nicotine, but they are harmful to health and it is important that they are not allowed.**



I've aimed for accuracy and clarity while maintaining the tone of a public health warning.

translations.source: Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, ó ti yá jù láti pèsè ìdáhùn pípé lórí ipa ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ ìlò HTPs àti/tàbí àwọn sìgá oníná. Ipa: Ìṣirò ènìyàn bílíọ́nù 1.3 káàkiri àgbáyé ń ṣàmúlò àwọn èròjà tábà, 80% àwọn tí wọ́n wà ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Ìlò tábà ń nípa lórí òsì nípa dídarí owónàá ilé kúrò lára àwọn ìlò abẹ́lé, bí oúnjẹ àti ilégbèé, sórí tábà. Ìwà ìnáwó yìí ṣòro láti yanjú nítorí pé tábà ní àbùdá àfẹ́sódì. Ó tún máa ń fa ikú àìtọ́jọ́ àti ààbọ̀ ara fún àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn wà ní ọjọ́ orí iṣẹ́ nínú ilé èyí tí ó ń ṣokùnfà àdínkù èrè àti iyeó ìtọ́jú ìlera. Ní àfikún sí ìpa ìparun tábà lórí ìlera, àpapọ̀ àléébù sìgá mímu lórí ọrọ̀ ajé (láti ara àwọn ìnáwó ìlera àti àwọn àdánù lápapọ̀) ní wọ́n ṣírò pé ó tó bí tírílíọ́nù US$ 1.4 lọ́dún, èyí tí ó jẹ́ àpapọ̀ 1.8% iye ọjà abẹ́lé gbogbo àgbáyé lọ́dọọdún (GDP). Ó tó ìdá 40% gbogbo àwọn iyeó yìí ni wọ́n wáyé ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ń gòkè àgbà, èyí tí ó ń tọ́ka sí àwọn àjàgà pàtàkì tí àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè wọ̀nyí ń kojú. Wọ́n máa ń ṣàmúlò àwọn owó orí tábà lápá kan láti gbógun ti ipa yìí bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n jẹ́ ọ̀nà tí ó dára jùlọ láti dáwọ́ ìlò tábà dúró, pàápàá jùlọ láàrin àwọn ọ̀dọ́ àti àwọn ènìyàn ní àwùjọ elérè pínnísín. Àlékún owó orí tí ó ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àwọn iyeó tábà pẹ̀lú àdínkù 10% ìlò tábà láti ọwọ́ bí 4% ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi àti 5% ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Ìwọ̀n àjálù ènìyàn àti ọrọ̀ ajé yìí yanilẹ́nu, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún ṣe é dènà.
translations.reference: However, it is too early to provide a clear answer on the long-term impact of HTPs and/or e-cigarette use. Impact: An estimated 1.3 billion people worldwide use tobacco products, 80% of whom are in low- and middle-income countries. Tobacco use contributes to poverty by diverting household spending from basic needs, such as food and shelter, to tobacco. This spending behaviour is difficult to curb because tobacco is so addictive. It also causes premature death and disability of productive age adults in households thus leading to reduced household income and increased healthcare costs. In addition to the detrimental impact of tobacco on health, the total economic cost of smoking (from health expenditures and productivity losses together) are estimated to be around US$ 1.4 trillion per year, equivalent in magnitude to 1.8% of the world's annual gross domestic product (GDP). Almost 40% of this cost occurred in developing countries, highlighting the substantial burden these countries suffer. Tobacco taxes are applied in part to combat this effect and are seen as the most cost-effective way of curbing tobacco use, particularly among youth and low-income populations. A tax increase that increases tobacco prices by 10% decreases tobacco consumption by about 4% in high-income countries and about 5% in low- and middle-income countries. WHO response: The scale of this human and economic tragedy is shocking, but it’s also preventable.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Despite this, it has been difficult to establish a complete understanding of the impact of prolonged tobacco use and/or nicotine addiction. Impact: Approximately 1.3 billion people worldwide are exposed to tobacco products, 80% of whom live in low- and middle-income countries. Tobacco use has an impact on health by diverting resources from essential household expenses, such as food and housing, to tobacco. This addiction is difficult to overcome because tobacco is highly addictive. It also leads to premature death and various illnesses for adults who are working at home, which further exacerbates poverty and healthcare costs. In addition to the health impact of tobacco, the total economic burden of smoking (from healthcare costs and lost productivity) is estimated to be US$ 1.4 trillion annually, which is 1.8% of the total global household expenditure (GDP). Over 40% of these costs are incurred in countries that experience high mortality rates, indicating the significant challenges these countries face. They often resort to increasing tobacco taxes as a way to mitigate this impact, and this is the most effective way to curb tobacco use, especially among young people and vulnerable populations. Specific excise taxes that increase the cost of tobacco by 10% have been shown to reduce tobacco use by as little as 4% in high-income countries and 5% in low- and middle-income countries. World Health Organization: The human and economic toll of this is alarming, but it is not insurmountable."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Cultural Context:** I've tried to maintain the tone and flow of the original Yoruba while ensuring clarity in English.
*   **Terminology:** I've used standard English terminology for health and economic concepts.
*   **Accuracy:** I've aimed for the most accurate translation possible, considering the nuances of the language.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Big Tobacco – pẹ̀lú gbogbo àwọn ilé iṣẹ́ olùpèsè ọjà tábà – ń gbìyànjú láti rí i pé àwọn ewu tí ó wà nínú ọjà wọn farasin, ṣùgbọ́n wọ́n ń gbógun ti èyí padà: Ní 2003, àwọn ìlú tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé fẹnukò láti ṣàmúlò Àpérò Ìhun Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé lórí Àkóso Tábà (WHO FCTC), àdéhùn ìlera àwùjọ kan ṣoṣo lábẹ́ ìdarí Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé). Nínú àṣẹ láti 2005, ó ní Ẹgbẹ́ 181 báyìí. Láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣe àmúlò WHO FCTC, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ MPOWER L, ètò àwọn òdiwọ̀n àti orísun ìmọ̀, ọ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan èyí tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ó kéré jù ìpèsè WHO FCTC kan. MPOWER kọ́ ipa àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣàmúlò àwọn òdiwọ̀n 6 láti ṣe àdínkù ìbéèrè fún àwọn ọjà tábà: ṣe àkóso ìlò tábà àti ìdènà àwọn ìlànà, dáàbò bo àwọn ènìyàn lọ́wọ́ ìlò tábà, pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti dáwọ́ ìlò tábà dúró, ṣe ìkìlọ̀ nípa àwọn ewu tábà, fi òfin de ìpolówó, ìgbéga àti ṣíṣe onígbọ̀wọ́ tábà àti ṣíṣe àlékún owó orí tábà.
translations.reference: Big Tobacco — along with all manufacturers of tobacco products — is fighting to ensure the dangers of their products are concealed, but we are fighting back: In 2003, WHO Member States unanimously adopted the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC), the only public health treaty under the auspices of WHO. In force since 2005, it has currently 181 Parties. To help countries implement the WHO FCTC, WHO introduced MPOWER, a package of technical measures and resources, each of which corresponds to at least one provision of the WHO FCTC. MPOWER builds the capacity of countries to implement 6 measures to reduce the demand for tobacco products: monitor tobacco use and prevention policies, protect people from tobacco use, offer help to quit tobacco use, warn about the dangers of tobacco, enforce bans on tobacco advertising, promotion and sponsorship and raise taxes on tobacco.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

Big Tobacco – along with all the companies that sell tobacco products – are eager to portray the dangers within their market as exaggerated, but they are fighting against this: In 2003, cities that were members of the World Health Organization (WHO) agreed to ratify the Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (WHO FCTC), a unique public health treaty under the leadership of the World Health Organization. The agreement, as of 2005, has 181 members. To help countries ratify the WHO FCTC, the World Health Organization launched MPOWER, a package of actions and resources, each of which addresses a key element of the WHO FCTC. MPOWER highlights the roles of countries in ratifying six measures to increase demand for tobacco products: controlling tobacco use and restricting advertising, protecting people from tobacco use, providing assistance to quit tobacco use, informing about the dangers of tobacco, taxing tobacco, reducing affordability and increasing the price of tobacco and implementing tobacco retail restrictions.

translations.source: Tirakómà Ìsọnísókí: Tirakómà jẹ́ àìsàn ojú arit asíwájú àìsàn tí ó ń fa ìfọ́jú káàkiri àgbáyé. Bakitéríà inú hóró ara kan tí orúkọ rẹ̀ ń jẹ́ Chlamydia trachomatis ni ó ń fà á. Àrùn náà máa ń ràn láti ara ènìyàn-sí-ènìyàn nípa ìkóràn tààrà tàbí àlùmọ̀kọ́rọ́yí ipin ojú tàbí ikun imú ẹni tí ó ti faragbá; lára ìkóràn àlùmọ̀kọ́rọ́yí ni ìgbésára àwọn ẹ̀yà esinsin kan. Àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n wà ní ọjọ́ orí jéléósinmi ní àká ńlá àrùn náà. Àwọn àwòṣe fi hàn pé ènìyàn yóò nílò àrùn tí ó lé ní 150 ní ayé kan kí ó tó ní àwọn ìṣòro ìfọ́jú tirakómà. Tirakómà jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó wọ́pọ̀ ní àárín àwọn ènìyàn kan tí wọ́n tòṣì jùlọ lágbàáyé, tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè abúlé àti ìgbèríko tí wọn kò sì ní àǹfààní tí ó tó sí omi, ìmọ́tótó àti ìtọ́jú ìlera. Áfríkà ni ẹ̀yà tí ó faragbá jùlọ, ṣùgbọ́n òun náà ni ó ní àwọn akitiyan àkóso tí ó fọ́n ká jùlọ. Àìsàn náà tún wà ní Àárín gbùngbùn àti Gúúsù Amẹ́ríkà, Ésíà, Australia, àti Àárín gbùngbùn Ìlà oòrùn. Ní 2 Oṣù Kìíní 2020, orílẹ̀ èdè 13 jábọ̀ àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn ìfòpinsí.
translations.reference: Trachoma Overview: Trachoma is a disease of the eye and the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide. It is caused by an obligate intracellular bacterium called Chlamydia trachomatis. Infection is transmitted from person to person by direct or indirect transfer of ocular and nasal discharges of infected people; indirect transfer includes carriage on the body of species of flies. Preschool-age children harbour the principal reservoir of infection. Models suggest that an individual requires more than 150 lifetime infections to develop the blinding complications of trachoma. Trachoma is endemic in some of the world’s poorest populations, who live in rural and remote areas and have highly inadequate access to water, sanitation and healthcare. Africa is the most affected continent, but it also has the most widespread control efforts. The disease is also found in Central and South America, Asia, Australia, and the Middle East. As of 2 January 2020, 13 countries had reported achieving elimination goals.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Chlamydia Infection Report:** Chlamydia is a sexually transmitted infection (STI) that is a leading cause of preventable blindness worldwide. It is caused by a bacterium called *Chlamydia trachomatis*. The infection is usually spread from person to person through sexual contact, including unprotected vaginal, anal, or oral sex; among sexually transmitted infections, it is the most common bacterial infection. Children are also at risk of the disease. Studies show that a person needs to have 150 infections in a lifetime to experience chlamydia eye complications. Chlamydia is a common infection among young people worldwide, particularly those living in impoverished communities with limited access to water, sanitation, and healthcare. Africa is the most affected region, but it also has the most control measures. The disease is also present in Latin America and the Caribbean, North and South America, Australia, and the Middle East. As of June 2, 2020, 13 countries reported the success of prevention interventions.

translations.source: Ní 2019 nìkan, àwọn ènìyàn 92, 622 gba itojut iṣẹ́ abẹ fún tirakómà líle bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni mílíọ̀nù 95.2 ènìyàn gba ìtọ́jú pẹ̀lú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò. Pẹ̀lú gbogbo àṣeyọrí wọ̀nyí, àìsàn náà sì jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ tí ó lè, pẹ̀lú ìṣirò àdánù bílíọ́nù US$8 bílíọ́nù lọ́dún nínú ìpèsè nítorí ìfọ́jú àti àléébù ojú. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Àrùn náà ni àṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn àyípadà ìrẹ̀dòdò nínú àwo inú ojú tí a mọ̀ sí "tirakómà tí ń ṣiṣẹ́". Ètò àjẹsára ẹ̀dá ènìyàn kan lè ṣe àmúkúrò ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àrùn kan ṣoṣo, ṣùgbọ́n ní àwọn àwùjọ tí àìsàn náà pọ̀ sí, àtúnkó kòkòrò naat máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ dáadáa. Lẹ́yìn ọ̀pọ̀ ọdún àṣetúnṣe àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìsàn tirakómà, inú awọ ẹyinjú lè ní àwọn àpá ńlá (àpá tirakómà awọ ojú) tí yóò sì sẹ́po sínú èyí tí yóò mú kí irun ìpéǹpéjú máa nu ẹyinjú (tirakómà tirikíásì), èyí tí yóò máa fa ìrora lemọ́lemọ́ àti àìlefarada ìmọ́lẹ̀; èyí àti àyípadà ẹyinjú mìíràn lè fa àpá nínú ẹyinjú. Bí ó bá wà láìsí ìtọ́jú, ìṣòro yìí yóò yọrí sí ìkójọ àwọn ìwò bàlùbàlù tí kò ṣe é yí padà, èyí tí yóò yọrí sí àléébù ojú tàbí ìfọ́jú. Ọjọ́ orí tí èyí yóò ṣẹlẹ̀ dá lórí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìdí tí ó fi mọ́ agbára ìkóràn abẹ́lé. Ní àwọn àwùjọ tí ó wọ́pọ̀ sí gan-an, ó lè ṣẹlẹ̀ ní ọmọdé, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìṣòro ojú láàrin ọdún 30 sí 40 wọ́pọ̀ dáadáa. Àwọn obìnrin máa ń fojú ní ìlọ́po mẹ́rin bí àwọn ọkùnrin, bóyá nítorí ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àwọn àwọn ọmọ tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn àwùjọ olósì àti ìgbèríko Áfríkà, Ésíà, Australia, Àárín gbùngbùn Ìlà oòrùn, Àárín gbùngbùn àti Gúúsù Amẹ́ríkà àti àwọn Erékùṣù Pàsífíìkì wà nínú ewu.
translations.reference: In 2019 alone, 92 622 people received surgical treatment for advanced trachoma and 95.2 million were treated with antibiotics. Despite these successes, the disease remains a serious public health concern, with an estimated US$ 8 billion annual loss in productivity due to blindness and visual impairment. Symptoms: Infection is associated with inflammatory changes in the conjunctiva known as “active trachoma”. An individual’s immune system can clear a single episode of infection, but in endemic communities, re-acquisition of the organism occurs frequently. After years of repeated episodes of active trachoma, the inside of the eyelid can become so severely scarred (trachomatous conjunctival scarring) that it turns inwards and causes the eyelashes to rub against the eyeball (trachomatous trichiasis), resulting in constant pain and light intolerance; this and other alterations of the eye can lead to scarring of the cornea. Left untreated, this condition leads to the formation of irreversible opacities, with resulting visual impairment or blindness. The age at which this occurs depends on several factors including local transmission intensity. In very highly endemic communities, it can occur in childhood, though onset of visual impairment between the ages of 30 and 40 years is more typical. Women are blinded up to four times as often as men, probably due to more frequent contact with infected children. Individuals living in the poorest and most remote communities of Africa, Asia, Australia, the Middle East, Central and South America and the Pacific Islands are at risk.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

In 2019 alone, 92,622 people received cataract surgery, and 95.2 million people received treatment with corrective glasses. Despite all this success, the disease remains a major public health problem, with an estimated annual cost of US$8 billion due to vision impairment and blindness.

**Disease Characteristics:** The disease is associated with changes in the density of the inner eye fluid, known as "working cataract." A single person's genetic history can prevent a single occurrence of the disease, but in populations where the disease is prevalent, cataract surgery is common. After many years of cataract disease events, significant changes (cataract color changes) can develop in the lens, which can lead to cloudy vision (traumatic cataract), which can cause blurred vision and light sensitivity; this and other lens changes can cause changes in the lens. If left untreated, this condition can lead to a variety of visual impairments that do not resolve on their own, leading to blindness or vision loss. This often occurs over a period of several years, driven by multiple factors that increase the risk of cataracts. In very large populations, it can occur in children, with the onset of eye disease typically between the ages of 30 and 40. Women are more likely to experience vision impairment than men, possibly due to prolonged contact with their children. People living in high-poverty and indigenous communities in Africa, Asia, Australia, the Middle East and North Africa, Central and South America, and the Pacific Islands are at risk.

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú: Tirakómà ṣe é dènà nípa mímú àdínkù bá àwọn ewu àyíká. Lára àwọn wọ̀nyí ni àìtó ìmọ́tótó, àwọn ilé tó kún fún ọ̀pọ̀ èrò, àìní àǹfààní tó tó sí omi àti àìní àǹfààní tó tó sí àti ìlò ìmọ́tótó. Àwọn àrùn sáábà máa ń bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ọjọ́ orí pínnísín bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n kì í dàgbà dáadáa àti pé àsìkò wọn kéré pẹ̀lú ọjọ́ orí tí ó ń lọ síwájú. Àwọn àrùn sáábà máa ń jẹ́ kíkó nípasẹ̀ ẹbí àti ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àìsàn náà. Àṣetúnṣe àwọn àrùn náà máa ń fa àwọn àmì àìsàn líle àrùn náà. Wọ́n lè fòpin sí tirakómà gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò ọgbọ́n SAFE, tí ó ní i: Iṣẹ́ abẹ láti ṣe ìtọ́jú tirakómà tirikíásì, ìpele ìfọ́jú àìsàn náà.; Òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò láti pa àrùn, pàápàá jùlọ lílo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò azithromycin, èyí tí olùpèsè rẹ̀ fi tọrẹ fún àwọn ètò orílẹ̀ èdè nípasẹ̀ Ètò Tirakómà ti Àgbáyé.; Ìmọ́tótó ojú; àti Ìdàgbàsókè àyíká, pàápàá jùlọ níní àǹfààní sí omi àti ìmọ́tótó.
translations.reference: Treatment: Trachoma can be prevented by limiting environmental risk factors. These include inadequate hygiene, crowded households, inadequate access to water and inadequate access to and use of sanitation. Infections usually begin at an early age and grow less frequent and shorter in duration with increasing age. Infections are most often acquired through family and contact with others with active disease. Repeated infections cause the most serious symptoms of the disease. Trachoma can be eliminated as a public health problem using the SAFE strategy, comprising: Surgery to treat trachomatous trichiasis, the blinding stage of the disease; Antibiotics to clear infection, particularly mass drug administration of the antibiotic azithromycin, which is donated by the manufacturer to national programmes through the International Trachoma Initiative; Facial cleanliness; and Environmental improvement, particularly better access to water and sanitation.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Topic:** Strategies to combat malaria in the face of persistent challenges. Among these are diagnostic errors, overcrowded health facilities, lack of access to water, lack of access to sanitation, and the use of inaccurate diagnostics. Malaria cases often begin on Mondays and do not worsen significantly, with their duration being short, lasting only a few days as the week progresses. Malaria is often transmitted through families and community spread alongside the illness. Treatment of these illnesses can cause symptoms similar to those of the illness. They can escalate into severe malaria, becoming a public health crisis through the implementation of the SAFE expert protocol, which includes: Early detection to diagnose malaria quickly and assess the severity of the illness; Antimalarial drugs to treat the disease, especially the use of multiple antimalarial drugs like azithromycin, which its manufacturer has approved for national programs through the Global Malaria Initiative; Eye examination; and Comprehensive diagnostics, especially in areas with access to water and diagnostics.

translations.source: Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé lo ọgbọ́n SAFE ní 1993 bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n tẹ̀síwájú nínú ètò wọn láti pèsè ìdarí àti àkóso àwọn akitiyan àgbáyé láti fòpin sí tirakómà gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ.
translations.reference: WHO adopted the SAFE strategy in 1993 and continues in its mandate to provide leadership and coordination of international efforts to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

"The World Health Assembly adopted SAFE in 1993 and has continued to develop its program to prevent and control global activities related to public health emergencies."



**Explanation of some key terms and choices:**

*   **Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé:** World Health Assembly
*   **ọgbọ́n SAFE:** adopted SAFE (SAFE is likely an acronym, and I've kept it as is, assuming it's a known program)
*   **ìdarí àti àkóso:** prevention and control
*   **akitiyan àgbáyé:** global activities
*   **fòpin sí tirakómà gẹ́gẹ́:** related to public health emergencies
*   **ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ:** public health emergencies

translations.source: Òògùn ìbílẹ̀, àfikún àti àṣepọ̀. Ìsọnísókí: Òògùn ìbílẹ̀ Òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ní ìtàn tí ó ti pẹ̀. Ó jẹ́ àpapọ̀ gbogbo ìmọ̀, ìmọ̀-ọ́nṣe, àti àwọn iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n da lórí àwọn ìlànà, ìgbàgbọ́, àti ìrírí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú onírúurú àṣà, yálà ó ṣe é ṣàlàyé tàbí bẹ́ẹ̀ kọ́, tí wọ́n ń lò fún àkóso ìlera àti fún ìdènà, àyẹ̀wò, ìlọsíwájú tàbí ìtọ́jú ìlera àgọ́ ara tàbí ìlera ọpọlọ. Òògùn àfikún Ọ̀rọ̀ ìperí "òògùn àfikún" tàbí "arọ́pò òògùn" ni àwọn onírúurú ètò iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ikera tí wọn kì í ṣe ara àṣà ti orílẹ̀ èdè yẹn tàbí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọn kò sè àmúlò wọn tán sínú ètò ìtọ́jú ìlera tí ó gbòde. Wọ́n máa ń lò wọ́n ní ìpààrọ̀ pẹ̀lú òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè kan. Àwọn òògùn egbòogi Lára àwọn òògùn egbòogi ni àgbo, èròjà egbòogi, àwọn ìpèsè egbòogi àti ọjà egbòogi tí wọ́n ti parí iṣẹ́ sí lára, tí ó ní àwọn èròjà ara àwọn ohun ọ̀gbìn, tàbí àwọn èròjà ohun ọ̀gbìn mìíràn, tàbí àwọn àpapọ̀. Ipa: Ìlànà Òògùn Ìbílẹ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 2014-2023 ni wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ tí wọ́n sì fi lólẹ̀ ní ìfèsì sí ọ̀nà àbáyọ Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ (WHA62. 13).
translations.reference: Traditional, Complementary and Integrative Medicine Overview: Traditional medicine Traditional medicine has a long history. It is the sum total of the knowledge, skill, and practices based on the theories, beliefs, and experiences indigenous to different cultures, whether explicable or not, used in the maintenance of health as well as in the prevention, diagnosis, improvement or treatment of physical and mental illness. Complementary medicine The terms “complementary medicine” or “alternative medicine” refer to a broad set of health care practices that are not part of that country’s own tradition or conventional medicine and are not fully integrated into the dominant health-care system. They are used interchangeably with traditional medicine in some countries. Herbal medicines Herbal medicines include herbs, herbal materials, herbal preparations and finished herbal products, that contain as active ingredients parts of plants, or other plant materials, or combinations. Impact: The WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014–2023 was developed and launched in response to the World Health Assembly resolution on traditional medicine (WHA62.13).
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Traditional Medicine, Complementary and Integrative.**

**Introduction:** Traditional Medicine

Traditional medicine has a long history. It is a synthesis of all knowledge, medical knowledge, and practices based on the customs, beliefs, and experiences that have existed for a long time, and it explains how they are used for the management of illness and for prevention, diagnosis, promotion or rehabilitation of individual or community health.

**Complementary Medicine**

The term "complementary medicine" or "alternative medicine" refers to various health care services that are not part of the traditional medical practices of a given country or traditional medicine, and they are not fully integrated into the national health care system. They are often used in conjunction with traditional medicine in some countries.

**Herbal Medicines**

Among the herbal medicines are herbs, herbal preparations, herbal powders and herbal markets that have been processed, which contain the active ingredients of medicinal plants, or other medicinal plant ingredients, or combinations thereof.

**Impact:**

The World Health Organization's Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014-2023 was launched and implemented in line with the global approach to traditional medicine (WHA62. 13).

translations.source: Ìlànà náà fojúsun ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìlànà ìjígìrì àti àmúlò ètò ìṣe tí yóò ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ipa tí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ ń kó nínú mímú kí àwọn ènìyàn wà ní àlàáfíà. Wíwá ojútùú sí àwọn ìṣòro, fífèsì sí àìní tí àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ṣe ìdámọ̀ àti ìtẹ̀síwájú lórí iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n ṣe lábẹ́ Ìlànà Òògùn abẹ́lé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: 2002-2005, ìlànà àmúdójú-ìwọ̀n fún àsìkò 2014-2023 fi àsìkò púpọ̀ sílẹ̀ ju aṣáájú rẹ̀ lọ fún mímú fífi ọwọ́ pàtàkì mú àwọn iṣẹ́ àti ètò ìlera, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn èròjà òògùn ìbílẹ̀ àti àfikún, àwọn àṣà àti aṣàmúlò. Àwọn ìlànà èròǹgbà ni: Láti ṣẹ̀dá ọgbọ́n àgbékalẹ̀ fún àkóso iṣẹ́ T & CM nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìlànà orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n tọ́.; Láti ṣe ìrónilágbára fún ìdánilójú dídára, ààbò, ìlò tí ó tọ́ àti ìmúnádóko T & CM nípa fífi òfin de àwọn ọjà, àṣà àti aṣàmúlò.; Láti ṣe àgbéga ìpèsè ìlera káríayé nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn iṣẹ́ T & CM nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera àti ìtọ́jú ìlera ara ẹni. Èsì Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé: Ìròyìn àgbáyé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí òògùn ìbílẹ̀ àti àfikún 2019 ni wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ láti kojú àlàfo tí ó wà nínú ìṣeégbáralé, ìdára àti ojúlówó ìwífun-àlàyé láti ọwọ́ àwọn Orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ní agbègbè T & CM. Ìròyìn náà ṣàyẹ̀wò ìlọsíwájú káríayé T & CM láàrin àwọn ogún ọdún sẹ́yìn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó dá lórí àwọn ipa láti ọwọ́ àwọn Ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 179. Ó ń pèsè àlàyé gidi fún àwọn aṣòfin, alámọ̀já ìlera àti àwùjọ fún ṣíṣe àmúlò ipa ọjọ́ ọ̀la ìlera àti àlàáfíà T & CM.
translations.reference: The strategy aims to support Member States in developing proactive policies and implementing action plans that will strengthen the role traditional medicine plays in keeping populations healthy. Addressing the challenges, responding to the needs identified by Member States and building on the work done under the WHO traditional medicine strategy: 2002–2005, the updated strategy for the period 2014–2023 devotes more attention than its predecessor to prioritizing health services and systems, including traditional and complementary medicine products, practices and practitioners. The strategic objectives are: To build the knowledge base for active management of T&CM through appropriate national policies; To strengthen the quality assurance, safety, proper use and effectiveness of T&CM by regulating products, practices and practitioners; To promote universal health coverage by integrating T&CM services into health care service delivery and self-health care. WHO response: The WHO global report on traditional and complementary medicine 2019 was developed to address the gap in reliable, credible and official data from Member States in the area of T&CM. This report reviews global progress in T&CM over the past two decades and is based on contributions from 179 WHO Member States. It provides valuable information for policy-makers, health professionals and the public for capitalizing on the potential contribution of T&CM to health and well-being.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The strategy focuses on action and sustainability for countries to develop national drug policies and health system programs that will support the role of traditional medicine in maintaining people's health.  Addressing the urgency of challenges, and recognizing the importance of progress made by member countries on work done under the World Health Organization’s Drug Policy 2002-2005 strategy, the accelerated action plan for the period 2014-2023 has taken considerably more time than its predecessor for the implementation of prioritizing health programs and projects, including traditional medicine and complementary therapies, practices, and customs. The strategic approaches are: To create a knowledge base for the management of T&CM (Traditional and Complementary Medicine) work through national strategies that are appropriate; To strengthen the capacity for targeted regulation, quality, proper use, and safe T&CM practices by clarifying the rules of trade, practices, and customs; To strengthen global health security by implementing T&CM programs within the framework of emergency health response and self-care services. World Health Organization Report: The World Health Organization’s global report on traditional medicine and complementary therapies 2019 was launched to address the gaps in research, data and evidence-based information from member countries in the T&CM area. The report examines global T&CM progress over the past few years and is based on the impact from the 179 member countries of the World Health Organization. It provides real information for policymakers, health professionals and communities for the implementation of effective health and well-being T&CM actions."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **T&CM:** I've consistently used "Traditional and Complementary Medicine" as the expansion for T&CM, as it's the standard English term.
*   **Specificity:** I've tried to maintain the specific terminology used in the original Yoruba text, as it relates to health policy and traditional medicine.
*   **Flow:** I've aimed for a clear and readable English translation while staying true to the meaning of the original.

translations.source: Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara Ìsọnísókí: Ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara àti àwọn ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn. Ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara ń gba ẹ̀mí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ènìyàn là bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ń mú àwọn ojúṣe padà bọ̀ sípò níbi tí kò ti sí ìmúnádóko tí ó ṣe é fi wé. Ní ọdún 50, ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ti di àṣeyọrí àṣà káàkiri àgbáyé. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, àwọn ìyàtọ̀ ńlá wà láàrin àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè nínú àǹfààní wọn sí ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara tí ó bojúmu àti nínú ìpele ààbò, dídára, agbára ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n pèsè fún ìpààrọ̀ àti ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró, ìṣù ara àti ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn. Àwọn agbọn ẹ̀kọ́ ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara síwájú. Pàápàá jùlọ, àwọn àìní aláàárẹ̀ tí kò jẹ́ bíbá pàdé àti àìtó àwọn ẹ̀yà ara fún ìpààrọ̀ máa ń yọrí ìdánwò ìjíngbéyọ-ẹ̀yà ara fún ìpààrọ̀. Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn Àwọn hóró ara àti ìṣù ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀ lè gba àwọn ẹ̀mí là tàbí kí ó mú ìpadàbọ̀sípò bá àwọn ojúṣe pàtàkì. Fún àpẹẹrẹ iṣẹ́ abẹ ìpààrọ̀ ìṣù ẹyinjú lè mú ìpadàbọ̀sípò bá ìríran fún ìfọ́jú inú ẹyinjú.;
translations.reference: Transplantation Overview: Transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs Transplantation of human cells, tissues or organs saves many lives and restores essential functions where no alternatives of comparable effectiveness exist. In 50 years, transplantation has become a successful worldwide practice. However, there are large differences between countries in access to suitable transplantation and in the level of safety, quality, efficacy of donation and transplantation of human cells, tissues and organs. The ethical aspects of transplantation are at the forefront. In particular, the unmet patients’ needs and the shortage of transplants lead to the temptation of trafficking in human body components for transplantation. Human transplantation Human cells and tissues for transplantation can save lives or restore essential functions. For examplea corneal graft can restore sight in corneal blindness;
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**The Impact of Genetic Testing: Genetic Diseases, Health Risks, and Human Genetic Traits.** Genetic testing, health risks, and genetic traits affect a large number of people and can lead to negative consequences where there is no proper guidance. Over 50 years, genetic testing has become a common practice worldwide. However, significant differences exist between countries in their adoption of genetic testing, the level of awareness, and the genetic traits they test for in genetic testing and the testing of health risks and human genetic traits. Advanced genetic testing research continues. Most importantly, the lack of adequate counseling and the misinterpretation of genetic traits for testing lead to flawed multi-gene testing for genetic testing. **Human Genetic Testing** Human health risks and human genetic traits for testing can affect lives or lead to negative consequences for critical indicators. For example, a pregnancy genetic testing of eye injury can lead to negative consequences for vision in the injured eye.

translations.source: ìpààrọ̀ àwọn hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ ìpìlẹ̀ aláìgbó lè ṣe ìwòsàn fún àwọn àìsàn àtìgbà èwe wá tàbí àìsàn tí ènìyàn kó bí jẹjẹrẹ inú ẹ̀jẹ̀. Ẹ rí àlàyé sí i lórí Hóró Ẹ̀jẹ̀ Ìpìlẹ̀ Aláìgbó. ìpààrọ̀ òpó ìdarí ọkàn ènìyàn sáábà máa ń ní àwọn ipò ìpààrọ̀ tí wọ́n dára jùlọ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn aláìsàn kò nílò ìwòsàn àìmẹ́jẹ̀dì ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara pẹ̀lú ti ẹranko Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara pẹ̀lú ti ẹranko, ẹranko sí ènìyàn, ni àwọn hóró alààyè, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n wá láti ara ẹranko àti àwọn omira ẹ̀dá ènìyàn, hóró, ìṣù ara tàbí ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n kò sí lára àwọn ẹ̀dá alààyè wọ̀nyí tẹ́lẹ̀, àwọn èròjà ẹ̀dá mìíràn, ní agbára láti jẹ́ arọ́pò fún àwọn èròjà tí wọ́n ti ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn wá kí wọ́n sì dí àlàfo tí ó wà nínú èròjà ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀. Ìtọrẹ: Ìtọrẹ láti ara àwọn òkú gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ ṣíṣe ìdàgbàsókè fún dórí òpin agbára ìwòsàn wọn yàgò fún, nígbàkigbà tí ó bá ṣe é ṣe, àwọn ewu fún olùtọrẹ ààyè, gẹ́gẹ́ bí Ilanat Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé 3 ṣe sọ ọ́. Ìdámọ̀ àmúyẹ kan wà fún àwọn àwùjọ, àti alámọ̀já ìlera, àti kẹ́kọ̀ọ́ daradaeat sí i nípa ìtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìyẹn jẹ́ pàtàkì fún àṣeyọrí àwọn ètò ìtọrẹ òkú. Àmọ́ sá, pẹ̀lú gbogbo òòrèkóòrè ìlò àwọn èròjà tí àwọn òkú fi tọrẹ, àwọn ìtọrẹ láti ọwọ́ àwọn alààyè olùtọrẹ pọn dandan fún àwọn oríṣìí ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara kan tàbí láti pèsè fún àwọn èròjà kan tí wọ́n kò sí ní ànító láti ọwọ́ àwọn òkú olùtọrẹ láti ṣe alábàápàdé àwọn àìní aláìsàn. Nítorí náà ni wọ́n ṣe máa ń ṣe àmúlò ìtọrẹ alààyè bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ó ní àwọn ewu tí wọ́n lè má ṣe é dijú sí fún olùtọrẹ. Bí gbígba ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn fún ìpààrọ̀ láti ara òkú tàbí alààyè olùtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ tí ó tẹ̀lé lè ní ewu ẹ̀kọ́ àti ààbò fún agbàtọrẹ àti olùtọrẹ, àwọn àkóso tí wọ́n dúró dáadáa àti àbójútó tí ó múnádóko gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ gbígbé kalẹ̀ láti ọwọ́ àwọn aláṣẹ ìlera láti dáàbò bò wọ́n.
translations.reference: the transplantation of haematopoietic stem cells can cure congenital or acquired diseases including some leukaemias See further information on Haematopoeitic Stem Cells the transplantation of a human heart valve often constitutes the best replacement situation and recipient patients do not require long term anti-coagulation therapy. Xenotransplantation Xenotransplantation, animal to human, defined as living cells, tissues or organs of animal origin and human body fluids, cells, tissues or organs that have ex vivo with these living, xenogeneic materials, has the potential to constitute an alternative to material of human origin and bridge the shortfall in human material for transplantation. Donation: Donations from deceased persons should be developed to their maximum therapeutic potential avoiding, whenever possible, the inherent risks to live donors, as stated in WHO Guiding Principle 3. There is a recognized need for communities, and health professionals, to become better educated about donation and transplantation and that is the key to the success of deceased donation programmes. However, despite the frequent use of materials donated from deceased donors, the donations from living donors are necessary for some types of transplants or to compensate for the limited supply of material available from deceased donors in order to meet patient needs. Living donation is thus practised despite the fact that it involves risks for the donor that may not be negligible. As the procurement of human material for transplantation from deceased or living donors and the subsequent allogeneic transplantation may entail ethical and safety risks for both the recipient and the donor, strict controls and effective oversight should be carried out by the health authorities to protect them.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The properties of animal horns and bone marrow can be used to treat ailments and diseases that arise from the body or illnesses that people contract. Read on for details about Animal Horns and Bone Marrow. The number of bone marrow cells has the best positions for healing, so patients do not need long-term medical treatment. Animal and livestock bone marrow properties. Animal and livestock bone marrow, livestock to humans, are animal horns, tissue, or bone marrow that come from livestock and human genetic material, horns, tissue, or bone marrow that are not among these animal genetic materials. Other genetic products have the ability to be a substitute for genetic products that have human genetic material and to neutralize the substance found in human genetic products for bone marrow.

Note: Tissue from the dead must be processed for donation to ensure their healing power remains, as sometimes it can be, according to the World Health Organization’s International Guidelines for Organ Transplantation 3. There is a lack of awareness among communities and healthcare professionals, and a lack of education about tissue and bone marrow donation, which is crucial for the success of deceased organ donation programs. However, with all the various uses of products from the dead, tissue donations from animal donors are essential for various bone marrow properties or to supplement a product that is not available from deceased donors to address the underlying causes of illness. Therefore, they perform animal tissue donation even though it has risks that may not be addressed for the donor. As with human genetic tissue for bone marrow from a deceased donor or animal donor tissue donation that follows, it can have risks of infection and rejection for the recipient and donor, so proper and reliable controls must be in place from healthcare professionals to prevent them."

**Important Notes on Translation Challenges & Considerations:**

*   **Cultural Context:** The document deals with traditional practices and beliefs surrounding animal parts, bone marrow, and deceased tissue. Some concepts don't have direct English equivalents and require careful explanation to convey the intended meaning.
*   **Terminology:**  Medical and scientific terminology in Yoruba can be different from English. I've tried to use the most appropriate English terms, but some nuances might be lost.
*   **Sentence Structure:** Yoruba sentence structure can be quite different from English. I've restructured sentences for clarity and readability in English while attempting to remain faithful to the original meaning.
*   **Specificity:** Some phrases are quite general and could have multiple interpretations. I've made my best judgment based on the overall context.

translations.source: Àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà kàn ìtọ́jú tí ó dára jùlọ nípa fún àwọn olùtọrẹ àti agbàtọrẹ. Àbójútó tí ó hànde láti ọwọ́ àwọn aláṣẹ lórí ìtọrẹ àti àwọn iṣẹ́ ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara tún ṣe pàtàkì láti mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìgbẹ́kẹ̀lé àwùjọ nínú ètò náà. Ní àfikún, ìpinnu láti jẹ olùtọrẹ sáábà máa ń dá lórí ìlóye pé kíkó ipa nínú wíwà aowm èròjà ìpààrọ̀ lè jẹ́ àǹfààní fún àìní ìlera ẹbí olùtọrẹ ní ọjọ́ kan. Bí a bá gbáradì láti gba ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara nígbà tí s bá nílò ọ̀kan, nígbà náà ó yẹ ká ṣetán láti tọrẹ Àwọn Ẹ̀kọ́: Àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé lórí Ìpààrọ̀ Hóró Ẹ̀dá Ènìyàn, Ìṣù ara àti Ẹ̀yà ara. Ní 1987, Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé tí kò sí mọ́, lórí ìṣòwò fún èrè lórí ẹ̀yà ara ẹ̀dá ènìyàn, ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ ìgbáradì àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé kìíní lórí Ìpààrọ̀ Ẹ̀yà ara, èyí tí Àjọ náà fọwọ́ sí ní 1991 nínú onat àbáyọ WHA44. 25. Àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà wọ̀nyí ti nípa ńlá lórí àwọn òfin àti ìṣe alámọ̀já àti ilé aṣòfin káàkiri àgbáyé fún bí ogún ọdún. Lẹ́yìn ìjùmọ̀sọ̀rọ̀ tí ó gba ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún, Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé Ìkẹtalélọ́gọ́ta ṣàmúlò ọ̀nà àbáyọ WHA463. 22 ní 21 Oṣù karùn-ún 2010, tí eomt sì fọwọ́ sí ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n àwọn Ìlànà Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé àti ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn agbọn fún ìtẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àmúdára àwọn àṣà ìtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara. Ìkéde Istanbul Àwọn alámọ̀já ìtọrẹ àti ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara láti gbogbo àgbègbè, nípasẹ̀ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àjọ àti ibùdó wọn, ń fọwọ́ sí Ìkéde Istanbul lórí ìjíngbéyọ-ẹ̀yà ara àti ìrìn àjò lọ sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí ẹ̀yà ara ti rọjú tí wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ lábẹ́ ìdarí Àjọ Ìpààrọ̀ ẹ̀yà ara àti Àjọ Àgbáyé lórí ìmọ̀ kíndìnrín ní Oṣù Karùn-ún 2008.
translations.reference: The Guiding Principles mandate optimal care for donors and recipients. The transparent oversight of the health authorities over donation and transplantation activities is also essential to increase the trust of the public in the system. In addition, the decision to be a donor is often based on the understanding that a contribution to the availability of transplant resources may someday benefit the health needs of the donor’s family. If we are prepared to receive a transplant should we need one, then we should be ready to give Ethics: WHO Guiding Principles on Human Cell, Tissue and Organ Transplantation In 1987 the fortieth World Health Assembly, concerned at the trade for profit in human organs, initiated the preparation of the first WHO Guiding Principles on Transplantation, endorsed by the Assembly in 1991 in resolution WHA44.25. These Guiding Principles have greatly influenced professional codes and practices as well as legislation around the world during almost two decades. After a consultation process that took several years, the Sixty-third World Health Assembly adopted resolution WHA63.22 on 21 May 2010, endorsing the updated WHO Guiding Principles and identifying areas of progress to optimize donation and transplantation practices. The Declaration of Istanbul Professionals of donation and transplantation from all regions, through many of their organizations and institutions, are endorsing the Declaration of Istanbul on organ trafficking and transplant tourism developed under the leadership of the Transplantation Society and the International Society of Nephrology in May 2008.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Guidelines on Ethical Considerations Regarding Organ Donation for the Benefit of Donors and Recipients.** The commitment obtained from authorities regarding donation and altruistic living tissue donation is also crucial to advancing transplantation within the system. In addition, the decision to become a donor is based on the understanding that active participation in the market for living tissue can be beneficial for the donor's family in the event of illness. If there is an opportunity to receive living tissue when needed, one should be ready to donate. The Guidelines on Ethical Considerations of the World Health Assembly on Living Tissue Donation, Body and Organ Transplantation. In 1987, the unknown World Health Organization, on the issue of the ethics of human tissue donation, established the basis for the World Health Assembly Guidelines on Living Tissue Donation, which the Organization adopted in 1991 during agenda item WHA44.25. These Guidelines have had a significant impact on national and parliamentary legislation around the world for over a decade. Following extensive consultations, the Second World Health Assembly Resolution 63.22 was adopted on June 21, 2010, which also endorsed the updating of the World Health Assembly Guidelines on Ethical Considerations and the implementation of measures to promote the standardization of donation and living tissue transplantation practices. The Istanbul Declaration Organ donation and living tissue transplantation professionals from all regions, through various organizations and institutions, endorsed the Istanbul Declaration on the prevention of organ trafficking and the safe journey to the country where the tissue has been transplanted under the leadership of the Organ Transplantation Agency and the World Agency on indigenous knowledge in June 2008.



**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Cultural Context:** I've tried to maintain a formal and respectful tone, reflecting the nature of the document.
*   **Terminology:** Some terms related to organ donation and transplantation are specialized. I've used the most common English equivalents.
*   **Clarity:** I've aimed for clarity and readability while staying as faithful as possible to the original Yoruba text.
*   **Some phrases were difficult to interpret precisely due to the nuances of the Yoruba language and the context.** I've done my best to provide a reasonable and accurate translation.

translations.source: Ìrìn àjò àti ìlera Ìsọnísókí: Ìrìn àjò àgbáyé lè mú onírúurú ewu bá ìlera, èyí dá lórí àwọn àbùdá arìnrìn-àjò àti ìrìnàjò náà. Àwọn arìnrìn-àjò lè ní àwọn àyípadà òjìji nínú ìrúkèrúdò, òtútù, kòkòrò àìfojúrí, àti ìgbóná, èyí tí ó lè yọrí sí àìlera. Ní àfikún, àwọn ewu ìlera líle lè dìde ní àwọn àgbègbè tí ilégbèé kò ti dára, ìmọ́tótó àti àyíká mímọ́ kò tó, àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera kò ní ìdàgbàsókè tó bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni kò sí omi tí ó mọ́. Ìrìnàjò àgbáyé ń pọ̀ sí i, bí ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ènìyàn ṣe ń rin ìrìn àjò fún ìdí iṣẹ́, àwùjọ, ìgbádùn àti ọ̀rọ̀ ọmọnìyàn lọ́dọọdún. Gbogbo àwọn arìnrìn-àjò gbọ́dọ̀ gbáradì fún onírúurú ewu tí wọ́n lè kojú ní àwọn àyíká tí wọn kò dé rí tẹ́lẹ̀, lásìkò àti lẹ́yìn tí wọ́n rin ìrìn àjò. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbèrò láti rin ìrìn àjò gbọ́dọ̀ gbàmọ̀ràn lórí ewu tí ó lè wà ní àwọn àgbègbè tí wọ́n ń lọ kí wọ́n sì lóye ọ̀nà tí ó dára jùlọ láti dáàbò bo ìlera wọn kí wọ́n sì mú àdínkù bá ewu kíkó àìsàn. Ìṣètò ọjọ́ iwájú, àwọn òté ìdáàbòbò tí wọ́n tọ́ àti ìsọ́ra tí ó yẹ lè dáàbò bo ìlera àwọn arìnrìn-àjò, kí ó sì ṣe àdínkù ewu ìjàm̀bá àti kíkó àìsàn. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ilé-iṣẹ́ iṣẹ́ ìṣègùn àti ìrìn àjò lè pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ gbígbòòrò àti ìmọ̀ràn ìjìnlẹ̀, ó sì jẹ́ ojúṣe arìnrìn-àjò láti wá ìròyìn, láti lóye àwọn ewu tí wọ́n ní í ṣe àti láti gbé ìgbésẹ̀ tí ó yẹ láti dáàbò bo ìlera wọn nígbà tí wọ́n bá ń rin ìrìn àjò. Ewu: Nígbà tí a bá ń ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn ewu ìlera fún ìrìnàjò àgbáyé, àwọn arìnrìn-àjò àti àwọn alámọ̀já ìlera gbọ́dọ̀ wo àwọn kókó pàtàkì wọ̀nyí:
translations.reference: Travel and health Overview: International travel can pose various risks to health, depending on the characteristics of both the traveller and the travel. Travellers may encounter sudden and significant changes in altitude, humidity, microbes, and temperature, which can result in ill-health. In addition, serious health risks may arise in areas where accommodation is of poor quality, hygiene and sanitation are inadequate, medical services are not well developed and clean water is unavailable. International travel is on the rise, as millions of people travel for professional, social, recreational and humanitarian purposes each year. All travellers must prepare for the variety of health risks they can be exposed to in unfamiliar environments before, during and after they travel. People planning to travel should seek advice on the potential hazards in their chosen destinations and understand how best to protect their health and minimize the risk of acquiring disease. Forward planning, appropriate preventive measures and careful precautions can protect the health of travellers, and minimize the risks of accident and of acquiring disease. Although the medical profession and the travel industry can provide extensive help and sound advice, it remains the traveller’s responsibility to seek information, to understand the risks involved and to take the necessary precautions to protect their health while travelling. Risk: When determining the health risks of international travel, travellers and health professionals should consider the following key factors:
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Travel and Health Advisory: Global Travel and Disease Risks:** Travel can expose individuals to various health risks, stemming from the conditions of travel and destination. Travelers may experience diarrhea, fever, malaria, and other infections, which can lead to illness. Furthermore, health risks can arise in areas with poor sanitation, limited access to accurate information and healthcare, inadequate healthcare services, and a lack of safe drinking water. Global travel is increasing, as more people travel for work, leisure, enjoyment, and social connections. All travelers should be aware of the various risks they may encounter in unfamiliar environments, both during and after their travels. Individuals planning to travel should be informed about the potential risks in the areas they are visiting and should understand the best ways to protect their health and minimize the risk of illness. Pre-travel consultations, appropriate vaccinations, and necessary medications can protect the health of travelers and reduce the risk of serious illness and complications. While travel health and safety organizations can provide significant support and guidance, it is the traveler's responsibility to stay informed about the risks they face and to take appropriate steps to protect their health while traveling. Risks: When assessing health risks for global travel, travelers and healthcare professionals should consider the following key points:

translations.source: ọna ìrìnrìn-àjò (àwọn) ibi afẹ́dé àsìkò àti ìgbà ìrìnàjò ìdí ìrìnàjò ìkojúwọ̀n ilégbèé, ìmọ́tótó oúnjẹ àti àyíká mímọ́ ìwà arìnrìn-àjò ìlera àtẹ̀yìnwá arìnrìn-àjò Arìnrìn-àjò lè kojú àwọn ewu ìlera wọ̀nyí dá lórí ibi afẹ́dé wọn: ìgéjẹ ẹranko tàbí kòkòrò, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ẹ̀fọn, ìdun, àfòpiná, àti àwọn kòkòrò àfòká mìíràn, tí wọ́n lè tan àìsàn bí maléríà. ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú ẹ̀jẹ̀ tàbí àwọn omira mìíràn
translations.reference: mode of transport destination(s) duration and season of travel purpose of travel standards of accommodation, food hygiene and sanitation behaviour of the traveller underlying health of the traveller Travellers may encounter the following health risks depending on their destination: animal and insect bites, including mosquitoes, ticks, fleas, and some flies, which can spread diseases such as malaria exposure to blood or other bodily fluids
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English, aiming for accuracy and clarity:

**Translation:**

Travel route (regarding) designated resting points and travel times, the reason for travel, accommodation, food availability, and proper conduct for travelers. Travel health, prevention of illness. Travel can protect against these diseases based on their designated resting points: mosquito or tsetse fly bites, which transmit diseases like yellow fever, fever, meningitis, and other biting flies, which can cause illnesses like malaria. Protection from bites with nets or other repellents.

**Explanation of some choices:**

*   **"ọna ìrìnrìn-àjò"**:  I translated this as "Travel route" as it seems to be the core topic.
*   **"ìgbà ìrìnàjò"**: Translated as "travel times" to be more understandable.
*   **"ìdí ìrìnàjò"**: Translated as "the reason for travel."
*   **"ìkojúwọ̀n ilégbèé"**: Translated as "accommodation."
*   **"mímọ́ ìwà arìnrìn-àjò"**: Translated as "proper conduct for travelers."
*   **"àtẹ̀yìnwá arìnrìn-àjò"**: Translated as "prevention of illness."
*   **"ẹ̀fọn"**: Translated as "yellow fever" as it is the most common disease associated with mosquito bites in the region.
*   **"ìdun"**: Translated as "fever."
*   **"àfòpiná"**: Translated as "meningitis."
*   **"ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú ẹ̀jẹ̀ tàbí àwọn omira mìíràn"**: Translated as "Protection from bites with nets or other repellents."

translations.source: àyípadà àyíká, bí ìrúkèrúdò, òtútù, ìgbóná àwọn àìsàn àtara-oúnjẹsẹ̀ àti àtara-omisẹ̀ àìtó àwọn iṣẹ́ ìṣègùn ìfarapa, tí ó fi mọ́ láti ara ìjàm̀bá ojú pópó tàbí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìnajú ìlera ọpọlọ, tí ó fi mọ́ wàhálà àti ìyàlẹ́nu àṣà Àwọn arìnrìn-àjò tún lè faragbá onírúurú iye àwọn àìsàn, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn wọ̀nyí ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn àrùn kán wà, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn àrùn tí wọ́n léwu jùlọ, èyí tí kò sí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára fún. Àwọn ibi afẹ́dé níbi tí ilégbèé, ìmọ́tótó àti àyíká mímọ́, ìtọ́jú ìṣègùn àti àbùdá omi ti dára púpọ̀ ní ewu líle níwọ̀n fún ìlera àwọn arìnrìn-àjò. Níbi tí àwọn iṣẹ́ wọ̀nyí kò ti jẹ́ pípèsè, àwọn arìnrìn-àjò gbọ́dọ̀ gbé ìgbésẹ̀ ìsọ́ra gidi láti yàgò fún àìsàn. Àwọn ikilọ ìrìnàjò láti ọwọ́ àwọn àjọ ìjọba gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ fífi ọwọ́ líle mú.
translations.reference: environmental changes, such as altitude, humidity, temperature foodborne and waterborne diseases inadequate medical services injuries, including from road traffic accidents or recreational activities psychological health, including stress and culture shock Travellers may also be exposed to a number of infectious diseases, and many of these can be prevented by vaccination. However, there are some infectious diseases, including some of the most dangerous, for which no vaccines exist. Destinations where accommodation, hygiene and sanitation, medical care and water quality are of a high standard pose relatively few serious risks to the health of travellers. Where these services are not provided, travellers should take stringent precautions to avoid illness. Travel warnings from governmental sources should be taken seriously.

"The emergence of diseases, as complications, certainty, the severity of foodborne and waterborne illnesses resulting from inadequate sanitation, which stems from poor drainage systems or multiple health issues, which cause distress and frustration within the community. Travelers can also experience a variety of illnesses, and many of these are transmitted through contaminated water sources. Indeed, some diseases exist that are very dangerous, and for which there are no water-based preventative measures. Where these services are not adequate, travelers must take concrete preventative steps to protect themselves from illness. Travel advisories from government agencies should be strictly adhered to."

translations.source: Ìdáàbòbò: Síwájú ìrìnàjò: ẹ kàn sí ibùdó ìṣègùn fún ìrìnàjò tàbí oníṣègùn, bí ọ̀sẹ̀ 4-8 kí ẹ tó rin ìrìnàjò láti gba àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tí ó yẹ. ẹ ka nípa àìsàn abẹ́lé tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ibi afẹ́dé yín; ẹ béèrè fún ìròyìn lórí ewu maléríà àti ìdènà ẹ̀fọn jíjẹ. Èyí lè mọ́ ríra òògùn idenat tí ó tọ́ àti kíkó àwọ̀n ibùsùn àti òògùn ẹ̀fọn; ẹ ṣé àyẹ̀wò ìṣègùn àti eyín; ẹ gba àwọn àbá àkọsílẹ̀ fún òògùn gẹ́gẹ́ bí àsìkò tí ẹ máa lò kí ẹ sì ṣe àtòjọ irinṣẹ́ ìṣègùn tí ó yẹ; ẹ ra adójútòfo ìṣègùn tí ó yẹ pẹ̀lú ìpèsè tí ó tọ́ lẹ́yìn odi. Lásìkò ìrìnàjò: ẹ jẹ oúnjẹ tí wọ́n ṣe dáadáa àti ohun mímu tútù tí ó wà nínú ìgò tàbí ìdì nìkan. Ẹ ṣe omi mímu bí ẹ bá ń ṣiyèméjì nípa ààbò.
translations.reference: Prevention: Before travel: contact a travel medicine centre or a physician, preferably 4–8 weeks before departure to receive any required vaccinations; read up on local diseases related to your destination; request information on malaria risk and prevention of mosquito bites. This may include purchasing appropriate preventive medication and packing a bednet and insect repellent; get a medical and dental check-up; obtain prescriptions for medication according to length of stay and assemble a suitable medical kit; purchase medical insurance with appropriate cover abroad During travel: eat only thoroughly cooked food and drink only bottled or packaged cold drinks. Boil drinking-water if safety is doubtful.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Preparation:** Before traveling: consult a health professional for travel advice, about 4-8 weeks before you travel to receive the necessary yellow fever vaccinations. Discuss any skin conditions you have, especially those affecting the liver; ask for information about the risk of malaria and typhoid fever. This may involve a blood test to determine the appropriate medication and a supply of essential medicines and typhoid vaccines; have a health check and examination; obtain prescriptions for all necessary medications, including the timing of when to take them, and create a list of required health services; purchase the appropriate travel health kit with the correct dosage instructions after vaccination. **During Travel:** Eat healthy food and drink safe water from a bottle or well. Drink plenty of fluids as needed.

translations.source: ẹ ṣàkíyèsí àwọn ìjàm̀bá tàbí ìṣòro tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìjàm̀bá, ẹranko, èèwọ̀, òòrùn àti eré ìdárayá; ẹ mú ìwé pélébé tí ó ń ṣàfihàn ẹ̀yà ẹ̀jẹ̀ yín lọ́wọ́; ẹ wọ ẹ̀gbà ọwọ́ ìtanilólobó ìlera, bí ẹ bá ní àwọn èèwọ̀ tàbí àìsàn líle. Àwọn arìnrìn-àjò gbọ́dọ̀ ṣe àyẹ̀wò ìṣègùn nígbà tí wọ́n bá padà bí wọ́n ba: padà pẹ̀lú ibà láti orílẹ̀ èdè kan tí maléríà wà tàbí lè wà ní àìsàn líle ní àìsàn lẹ́yìn ọ̀sẹ̀ díẹ̀ tí wọ́n padà sí ilé gba ìtọ́jú maléríà nígbà tí wọ́n ń rin ìrìn àjò lè ti ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú àrùn líle kan lò ju oṣù mẹ́ta lọ ní orílẹ̀ èdè tí ń gòkè àgbà
translations.reference: be aware of accidents or problems related to traffic, animals, allergies, sun and sport; carry a card showing your blood group; wear a medical alert bracelet, if you have allergies or a chronic disease Travellers should obtain a medical examination on their return if they: return with a fever from a country where malaria is or may be present suffer from a chronic disease experience illness in the weeks following their return home received treatment for malaria while travelling may have been exposed to a serious infectious disease spent more than 3 months in a developing country
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"It is advisable to investigate fevers or illnesses associated with malaria, chills, weakness, headache, and convulsions; take a thermometer to check your body temperature; wear mosquito repellent clothing, if you have chills or illness. Travelers must have a medical check-up when they return, if they: return with a fever from a country where malaria exists or could exist, or experience illness within a few weeks of returning home, get a malaria test while traveling, or experience an illness for more than three months in a country with a high prevalence of malaria."

translations.source: Ikọ́ọfe Ìsọnísókí: Ikọ́ọfe ni bakitéríà (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) máa ń fà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó daabat máa ń mú àwọn ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró. Ikọ́ọfe máa ń tàn ká nípa afẹ́fẹ́ nígbà tí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọn ní ikọ́ọfe ní ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró bá wúkọ́, sín tàbí tutọ́. Eniyan nílò láti fa àwọn kòkòrò àrùn díẹ̀ sínú láti ṣe àìsàn. Lọ́dọọdún, mílíọ̀nù 10 ènìyàn máa ń ṣàìsàn ikọ́ọfe (TB). Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ó jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó ṣe é dènà tí ó sì se é wò, mílíọ̀nù 1.5 ènìyàn kú nítorí TB ní ọdọọdún – tí ó mú kí ó jẹ́ àrùn apànìyàn tí ó lékè jùlọ lágbàáyé. Ikọ́ọfe jẹ́ okùnfà ikú àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní KASA àti okùnfà ìlòdì sí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò bakitéríà gbòógì. Púpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ṣàìsàn ikọ́ọfe ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè, ṣùgbọ́n ikọ́ọfe wà káàkiri gbogbo àgbáyé. Bí ìdajì àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé pẹ̀lú ikọ́ọfe lè wà ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè 8 : Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippinesàti South Africa. Bí ìdá kan nínú mẹ́rin àwọn ènìyàn àgbáyé ni wọ́n ṣírò pé wọ́n ní kòkòrò bakitéríà ikọ́ọfe, ṣùgbọ́n ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ènìyàn kò ní tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àrùn TB bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn kan yóò san nínú àrùn náà.
translations.reference: Tuberculosis Overview: TB is caused by bacteria (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) and it most often affects the lungs. TB is spread through the air when people with lung TB cough, sneeze or spit. A person needs to inhale only a few germs to become infected. Every year, 10 million people fall ill with tuberculosis (TB). Despite being a preventable and curable disease, 1.5 million people die from TB each year – making it the world’s top infectious killer. TB is the leading cause of death of people with HIV and also a major contributor to antimicrobial resistance Most of the people who fall ill with TB live in low- and middle-income countries, but TB is present all over the world. About half of all people with TB can be found in 8 countries: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines and South Africa. About a quarter of the global population is estimated to have been infected with TB bacteria, but most people will not go on to develop TB disease and some will clear the infection.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

**Tuberculosis Information Sheet: Tuberculosis is a disease caused by the bacterium (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) and it causes pulmonary lesions. Tuberculosis spreads through the air when people with pulmonary tuberculosis cough, sneeze, or speak. A person needs to expel germs into the air to cause infection. Every year, 10 million people get tuberculosis (TB). Although it is a disease that can be treated and is curable, 1.5 million people die from TB each year – making it the most deadly infectious disease worldwide. Tuberculosis is a major cause of death among people with HIV/AIDS and a major driver of drug-resistant bacterial infections. Many people who get tuberculosis live in poor and underdeveloped countries, but tuberculosis exists all over the world. The number of people living with tuberculosis can be found in 8 countries: Bangladesh, China, India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Pakistan, Philippines, and South Africa. One in four people worldwide are estimated to have a tuberculosis bacterium, but many people do not progress to develop active TB disease, and some will relapse into the disease.**

translations.source: Àwọn tí wọ́n ní àrùn náà ṣùgbọ́n tí wọn kò tíì ṣe àìsàn kò lè ṣe ìkóràn àìsàn náà. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní bakitéríà ikọ́ọfe ní ewu ìgbésí ayé 5-10% láti ṣàìsàn ikọ́ọfe. Àwọn tí wọ́n ní ìṣòro ètò àjẹsára, bí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé pẹ̀lú KASA, àìjẹunrekánú tàbí àtọ̀gbẹ, tàbí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń fa tábà, wà nínú ewu púpọ̀ láti ṣe àìsàn. Àmì àìsàn: Lára àwọn àmì àrùn ikọ́ọfe tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni: Ikọ́ líle, Àyà dídùn, Rírẹ̀ tàbí ọ̀rẹ̀rẹ̀, RÍrù, Ibà, Òógùn òru Lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà, àwọn àmì àìsàn yìí yóò wà níwọ̀n fún ọ̀pọ̀ oṣù, èyí tí yóò yọrí sí àwọn ìdádúró láti wá ìtọ́jú bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò mú ìlọsíwájú bá ewu kíkó àrùn náà ran àwọn ẹlòmíràn. Bí oníṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera bá kẹ́fín pé aláìsàn kan ní àrùn ikọ́ọfe, wọn yóò rán aláìsàn náà lọ ṣe àyẹ̀wò. Ní ti ọ̀ràn ìkẹ́fín àrùn ikọ́ọfe ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró, wọn yóò sọ fún àwọn aláìsàn kí wọ́n pèsè kẹ̀lẹ̀bẹ̀ wọn fún àyẹ̀wò bakitéríà TB. Fún àwọn àrùn TB tí kò jẹ́ mọ̀ ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró, àwọn àpẹẹrẹ omira àti ìṣù ara tí ọ̀rọ̀ kan lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe àyẹ̀wò. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé dábàá àwọn àyẹ̀wò mòlíkúlà ayákánkán gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn àyẹ̀wò aṣáájú fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ṣàfihàn àmì àti àpẹẹrẹ TB. Àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìṣàyẹ̀wò mìíràn lè jẹ́ àyẹ̀wò fínnífínní kẹ̀lẹ̀bẹ̀ àti àwòrán onítànsàn-X àyà.
translations.reference: Those who are infected but not (yet) ill with the disease cannot transmit it. People infected with TB bacteria have a 5–10% lifetime risk of falling ill with TB. Those with compromised immune systems, such as people living with HIV, malnutrition or diabetes, or people who use tobacco, have a higher risk of falling ill. Symptoms: Common symptoms of TB disease include: Prolonged cough, Chest pain, Weakness or fatigue, Weight loss, Fever, Night sweats Often, these symptoms will be mild for many months, thus leading to delays in seeking care and increasing the risk of spreading the infection to others. If the healthcare provider suspects a patient to have TB disease, they will send the patient for testing. In the case of suspected lung TB disease, patients will be asked to give a sputum sample for testing for TB bacteria. For non-lung TB disease, samples of affected body fluids and tissue can be tested. WHO recommends rapid molecular diagnostic tests as initial tests for people showing signs and symptoms of TB. Other diagnostic tools can include sputum smear microscopy and chest X-rays.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Those who have the disease and are not experiencing symptoms cannot transmit the disease. People with bacterial infections have a 5-10% risk of developing tuberculosis. Those with risk factors for tuberculosis, such as people living with HIV/AIDS, immunocompromised individuals, or those who smoke, are at a higher risk of contracting the disease. Symptoms: Common symptoms of tuberculosis include: Cough, fever, chills, night sweats, weight loss, and fatigue. These symptoms can last for several months, which can lead to delays in diagnosis and increase the risk of spreading the disease to others. If a healthcare professional suspects that a patient has tuberculosis, they will refer the patient for further examination. In the case of suspected pulmonary tuberculosis, patients will be asked to provide sputum samples for TB bacterial testing. For non-pulmonary TB infections, samples of bodily fluids may be tested. The World Health Organization recommends molecular tests, such as rapid tests for people presenting with symptoms and TB samples. Other diagnostic tests may include microscopic examination of sputum and chest X-rays."

translations.source: Pẹ̀lú àrùn TB, ènìyàn lè ní àrùn bakitéríà TB tí yóò wà nínú ara láìṣe ọsẹ́. Àrùn yìí lè dàgbà di àrùn ikọ́ọfe bí ètò àjẹsára wọn bá káàárẹ̀. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní àrùn TB kì í ṣàfihàn èyíkéyìí àmì tàbí àpẹẹrẹ TB. Láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àrùn TB, àwọn oníṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera yóò ṣàyẹ̀wò àwọn aláìsàn tí wọ́n nínú ewu láti wọ́gilé TB tí ó wà nínú ara, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè lo àyẹ̀wò awọ ara tàbí ẹ̀jẹ̀ láti ṣàyẹ̀wò àrùn TB. Ìtọ́jú: Àrùn ikọ́ọfe ṣe é wò. Wọ́n máa ń tọ́jú rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú gbèdéke oṣù 6 plus àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò 4. Lára àwọn òògùn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ni rifampicin àti isoniazid. Ní ti àwọn ọ̀ràn kan bakitéríà TB kì í gbọ́ iwodsnt láti ara àwọn òògùn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ kan. Ní ti ọ̀ràn yìí, aláìsàn náà ni TB alòdìsí-òògùn. nítorí pé TB alòdìsí-òògùn máa ń pẹ́ ó sì ṣòro púpọ̀.
translations.reference: With TB infection, a person gets infected with TB bacteria that lie inactive in the body. This infection can develop into TB disease if their immune system weakens. People with TB infection do not show any signs or symptoms of TB. To identify TB infection, healthcare providers will screen at-risk patients to rule out active TB, and they may use a skin or blood test to check for TB infection. Treatment: TB disease is curable. It is treated by standard 6 month course of 4 antibiotics. Common drugs include rifampicin and isoniazid. In some cases the TB bacteria does not respond to the standard drugs. In this case, the patient has drug-resistant TB. for drug-resistant TB is longer and more complex.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

"With TB disease, a person can have bacterial TB infection that will be in the body without symptoms. This disease can become a severe illness depending on the severity of their condition. People with TB disease do not always show typical symptoms or signs of TB. To diagnose TB disease, healthcare workers will examine those at risk to detect TB that is in the body, and they can also use skin tests or sputum tests to check for TB. Treatment: Pulmonary tuberculosis is treated. It is treated with a full 6 months plus 4 intensive drugs. Among the common drugs are rifampicin and isoniazid. In some cases, the bacteria causing TB does not respond to the common drugs. In this case, the patient has multi-drug resistant TB, because multi-drug resistant TB is difficult and severe."

translations.source: Wọn yóò pèsè ìlò àwọn òògùn náà fún aláìsàn pẹ̀lú àlàyé, àmójútó àti àtìlẹ́yìn láti ọwọ́ òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera tàbí akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ afarajìn. Láìsí àtìlẹ́yìn yẹn, ìfarajìn fún ìtọ́jú lè ṣòro. Bí ìtọ́jú náà kò bá di ṣíṣe tan, àìsàn náà lè di alòdìsí-òògùn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ràn ká. Ní ti ọ̀ràn àrùn TB (níbi tí aláìsàn ti ní àrùn TB ṣùgbọ́n tí kò ṣàìsàn), ìtọ́jú ìdènà TB lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe láti dáwọ́ ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ àrùn náà dúró. Ìtọ́jú yìí ń ṣàmúlò àwọn òògùn kan náà fún ìgbà díẹ̀. Àwọn àṣàyàn ìtọ́jú lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ ti kéré àsìkò fún ìtọ́jú sí oṣù 1 tàbí 3, ní àfiwé oṣù 6 ní ìgbà kan.
