translations.source: WHO àti Republic of Korea buwọ́lu àdéhùn tó lápẹrẹ láti mú àlékún bá ìkápá ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí WHO àti Republic of Korea ti tọwọ́ bọ Ìwé Àdéhùn Àsọyépọ̀ láti ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ ibùdó ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àgbáyé fún ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí. Ààyè ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àgbáyé náà máa ṣiṣẹ́ sin gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré àti agbede-méjì (LMICs) láti pèsè àwọn ajẹmọ́-ìbí, bíi àjẹsára, insulínì, àkàndá sójà ara àti àwọn oògùn ààrùn jẹjẹrẹ. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 ṣàfihàn ìdí láti fẹ ìpèsè àjẹsára lábẹ́lé àti ní ẹkùn agbègbè àti àwọn ohun èlò ìlera mìíràn tó ṣe pàtàkì nínú LMICs láti ṣe àrídájú ànfààní tó dọ́gba lójú. Láti pèsè ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè láti lè tètè pèse àjẹsára mRNA, WHO, ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀, ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ ètò Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀ Ẹ̀rọ nRNA, pẹ̀lú ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ilé-ìwòsàn ibùdó ní South Africa ní oṣù Igbe ọdún 2023. Ìgbésẹ̀ oríṣiríṣi yìí àti àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ ìpèsè lábẹ́lẹ́ mìíràn láìpẹ́ yìí pè fún ìlànà-iṣẹ́ ìdagbàsókè àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ aláfọkànsìn fún ìgbaniwọlé, ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àti láti tẹ̀síwájú pẹ̀lú àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ ní àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn wọ̀nyìí, pẹ̀lú ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe tó yàtọ̀ tí ó ṣe pàtàkì nínú ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí. Láti wá iyànjú sí àlàfo yìí, WHO àti Republic of Korea ti kópa nínú àjọṣepọ̀ abẹ́lé láti bí ọdún kan wọ́n sì ti ń sọ ìfọkànsìn wọn fún ìdásílẹ̀ ibùdó ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àgbáyé fún ajẹmọ́-ìbí di tòótọ́. "Inú wa dùn láti buwọ́lù Ìwé Àdéhùn Àsọyépọ̀ yìí láti gbé àjọṣepọ̀ wa lọ sí ìpele tí ó kàn kí á sì ní àjọṣepọ̀ nínú àgbékalẹ̀ kọ̀ríkúlọ́mù Ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ kíkún. A lérò wí pé ibùdó ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ tó dára jùlọ lágbàyé yìí máa ràn wá lọ́wọ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ tí wọ́n nímọ̀ àti láti ríi dájú pé àwọn ohun èlò ìlera tó jẹ́ ojúlówó lè di ṣíṣe káàkiri àgbáyé, Dr Yukiko Nakatani, Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, Ànfààní sí Àwọn Oògùn àti Àwọn Ohun Èlò Ìlera. Ìjọba orílẹ̀-èdè Republic of Korea ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ orílẹ̀-èdè àti ètò lóriṣiríṣi tó kárí gbogbo ìpele ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí tó sì ń fẹ iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ lójú láti ṣe àfikún ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn akẹ́kọ̀ọ́ láti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè mìíràn pẹ̀lú kíkọ́ Ọgbà Ilé-ẹ̀kọ́ Ìbí Àgbáyé tí wọn yóò ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2025.
translations.reference: WHO and Republic of Korea sign landmark agreement to boost biomanufacturing capacity WHO and the Republic of Korea have signed a Memorandum of Understanding to establish a global training hub in biomanufacturing. This global training centre will serve all low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) to produce biologicals, such as vaccines, insulin, monoclonal antibodies, and cancer medicines. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need to expand the local and regional production of vaccines and other essential health products in LMICs to ensure equitable access. To provide countries with the know-how to rapidly produce mRNA vaccines, WHO, in collaboration with partners, established the mRNA Technology Transfer programme, with the formal inauguration of the Hub facility in South Africa in April 2023. This multilateral effort and other recent local production initiatives call for ambitious workforce development strategies to recruit, upskill and retain the staff working at these facilities, with unique competencies required in bioproduction. To address this gap, WHO and the Republic of Korea have engaged in closed collaboration for over a year and are now formalizing their commitment towards the establishment of a global training hub in biomanufacturing. “We are delighted to sign this Memorandum of Understanding to elevate our partnership to the next level and collaborate in the development of a comprehensive training curriculum. We hope this world-class training hub will help to build a skilled workforce and ensure quality health products can be manufactured around the world,” said Dr Yukiko Nakatani, WHO Assistant Director-General, Access to Medicines and Health Products. The Government of the Republic of Korea is offering a large range of national training facilities and programmes covering the full spectrum of biological manufacturing and is expanding its operations to accommodate more trainees from other countries with the construction of a Global Bio Education Campus to be launched in 2025.

Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

The World Health Organization (WHO) and the Republic of Korea have reaffirmed their commitment to strengthening immunization against preventable diseases. The WHO and Republic of Korea have agreed to the Joint Implementation Plan for the establishment of a global vaccine research hub. This global research hub will serve all low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) to prevent preventable diseases, such as measles, polio, rotavirus, and other infectious diseases. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need to strengthen immunization coverage and access to essential health products in LMICs to address the existing gaps. To enhance capacity for countries to assess mRNA vaccines, WHO, in partnership with partners, established the mRNA Technology Transfer Hub program, with the launch of a pilot training center in South Africa in October 2023. These various steps and other immunization initiatives recently undertaken call for a workforce development strategy for the sustainability of vaccinators, research, and to continue working with healthcare workers in these facilities, with the specialized knowledge that is critical in immunization. To address this need, WHO and the Republic of Korea have partnered in a long-term collaboration and have been advocating for the establishment of a global vaccine research hub. "We are delighted to partner with the Joint Implementation Plan to take our partnership to the next level, including collaboration in the establishment of a large-scale research hub. We believe that this robust research hub will contribute to building a skilled workforce and ensuring that essential health products are accessible worldwide," said Dr. Yukiko Nakatani, Assistant Director-General of the WHO, Access to Medicines and Health Products. The Republic of Korea government is establishing national research institutes and various programs covering all levels of immunization and is working to expand partnerships with other countries through the establishment of the Global Maternal and Child Health Institute, which will be launched in 2025.

translations.source: Mo lérò pé Àdéhùn Àsọyépọ̀ yìí máa ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ láàrín Republic of Korea àti WHO láti ró ìkápá ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí fún LMICs lágbára," Mr Hwang Seunghyun Olùdarí Àpapọ̀, Ọ́fíísì Ibùdó Àjẹsára Àgbáyé ní Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera àti Ìgbáyégbádùn láti Republic of Korea. Ní àfiwé, WHO ń ṣe àtúnṣe sí ìrólágbára ètò òfin láti rí i dájú pé ojúlówó àti ààbò àwọn ohun èlò ìlera wà. Àwọn àjọ alámòjútó tó lágbára máa tún mú kí ìgbàgbọ́ wà nínú àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n ṣe ní abẹ́lé tí yóò sì gbógun ti ìròyìn ẹlẹ́jẹ̀ àti wiwà àwọn oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó tàbí ayédèrú tó léwu.
translations.reference: “I hope this Memorandum of Understanding will enhance cooperation between the Republic of Korea and WHO to strengthen biomanufacturing capacities of LMICs,” said Mr Hwang Seunghyun Director General, Global Vaccine Hub Office at the Ministry of Health and Welfare from Republic of Korea. In parallel, WHO is intensifying regulatory system strengthening to ensure the quality and safety of health products. Stronger regulatory agencies will also enhance confidence in locally produced products and counter misinformation and the availability of unsafe substandard or falsified medicines.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"I believe that this Memorandum of Understanding will serve as a support for collaboration between the Republic of Korea and the WHO to address the challenge of maternal mortality for LMICs (Low and Middle-Income Countries)," said Mr. Hwang Seunghyun, Director General, Health and Welfare Diplomacy Division, Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Korea. According to the statement, WHO is strengthening its regulatory systems to ensure the safety and quality of medical supplies. Strong civil society organizations will also be encouraged to have a voice in the selection of medical supplies, which will further strengthen the monitoring of medicines and the quality of vaccines that are safe and effective.

translations.source: WHO rọ àwọn ìjọba láti dáwọ́ owó ìrànwọ́ lórí àwọn ọ̀gbin tábà tó lè sẹku pani dúró Ní Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Kò Sí Tábà, WHO rọ àwọn ìjọba láti dáwọ́ ìpèsè owó ìrànwọ́ fún dídáko tábà kí wọ́n sì ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ohun ọ̀gbìn alálòpẹ́ tí ó lè bọ́ àwọn mílíọ̀nù. "Tábà ni okùnfà fún mílíọ̀nù 8 ikú ní ọdún kan, síbẹ̀ àwọn ìjọba káàkiri ayé ń ná mílíọ̀nù láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún oko tábà," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO. "Pẹ̀lu yíyan láti gbin oúnjẹ dípò tábà, a ti mú ìlera lọ́kùkúdùn, tọ́jú àwùjọ àwọn abẹ̀mí, àti ìrólágbára fún ààbò oúnjẹ fún gbogbo ènìyàn." Ó lé ní mílíọ̀nù 300 àwọn ènìyàn lágbàyé tí wọ́n ń kojú àìsí ààbò oúnjẹ tó lágbára. Ní báyìí ó lé ní mílíọ̀nù 3 saare ilẹ̀ káàkiri àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó lé ní 120 tí wọ́n ń lò láti gbin tábà aṣekúpani, kódà ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè níbi tí ebi ti ń pa àwọn ènìyàn. Ìjábọ̀ tuntun WHO kan, "Gbin oúnjẹ, kìí ṣe tábà," ṣàfihàn àwọn aburú ọ̀gbin tábà àti àwọn ànfààní ṣíṣẹ́rí padà sí àwọn ọ̀gbin oúnjẹ alálòpẹ́ tó pọ̀ fún àwọn àgbẹ̀, àwọn àwùjọ, ọrọ̀ ajé, agbègbè, àti gbogbo ayé lápapọ̀. Ìjábọ̀ náà tún tù àṣírí ẹ̀ka ilé-iṣẹ́ tábà fún fífi àwọn àgbẹ̀ pamọ́ sínú gbèsè àìlópin, ṣíṣe ìpolongo fún ọ̀gbin tábà pẹ̀lú àsọdùn àwọn ànfààní ọrọ̀-ajérẹ̀ àti bíbẹ̀bẹ̀ nípasẹ̀ àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àgbẹ̀ ẹlẹ́yinjú àánú. Ọ̀gbin tábà ń fa ààrùn fún àwọn àgbẹ̀ fúnra wọn bẹ́ẹ̀ ó lé ní mílíọ̀nù 1 àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n ń ṣe òwò ẹrú tí wọ́n ṣírò pé wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́ lórí àwọn oko tábà, tí wọ́n sì ń pàdánù ànfààní wọn láti ní ẹ̀kọ́ ìwé. "Tábà kìí ṣe ewu ńlá fún ààbò oúnjẹ nìkan, ṣùgbọ́n fún àpapọ̀ ìlera, tó fi mọ́ ìlera àwọn àgbẹ̀ oní tábà.
translations.reference: WHO urges governments to stop subsidizing life-threatening tobacco crops On World No Tobacco Day, WHO urges governments to stop subsidizing tobacco farming and support more sustainable crops that could feed millions. “Tobacco is responsible for 8 million deaths a year, yet governments across the world spend millions supporting tobacco farms,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “By choosing to grow food instead of tobacco, we prioritize health, preserve ecosystems, and strengthen food security for all.” More than 300 million people globally are faced with acute food insecurity. Meanwhile more than 3 million hectares of land across more than 120 countries are being used to grow deadly tobacco, even in countries where people are starving. A new WHO report, “Grow food, not tobacco,” highlights the ills of tobacco growing and the benefits of switching to more sustainable food crops for farmers, communities, economies, the environment, and the world at large. The report also exposes the tobacco industry for trapping farmers in a vicious cycle of debt, propagating tobacco growing by exaggerating its economic benefits and lobbying through farming front groups. Tobacco farming causes diseases to the farmers themselves and more than 1 million child laborers are estimated to be working on tobacco farms, missing their opportunity for an education. “Tobacco is not only a massive threat to food insecurity, but health overall, including the health of tobacco farmers.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba document:

WHO urges governments to invest money in tobacco control measures that could save lives. In the No Tobacco Day report, WHO urges governments to allocate tobacco tax revenue to fund tobacco cessation programs and to support a range of essential health services that could benefit millions. "Tobacco is a killer for 8 million people each year, yet governments around the world are failing to invest in tobacco control," said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. "With the opportunity to grow food instead of tobacco, we can improve nutrition, reduce poverty, and increase food security for everyone." He noted that 300 million people worldwide are suffering from severe food insecurity. Currently, 3 million households in about 120 countries are using tobacco to combat hunger, even in countries where famine is killing people. A new WHO report, "Food, Not Tobacco," highlights the harms of tobacco cultivation and the benefits of shifting to food crops for farmers, communities, economies, regions, and the world at large. The report also calls for the dismantling of the tobacco industry, integrating farmers into sustainable livelihoods, and developing tobacco cultivation policies with the participation of vulnerable farming communities. Tobacco cultivation causes illness for farmers, and it is noted that 1 million children are involved in child labor, reportedly working on tobacco farms, and are missing out on educational opportunities. "Tobacco is not only a major threat to food security, but also to public health, impacting the health of tobacco farmers in particular."

translations.source: Ó ń gbé àwọn àgbẹ̀ síta sí àwọn oògùn apa kòkòro oní kẹ́míkà, èéfín tábà àti iye èròjà tábà tí a lè rí nínú sìgá 50 - tó ń yọrí sí àìsàn bíi ààrùn ẹdọ̀fóró tó lágbára àti májèlé èròjà tábà," Dr Ruediger Krech, Olùdarí Ìpolongo Ìlera ní WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. Ọ̀gbin tábà jẹ́ ìṣòro àgbáyé. Àfojúsùn náà ti wà tipẹ́ ní Asia àti South America, ṣùgbọ́n àwọn dátà tuntun fi hàn pé àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ tábà ń gbòòrò si sí ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀. Láti ọdún 2005, ó ti tó bíi àlékún 20% nínú oko ọ̀gbin tábà tó ti wà káàkiri ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀. WHO, Ilé-Iṣẹ́ Nǹkan Ọ̀gbìn àti Oúnjẹ àti Ètò Oúnjẹ Àgbáyé ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Àwọn Oko Aláìní Tábà tí yóò pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún 5000 àwọn àgbẹ̀ ní orílẹ̀-èdè Kenya àti Zambia láti gbin èso oúnjẹ alálòpẹ́ dípò tábà. Ní ọdọọdún Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Kò Sí Tábà máa ń bu ọlá fún àwọn tí wọ́n ń mú àyípadà bá ìdènà tábà. Ní ọdún yìí ọ̀kan nínú àwọn alámì-ẹ̀yẹ, Ms Sprina Robi Chacha, àgbẹ̀ obìnrin láti orílẹ̀-èdè Kenya, ni wọ́n ń fún ní àmì-ẹ̀yẹ fún ìṣípòrọpò láti ọ̀gbin tábà sí àwọn ẹ̀wà oní purotéénì púpọ̀ nìkan, ṣùgbọ́n fún kíkọ́ ọgọ́rùn lọ́na ọgọ́rùn àwọn àgbẹ̀ tí ó kù lórí bí wọ́n ṣe lè ṣe èyí láti pèsè àwùjọ tí ní àlàáfíà. Àwọn ẹgbẹ́ 182 níbi Àpérò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ WHO lórí Ìdènà Tábà ti ní àfọkànsìn láti "... ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn àfidípò tó dára fún ọrọ̀-ajéfún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ àti àwọn tó ń gbin tábà". Ọ̀nà kan tó ṣe pàtàkì tí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lè mú ojúṣe yìí wá sí ìmúṣẹ ni fífi òpin sí owó ìrànwọ́ fún ọ̀gbin tábà àti ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn èso tó ní àlàáfíà. Pẹ̀lu yíyan láti gbin oúnjẹ dípò tábà, a ti mú ìlera lọ́kùkúdùn, dáàbò bo àwùjọ abẹ̀mí, àti àlékún ààbò oúnjẹ.
translations.reference: Farmers are exposed to chemical pesticides, tobacco smoke and as much nicotine as found in 50 cigarettes – leading to illnesses like chronic lung conditions and nicotine poisoning,” said Dr Ruediger Krech, Director of Health Promotion at WHO. Tobacco growing is a global problem. The focus has so far been in Asia and South America, but the latest data show tobacco companies are expanding to Africa. Since 2005, there has been a nearly 20% increase in tobacco farming land across Africa. WHO, the Food and Agriculture Organization and the World Food Programme support the Tobacco Free Farms initiative that will provide help to more than 5000 farmers in Kenya and Zambia to grow sustainable food crops instead of tobacco. Every year World No Tobacco Day honours those making a difference in tobacco control. This year one of the Awardees, Ms Sprina Robi Chacha, a female farmer from Kenya, is being recognized for not only switching from growing tobacco to high protein beans, but also training hundreds of other farmers on how to do this to create a healthier community. 182 Parties to the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control have committed to “...promote economically viable alternatives for tobacco workers and growers”. A crucial way that countries can fulfill this obligation is by ending subsidies for tobacco growing and supporting healthier crops. By choosing to grow food instead of tobacco, we prioritize health, preserve ecosystems, and increase food security.

Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"It is shifting farmers to alternative crops like chemical-resistant cassava, beans, and the value of tobacco that can be seen in a 50-year cycle – leading to diseases like severe diabetes and the high value of tobacco," said Dr. Ruediger Krech, Head of Tobacco Policy at WHO. Tobacco cultivation is a global problem. The warning was initially prevalent in Asia and South America, but new data shows that tobacco companies are expanding into developing countries. Since 2005, it has accounted for about 20% of tobacco cultivation worldwide. WHO, the World Health Organization and the World Food Programme are supporting the establishment of Tobacco-Free Farming Initiatives that will provide assistance to 5000 farmers in Kenya and Zambia to grow nutritious food instead of tobacco. During World No Tobacco Day, praise is given to those who are reducing tobacco consumption. This year, one of the awardees, Ms. Sprina Robi Chacha, a female farmer from Kenya, is being honored for her advocacy in transitioning from tobacco to high-protein crops, but for the benefit of the nine farmers who died trying to figure out how to do it safely. 182 groups under the WHO’s Framework Convention on Tobacco Control have committed to "...provide support for viable alternatives for workers and those involved in tobacco cultivation." One key way countries can achieve this is by redirecting funding for tobacco cultivation and supporting nutritious food alternatives. With the shift to growing food instead of tobacco, health has improved, poverty has been reduced, and the percentage of hunger has decreased. "

translations.source: Ìjábọ̀ tó lápẹẹrẹ ya ojú ọ̀nà fún ṣíṣe àtúntò àwọn ọrọ̀ ajé láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn Nínú ìjábọ̀ àkọ́kọ́-irú-rẹ̀, Ìgbìmọ WHO lórí Àwọn ọrọ̀-ajéÌlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn ti ṣe àlàkalẹ̀ ọ̀nà tuntun láti ṣe àtúntò àwọn ọrọ̀-ajéláti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn nǹkan tó ṣe kókó - ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Ìgbìmọ̀ náà, tí Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní oṣù Bẹlu ọdún 2020 ní ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, tí Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Mariana Mazzucato jẹ́ alaga rẹ̀, ti lo ọdún méjì sẹ́yìn láti gbèrò ọrọ̀-ajénáà láti ojú ìwòye ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn, tí ó sì ń fi gbogbo agbára ti ìpìlẹ̀-òye pé ìlera ènìyàn àti àgbáyé gbọ́dọ̀ wà ní àárín gbùngbùn bí a bá ṣe ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò àwùjọ, ìlera àti ọrọ̀-ajéwa àti àwọn òfin. Ìgbìmọ̀ náà ti ṣe ìtìsíwájú àlàyé tuntun tó fojú hàn tó fẹsẹ̀múlẹ̀ nínú ọgbọ́n ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun láti ṣe àtúntò àwọn ọrọ̀-ajé láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn kárí àwọn kókó ọ̀rọ̀ mẹ́rin tó jọra: Iye - mímọ iye àti wíwọn àwọn nǹkan tó ṣe pàtàkì nípasẹ̀ àwọn mẹ́tíríkì ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun; Ìpèsè owó - bí a ṣe lè pèsè owó fún ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìdókówò ọlọ́jọ́-pípẹ́, tí kìí ṣe iye owó onígbà-díẹ̀; ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun- bí a ṣe lè mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunìlera fún dáadáa gbogbo ènìyàn; Ìkápá - bí a ṣe ró oríṣiríṣi ìkápá àwọn ẹ̀ka ìjọba lágbára láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. "Ní ọdún méjì sẹ́yìn, mo ní kí ikọ̀ àwọn onímọ̀ nípa ọrọ̀-ajé tó ń léwájú lágbàyé àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera àwùjọ - gbogbo wọn jẹ́ obìnrin - láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àyípadà iṣẹ́.
translations.reference: Landmark report charts route for reorienting economies to deliver health for all In the first-ever report of its kind, the WHO Council on the Economics of Health for All has outlined a bold new path to reorient economies to deliver what matters - health for all. The Council, created by WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus in November 2020 in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, and chaired by Professor Mariana Mazzucato, has spent that last two years rethinking the economy from a health for all perspective, and pushing forcefully the principle that human and planetary health must be at the heart of how we design our social, health and economic systems and policies. The Council has put forward a bold new narrative grounded in new economic wisdom to reorient economies to deliver health for all across four interrelated themes: Value - valuing and measuring what matters through new economic metrics; Finance - how to finance health for all as a long-term investment, not a short-term cost; Innovation - how to advance health innovation for the common good; Capacity - how to strengthen dynamic public sector capacity to achieve health for all. “Two years ago, I asked a team of the world’s leading economists and public health experts – all women – to create a paradigm shift.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Report with a Roadmap for Shaping Financial Words to Establish Health for All**

Within this report of its kind, the WHO Council on Financial Health for All has developed a new approach to shaping financial words to establish essential – health for all. The Council, established by WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus in memory of the COVID-19 pandemic in February 2020, with economist Mariana Mazzucato as chair, has spent two years refining the financial narrative from a health perspective for all. It emphasizes that people's health and global health must be at the center of social, health, and financial policies and systems.

The Council has advanced a new understanding that emerged within the new financial science to shape financial words to establish health for all, focusing on four key areas:

*   **Value:** Meaningful value and assessing essential items through new financial metrics.
*   **Capital:** How to allocate capital for health for all sustainably, rather than short-term financial returns.
*   **Innovation:** How to drive innovation in health to benefit everyone.
*   **Accountability:** How to hold various branches of government accountable to ensure health for all.

"Over the past two years, I have brought together a group of experts on global finance and social health – all of whom are women – to create a new wave of work."



**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for clarity and accuracy while maintaining the tone of the original document.
*   Some Yoruba terms related to finance and governance are complex and have been translated to the best of my ability to convey the intended meaning in English.
*   The structure of the Yoruba language can sometimes be different from English, so I've adjusted sentence structures for better readability in English.

translations.source: Báyìí, dípò kí wọ́n rí ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ẹrú fún ìdàgbàsókè ọrọ̀-ajé, a ní ìlànà-iṣẹ́ fún àtúntò àwọn igbéṣẹ̀ ọrọ̀-ajé ní ọ̀nà tí yóò mú wa débi èròngbà rírí gbogbo ènìyàn pẹ̀lú ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì tó yára pẹ̀lú àwọn àbájáde tó dára," Dr Tedros ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Láti bí ọdún méjì sẹ́yìn, Ìgbìmọ̀ WHO lórí Àwọn Ọrọ̀-ajé Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn ti ṣiṣẹ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àlàyé ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun - èyí tí ó lè mú àyípadà bá ìpèsè owó fún ìlera láti ìnáwó sí ìdókówò," Alága Ìgbìmọ̀ náà, Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Mariana Mazzucato ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "A ti ṣe àyẹ̀wò àwọn àyípadà tó nílò - tó fi mọ́ sí àgbékalẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀, àwọn àjọṣepọ̀ ìjọba-aládàni, àti àwọn ètò ìṣúná - láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọrọ̀-ajé tí yóò pèsè ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Nínú ìjábọ̀ ìgbẹ̀yin wa, a pè fún òfin ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun tí kìí ṣe nípa ìṣàkóso ọjà ṣùgbọ́n nípa ṣíṣe àto àwọn ọjà lọ́nà tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ àti ní àjọṣepọ̀ tí ó mú ìlera àwọn ènìyàn àti àgbáyé ní ọ̀kùkúdùn." Wọ́n ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní oní ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin, ìjábọ̀ náà, tí àkòrí rẹ̀ jẹ́ Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn: Mímú Àyípadà Bá Àwọn Ọrọ̀-ajé láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn nǹkan tó ṣe pàtàkì (https://www.who.int/groups/who-council-on-the-economics-of-health-for-all), pèsè ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tuntun tí wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ lórí àwọn òpó mẹ́rin tó wà lókè, pẹ̀lú àwọn àbá kan pàtó lábẹ́ ìkọ̀ọ̀kan - tó mú látara iṣẹ́ àtẹ̀yìnwá Ìgbìmọ̀ náà. Lára àwọn àbá pàtàkì ni: A nílò láti mọ iyì àti òdiwọn àwọn nǹkan tó ṣe pàtàkì tòótọ́ - gbígbèrú àwọn ènìyàn àti àgbáyé - yàtọ̀ sí lílé ìdagbàsókè ọrọ̀-ajé àti jíjẹ èrè GDP lọ́pọ̀ láì ní ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn àbájáde rẹ̀. Láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn, àwọn ìjọba gbọ́dọ̀ gbèrò iye kí wọ́n sì ṣe àtúntò àti ìdarí ọrọ̀-ajé tó dá lórí ìgbáyégbádùn àwùjọ àti àgbáyé, tí mẹ́tíríkì tuntun ń darí. Àtúnṣe ìpìlẹ̀ fún àwọn ètò orílẹ̀-èdè àti ilẹ̀ òkèèrè fún ìpèsè owó fún ìlera ni a nílò, kí a lè ṣe ìnáwó lórí ìlera gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìdókówò ọlọ́jọ́-pípẹ́. Ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn máa nílò ọ̀pọ̀ owó, àti ojúlówó ìpèsè owó tó pọ̀. ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunnílò òye àjọni - Kìí ṣe èso ilé-iṣẹ́ kan ṣoṣo tàbí ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba. Ṣùgbọ́n àyàfi tí wọ́n bá darí ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunfún dáadáa gbogbo ènìyàn, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn ni ó máa yọ kúrò nínú àwọn ànfààní rẹ̀.
translations.reference: Now, instead of health for all being seen as the servant of economic growth, we have a roadmap for structuring economic activity in a way that will allow us to reach the goal of seeing all people with access to essential health services faster with better results,” said Dr Tedros. “Over the past two years, the WHO Council on the Economics of Health for All has worked to craft a new economic narrative – one that transforms financing for health from an expenditure to an investment,” said the Council’s Chair, Professor Mariana Mazzucato. “We have examined the changes needed – including to the structure of patents, public-private partnerships, and budgets – to design an economy that delivers Health for All. In our final report, we call for new economic policy that is not about market fixing but about proactively and collaboratively shaping markets that prioritize human and planetary health.” Launched today in conjunction with the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly, the report, entitled Health for All: Transforming economies to deliver what matters (https://www.who.int/groups/who-council-on-the-economics-of-health-for-all), provides a new framework built on the above four pillars, with specific recommendations under each – drawing from the Council’s previous work. Key recommendations include: We need to value and measure the things that truly matter - human and planetary flourishing - rather than pursuing economic growth and GDP maximization regardless of the consequences. To achieve health for all, governments must rethink value and reshape and redirect the economy based on social and planetary well-being, guided by new metrics. A fundamental overhaul of national and international systems for financing health is needed, so that spending on health is treated as a long-term investment. Delivering Health for All will require both more money, and higher quality financing.Innovation requires collective intelligence—it is never the fruit of just one company or government agency. But unless innovation is governed for the common good, many people remain excluded from its benefits.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Currently, instead of prioritizing health for all as a public good, like a resource for economic development, we have a framework for reforming health financing in a way that will bring us closer to achieving universal health coverage with a focus on essential health services that are rapid and with good outcomes," Dr. Tedros stated. "For the past two years, the WHO Council on the Economics of Health for All has been working to develop new health financing models – those that can shift investment in health from illness to wellness," the Council's Chair, Professor Mariana Mazzucato, said. "We have examined the necessary shifts – focusing on the creation of foundations, public-private partnerships, and investment programs – to develop a financing model that will ensure health for all. In our report, we call for new economic policies that are not about market control but about shaping markets in a way that works and in partnership that promotes the health of people and the planet." Launched today in partnership with the Partnership for Global Health Security, the report, titled Health for All: Mobilizing Action through Economic Reforms to Build Essential Goods (https://www.who.int/groups/who-council-on-the-economics-of-health-for-all), presents a new approach built on four pillars, with specific actions under each – stemming from the Council’s work. Key actions include: We need to understand the value and cost-effectiveness of essential goods – for people and the planet – differently from the traditional economic development metrics and GDP growth that are not aligned with their benefits. To achieve health for all, governments must increase the amount and reform the management of economic resources based on social and global well-being, with new priorities driving them. Reforming the structure of national and regional programs for health financing is needed, so that we can invest in health throughout life as a long-term asset. Implementing Health for All will require significant investment and a substantial return on investment. Evidence-based knowledge – not just a single business or government entity. But unless they lead to good knowledge for everyone, many people will be excluded from its benefits."

translations.source: Àwùjọ ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunìlera ìbẹ̀rẹ̀-dé-òpin tuntun tí ó mú dáadáa gbogbo ènìyàn ní ọ̀kùkúdùn ni a nílò. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 ṣe ti fi hàn, ojúlówó àti ìkápá ìjọba ṣe pàtàkì. Àwọn ìjọba tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ kọ́ ni wọ́n kéré jùlọ, ṣùgbọ́n àwọn tí wọ́n ní àgbékalẹ̀ tó dára tí wọ́n sì ṣe ìpèsè fún dáadáa, pẹ̀lú ìpèsè owó àti èyí tó níṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn ènìyàn wọn àti ohun amáyédẹrùn. Ṣíṣe ìdókówò nínú ìkápá ìjọba ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn. Ìjábọ̀ náà tún pèsè àmọ̀ràn lórí ohun tí ó lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe nínú ìfikọ́ra láti ṣàmúlò àwọn àyípadà tí wọ́n nílò láti ṣe àtúntò àwọn ìlànà fún iye ọrọ̀-ajé, ìpèsè owó fún ìlera, ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunàti ṣíṣe ìdagbàsókè ìkápá ẹ̀ka àwùjọ nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Láàrín àwọn wọ̀nyìí, ìjábọ̀ náà mẹ́nu ba oríṣiríṣi àpẹẹrẹ, tó fi mọ́: Ilé-ìwòsàn ibùdó ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ mRNA ní South Africa: ètò adálórí-iye tí ó ń gbìyànjú láti gba ẹ̀tọ́ ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun, ìpèsè owó àti ìkápá lórí-àfojúsùn; Ìdókòwò àwùjọ orílẹ̀-ède Brazil nínú ilé ìtàjà ilé-iṣẹ́-ìlera tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún dáadáa gbogbo eniyan; Àwọn ilé-ìfowópamọ́ onídágbàsókè ní ẹkùn agbègbè gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn aṣokùnfa àyípadà ní Gúúsù Àgbáyé; Ìṣọ̀kan Ìgbáyégbádùn Ọrọ̀-ajé - ìṣọ̀kan oríṣiríṣi àwọn ìjọba àti àwọn àjọ tó lé ní 600 mìíràn tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá àwọn ètò ọrọ̀-ajé nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún ẹ̀mí;
translations.reference: A new end-to-end health innovation ecosystem that prioritises the common good is needed. As the COVID-19 pandemic has made clear, the quality and capacity of government matters. Effective governments are not the smallest, but those that are well-designed and properly resourced, both financially and in terms of their people and infrastructure. Re-investing in government capacity is crucial to delivering Health for All. The report also provides suggestions on what can be done in practice to implement the changes needed to reorient measures of economic value, the financing of health, innovation and building public sector capacity in the service of health for all. Among these, the report mentions several examples, including: The mRNA technology hub facility in South Africa: a values-driven system that tries to get the innovation, financing and capacity right ex-ante; Brazil’s public investment in a health-industrial complex that serves the common good; Regional development banks as enablers of change in the Global South; The Wellbeing Economy Alliance – an alliance of several governments and over 600 other organizations working together to transform economic systems in the service of life;
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"A robust science-policy interface for evidence-based policymaking is needed to benefit all people. Especially since the emergence of the COVID-19 pandemic, vigilance and government response have become critical. Governments that are functioning effectively are not many, but those with strong institutions and are committed to well-being, including financial commitments and actions with their people and health resources, are essential. Conducting research within government response is critical to building Universal Health Coverage. The issue also raises questions about what can be done within the framework to address the needs to refine policies for financial resources, financial commitment to health, scientific innovation to drive the expansion of government response capacity within the framework of universal health coverage. Among these, the issue highlights various examples, including: mRNA technology hub in South Africa: a value-addition initiative aimed at acquiring knowledge rights, financial commitment and capacity building on sustainability; National social security system in Brazil within the private health sector operating for the benefit of all people; Regional innovation hubs, such as the adaptation facilitators in the Global South; The Global Fund for Financing – a collaboration of various governments and 600 other organizations working together to bring adaptation to financial programs within the framework of health financing for life."

translations.source: Àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ fún ìpèsè owó fún ètò amúṣẹ́ṣe (NAPs) lórí ẹ̀yàwuuru jáàgùn nípasẹ̀ ètò ìṣúná àjọṣe ti oríṣiríṣi ẹ̀ka, pẹ̀lú pé ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn NAP ni wọn ò rí ìpèsè owó. Àwọn àbá tí wọ́n fi sínú ìjábọ̀ náà lè mú àyípadà bá ọ̀nà tí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ń gbà wò tí wọ́n sì ń pèsè owó fún ìlera. WHO ń pè àwọn aṣòfin, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àti àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ ìlera àti ọrọ̀-ajé láti gbèrò kíkún nípa àwọn àbá kí wọ́n sì lò wọ́n gẹ́gẹ́ bí atọ́ka-ọ̀nà láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn òfin ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun àti àwọn àgbékalẹ̀ tó lè mú wa bọ́ sí ojú ọ̀na láti sọ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn di tòótọ́.
translations.reference: Approaches to financing national action plans (NAPs) on antimicrobial resistance through multi sector joint budgeting, given that most NAPs remain unfunded. The recommendations included in the report could change the way countries view and finance health. WHO calls on policy-makers, civil society, and members of the health and economics communities to give full consideration to the recommendations and use them as a compass to develop new economic policies and structures that can move us along the road to making health for all a reality.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Steps for financial planning for National Action Plans (NAPs) on antimicrobial resistance through market-based intervention programs of various sectors, with the understanding that many NAPs do not have financial plans. The inclusions in the report could lead to deviations from the way countries allocate resources for health. WHO is calling on parliamentarians, civil society groups, and members of health and economic policy communities to raise awareness about the inclusions and to use them extensively as a roadmap to develop new financial frameworks and initiatives that can lead us closer to the path of providing health for all."

translations.source: Wọ́n ti yan àwọn gbajúgbajà òṣèré Renée Fleming àti Pretty Yende gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Aṣojú Onínúure fún Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti Ìlera Inú WHO dùn láti kéde ìyànsípò àwọn ìlú-mọ̀-ọ́-ká òṣèré nì Renée Fleming àti Pretty Yende gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn Aṣojú Onínúure fún Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti Ìlera. Ìkéde àmì-ẹ̀yẹ yìí ni wọ́n ṣe gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára ìrántí ayẹyẹ Àjọ̀dún 75th WHO lásíkò Àpèjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin ní ọjọ́ 22 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023. Renée Fleming, ọkàn nínú àwọn olórin àsìkò wa tó gba àmì-ẹ̀yẹ jùlọ, àti Pretty Yende, olóhùn tínrín aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ tí a mọ̀ fún orin rẹ̀ tó ń dáni lọrùn , ti wọ́n ti yàn fún àṣeyọrí iṣẹ́-ọnà wọn àti ìgbẹnusọ fún àyípadà àbájáde ìlera nípasẹ̀ àwọn ọ̀nà àtinúdá. Ní ọjọ́ 8 oṣù Igbe ọdún 2023, Fleming àti Yende ṣeré fún eré-orin aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ kan tí wọ́n pe àkòrí rẹ̀ ní Healing Arts, tí wọ́n fi sọrí àjọ̀dún 75th WHO ní Geneva, Switzerland. Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, sọ wí pé, "Iyì ńlá ni ó jẹ́ fún WHO pé Renée Fleming àti Pretty Yende dárapọ̀ mọ́ wa gẹ́gẹ́ bíí Aṣojú Onínúure fún Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti Ìlera. Ẹ̀bùn aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ wọn, ìfọkànsìn wọn sí iṣẹ́-ọnà náà, àti ìfọkànsìn wọn láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera àti ìgbáyégbádùn ló sọ wọ́n di aṣojú tó yẹ fún ìrìnàjò pàtàkì yìí. Ìdásí wọn máa ṣe ìwúrí fún àwọn ènìyàn, àwọn àwùjọ àti àwọn aṣòfin láti mọ rírì àti láti ṣàmúlò agbára àyípadà tí Iṣẹ́-ọnà ní nínú ìlépa ìlera tó dára fún gbogbo ènìyàn. *Fleming máa mú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe bọ̀ wá sí ojúṣe rẹ̀ tuntun. Ó fa ojú àwọn olùgbọ́ rẹ̀ jákèjádò àgbáyé mọ́ra pẹ̀lú òhun tínrín rẹ̀ tó rẹwà àti Iṣẹ́-ọnà rẹ̀ tó fànimọ́ra.
translations.reference: Renowned artists Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende appointed as WHO Goodwill Ambassadors for Arts and Health WHO is delighted to announce the appointment of internationally acclaimed artists Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende as Goodwill Ambassadors for Arts and Health. This prestigious announcement was made as part of WHO's 75th Anniversary commemoration during the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly on 22 May 2023. Renée Fleming, one of the most celebrated singers of our time, and Pretty Yende, an extraordinary soprano known for her dazzling performances, have been selected for their artistic achievement and advocacy for improved health outcomes through creative means. On 8 April 2023, Fleming and Yende performed for a special concert titled Healing Arts, dedicated to WHO's 75th anniversary in Geneva, Switzerland. Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General, said, "WHO is honoured to have Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende join as Goodwill Ambassadors for Arts and Health. Their unparalleled talent, dedication to the arts, and commitment to advancing health and well-being make them ideal ambassadors for this important cause. Their involvement will inspire individuals, communities and policy-makers to recognize and harness the transformative power of the arts in the pursuit of better health for all. "Fleming brings a wealth of expertise to her new role. She has captivated audiences worldwide with her luminous soprano voice and compelling artistry.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba document:

The renowned opera singers Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende have been selected as Goodwill Ambassadors for the World Health Organization’s (WHO) Arts and Health Initiative, delighted to address the challenges facing artists in Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende have been selected as Goodwill Ambassadors for the Arts and Health Initiative. This recognition was bestowed upon them as part of the WHO’s 75th Anniversary celebrations during the 76th World Health Assembly on May 22, 2023. Renée Fleming, one of the most decorated singers of our time, and Pretty Yende, a celebrated coloratura soprano known for her captivating voice, have been chosen for their successful careers and their advocacy for improving health outcomes through the arts. On August 8, 2023, Fleming and Yende performed a unique concert called Healing Arts to commemorate the WHO’s 75th anniversary in Geneva, Switzerland. Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General, stated, "It is a great honor for WHO that Renée Fleming and Pretty Yende have joined us as Goodwill Ambassadors for the Arts and Health Initiative. Their exceptional talent, their commitment to the arts, and their dedication to advancing health and well-being make them worthy ambassadors for this important cause. Their work will inspire people, communities, and policymakers to recognize and harness the power of the arts in creating a healthier world for all. Fleming will continue to bring many new scientists to her platform. She has captivated the eyes of her global audience with her beautiful coloratura and inspiring Arts."

translations.source: Yàtọ̀ sí ẹ̀bùn ohùn rẹ̀ tó lápẹrẹ, Fleming ti jẹ́ agbẹnusọ tọkàn-tọkàn fún ìwádìí ní ìdábùú àwọn Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti ìlera. Àwọn àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ jákèjádò àgbáyé pẹ̀lú àwọn onímọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera, ipò asíwájú rẹ̀ nínú àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ tó jẹ́ ojúlówó, àti àjọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú WHO ṣàpèjúwe ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ sí ṣíṣe àmúlò agbára àyípadà tí Iṣẹ́-ọnà ní fún àwùjọ tó dára. "Inú mi dùn pé àjọ elétò ìlera àgbáyé tó olókìkí nì ń mọ rírì ipa alágbára tí iṣẹ́-ọnà ní nínú ìlera, iyì ni ó sì jẹ́ fún mi pé wọ́n fi ìgbàgbọ́ sínú mi láti jẹ́ iṣẹ́ yìí," Fleming ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Ní ojoojúmọ́, ìtẹ̀síwájú nínú ìwádìí sáyẹnsì-ọpọlọ àti ajẹmọ́-ìtọ́jú ń ṣàfihàn ọ̀pọ̀ nǹkan nípa àwọn ànfààní ìtọ́jú ajẹmọ́ iṣẹ́-ọnà, àwọn ìrírí, àti ètò-ẹ̀kọ́, pẹ̀lú àwọn ìpadàbọ̀ rẹ̀ fún ìgbáyégbádùn ní gbogbo ìṣẹ̀mí ènìyàn. Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, a tún ṣẹ̀ṣẹ̀ bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí mọ agbára ìyanu iṣẹ́ yìí ni, bẹ́ẹ̀ ni mo sì ń fojú sọ́nà láti fẹ ìpolongo àti ànfààní náà lójú gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Aṣojú Onínúure WHO. "Pretty Yende ti gba ọkàn àwọn olùgbọ́ rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú ohùn alárinrin rẹ̀ àti àwọn eré rẹ̀ tó yanilẹ́nu. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí olóhun tínrín tí ó ní òye ìjìnlẹ̀ nípa agbára orin, ó ti ṣàpèjúwe ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ sí lílo ẹ̀bun rẹ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá àwùjọ. Ìfẹ́ Yende fún sísọ àwọn abala ìwòsàn orin àti ìfọkànsìn rẹ̀ sí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àwùjọ dọ́gba gidi gan-an pẹ̀lú ètò Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti Ìlera WHO. Yende sọ wí pé, "Inú mi dùn pé wọ́n yàn mí gẹ́gẹ́ bí Asojú Onínúure WHO fún Iṣẹ́-ọnà àti Ìlera. Mo nírètí láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún ìdásílẹ̀ ìpìlẹ̀-òye kan fún ìtẹ̀síwájú ìwádìí nínú àwọn iṣẹ́ ọnà láti jẹ́ ohun èlò ìpìlẹ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá ìrísí, ọpọlọ, àti ìgbáyégbádùn àwùjọ wa.
translations.reference: Beyond her remarkable vocal talent, Fleming has been a fervent advocate for research at the intersection of the arts and health. Her presentations worldwide with scientists and practitioners, her leading role in major initiatives, and her association with WHO demonstrate her commitment to harnessing the transformative power of the arts to better society. "I am elated that the preeminent global health agency is recognizing the powerful impact of the arts in health, and I am deeply honoured to be entrusted to share this message," said Fleming. “Every day, advances in neuroscience and clinical research reveal more about the benefits of arts therapies, experiences, and education, with implications for well-being throughout the human life span. However, we have only just begun to realize the amazing potential of this work, and I look forward to expanding awareness and access as a WHO Goodwill Ambassador. "Pretty Yende has mesmerized audiences with her exquisite voice and stunning performances. As a soprano with a deep understanding of the power of music, she has demonstrated her commitment to using her talent to improve society. Yende's passion for sharing the healing aspects of music and her dedication to social causes align perfectly with WHO’s Arts and Health agenda. Yende said, "I am delighted to be appointed a WHO Goodwill Ambassador for Arts and Health. I hope to help establish a foundation for continued investigation into the arts being a fundamental ingredient in improving our physical, mental, and social well-being.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Unlike his generous reward, Fleming has been a dedicated ambassador for health and well-being within the framework of the Health and Wellness initiatives. His global network includes scientists and healthcare professionals, his pioneering role in the foundations that are visionary, and his partnership with WHO demonstrates his commitment to implementing the powerful potential that Health offers for a better society. "I am delighted that the global health community, which recognizes the immense impact of health, has placed their trust in me to undertake this work," Fleming said. "Every day, advances in scientific and technological medicine reveal many things about the benefits of medical research, innovations, and education, including its contributions to well-being in all aspects of human life. However, we are also beginning to understand the miraculous power of this work, and I am eager to champion and realize those benefits as the WHO Goodwill Ambassador."

Pretty Yende has captivated the hearts of her fans with her beautiful voice and her captivating performances. Truly a soprano with a deep understanding of the power of music, she has dedicated her talent to using her platform to promote well-being for society. Yende's passion for developing music therapy and her commitment to addressing social issues are closely aligned with the WHO Health and Wellness initiatives. Yende stated, "I am thrilled to have been selected as the WHO Goodwill Ambassador for Health and Wellness. I am committed to helping establish a knowledge base for the advancement of health within wellness initiatives to serve as a foundation for improving the health, wealth, and well-being of our society."

translations.source: Mo dúpẹ́ púpọ̀ lọ́wọ́ WHO fún fífi ààyè sílẹ̀ fún ìwádìí tuntun àti ìpèsè ìkànnì àgbáyé wọn láti kéde ìhìnrere náà àti láti ṣe ìwádìí lórí àjọṣepọ̀ iṣẹ́-ọnà àti ìlera. "Mò ń fojú sọ́nà fún gbogbo aṣeéṣe láti ṣe iṣẹ́ pàtàkì ní ìlú mi orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa àti ní àgbáyé fún ọjọ́ ọ̀la tó dára tó sì ní àlàáfíà," Yende fi kún-un. Ìyànsípò Fleming àti Yende tọ́ka sí àjọṣepọ̀ tó fẹsẹ̀múlẹ̀ láàrín iṣẹ́-ọnà àti ìlera. Kíkópa nínú àwọn iṣẹ́ àtinúdá, bíi orin, iṣẹ́-ọnà, àti ijó, máa ní ipa rere lára ìrísí, ìlera ọpọlọ, ìgbáyégbádùn àwùjọ, àti àpapọ ojúlówó fún ìṣẹ̀mí. Nípasẹ̀ àwọn ojúṣe wọn gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Aṣojú Onínúure, Fleming àti Yende máa ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àfibọ̀ iṣẹ́-ọnà sínú ètò ìlera, ṣe agbẹnusọ fún ànfààní sí àwọn ìtọ́jú iṣẹ́-ọnà àtinúdá, kí wọ́n tún léwájú pàtàkì àwọn ìṣesí iṣẹ́-ọnà láti mú àyípadà bá àwọn àbájáde ìlera káàkiri àgbáyé.
translations.reference: I am grateful to WHO for paving the way for new research and providing their global platform to spread the word and generate research on the connection of arts and health. "I look forward to all the possibilities to do essential work in my native South Africa and globally for a better and healthier tomorrow," added Yende. The appointments of Fleming and Yende underscore the profound link between arts and health. Engagement in creative activities, such as music, art, and dance, positively impacts physical, mental health, social well-being, and overall quality of life. Through their roles as Goodwill Ambassadors, Fleming and Yende will promote the integration of arts into health-care systems, advocate for access to creative arts therapies, and champion the importance of artistic expression in improving health outcomes globally.

translations.source: WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ojú-òpóàgbáyé láti mọ̀ àti láti dènà àwọn ewu ààrùn àkóràn WHO àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ń ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ojú-òpóàgbáyé láti pèsè ààbò fún àwọn ènìyàn kúrò níbi àwọn ewu ààrùn àkóràn nípasẹ agbára apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn. Ojú-òpó ìtọpinpin Afàìsàn Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè (IPSN) máa pèsè ìkànnì láti so àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè pọ̀, mímú àyípadà bá àwọn ètò fún gbígbà àti ṣíṣe ìyànnàná ohun àyẹ̀wò péréte, lílo àwọn dátà yìí láti ṣètò àwọn ìpinnu ìlera àwujọ ṣíṣe, àti àti pínpín àwọn ìròyìn yẹn káàkiri lọ́pọ̀. Apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn máa ń yànnàná Kóódù jínnì àwọn kòkòrò ààrùn, batéríà àti àwọn abẹ̀mí afàìsàn mìíràn láti ní ìmọ̀ nípa bí wọ́n ṣe lè ràn sí, bí wọ́n ṣe lè ṣe okùnfà ikú sí, àti bí wọ́n ṣe ń ràn. Pẹ̀lú ìròyìn yìí, àwọn onímọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera láwùjọ lè ṣe ìdámò àti ìtọpa ààrùn láti dènà àti láti dáhùn sí ìbẹ́sílẹ̀ ààrùn gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára ètò ìtọpinpin ààrùn tó gbòòrò, àti láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìtọ́jú àti àjẹsára. IPSN náà, pẹ̀lú Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tí Ibùdó WHO gba àlejò rẹ̀ fún Ọgbọ́n Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé, ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ jákèjádò àgbáyé ní ibi ìyànnàná dátà apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran tó ń léwájú, láti ọ̀dọ̀ àwọn ìjọba, àwọn ẹlẹ́yinjú àánú, àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ lóriṣiríṣi, àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àwọn ilé-ẹ̀kọ́ àti ẹ̀ka aládàni. Gbogbo wọn ni wọ́n ní èròngbà kan náà: láti ṣàwárí àti láti dáhùn sí àwọn ewu ààrùn ṣíwájú kí wọ́n tó di àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé, àti láti ṣàmúlò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ ìtọpinpin ààrùn. Èròngbà ojú-òpó tuntun yìí ní àfojúsùn, ṣùgbọ́n ó kàn lè kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ààbò ìlera: láti fún gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní ànfààní sí tẹ̀lé-ǹ-tẹ̀lé apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn àti ìyànnàná gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára ètò ìlera àwùjọ rẹ̀," Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Gẹ́gẹ́ bí wọ́n ti ṣe ṣàpèjúwe rẹ̀ kedere fún wa ní àsìkò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, gbogbo àgbáyé ní agbára nígbà tí a bá jọ dúró papọ̀ láti gbógun ti àwọn ewu ìlera àjọni."
translations.reference: WHO launches global network to detect and prevent infectious disease threats WHO and partners are launching a global network to help protect people from infectious disease threats through the power of pathogen genomics. The International Pathogen Surveillance Network (IPSN) will provide a platform to connect countries and regions, improving systems for collecting and analyzing samples, using these data to drive public health decision-making, and sharing that information more broadly. Pathogen genomics analyzes the genetic code of viruses, bacteria and other disease-causing organisms to understand how infectious they are, how deadly they are, and how they spread. With this information, scientists and public health officials can identify and track diseases to prevent and respond to outbreaks as part of a broader disease surveillance system, and to develop treatments and vaccines. The IPSN, with a Secretariat hosted by the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, brings together experts worldwide at the cutting-edge of genomics and data analytics, from governments, philanthropic foundations, multilateral organizations, civil society, academia and the private sector. All share a common goal: to detect and respond to disease threats before they become epidemics and pandemics, and to optimize routine disease surveillance. The goal of this new network is ambitious, but it can also play a vital role in health security: to give every country access to pathogen genomic sequencing and analytics as part of its public health system,” said WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “As was so clearly demonstrated to us during the COVID-19 pandemic, the world is stronger when it stands together to fight shared health threats.”
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The World Health Organization (WHO) is establishing a global hub to monitor and prevent the risks of WHO and partner-related epidemic diseases. The global hub will provide countries and regional organizations with the capacity to respond to epidemic risks through genomic surveillance. The Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (Africa CDC) will provide the framework to bring together countries and regional bodies, strengthen programs for prevention and response, implement genomic surveillance, use this data to inform public health decision-making, and disseminate information widely. Genomic surveillance will identify variants of pathogens, batteries, and other epidemic biomarkers to understand how they spread, how they can lead to death, and how they behave. With this information, scientists and healthcare workers can assess and mitigate disease risks to prevent and respond to major outbreaks as part of a robust disease surveillance system, and to develop vaccines and countermeasures. The Africa CDC, together with the WHO’s government partner institution for the Global Outbreak and Epidemic Response Network, brings together global data experts at the forefront of genomic surveillance data analysis, from governments, philanthropic organizations, various industries, civil society organizations, educational institutions, and research centers. They all share a common goal: to anticipate and respond to emerging health threats before they become outbreaks and pandemics, and to strengthen disease surveillance systems. This new global hub has great potential, but it can play a critical role in public health: to provide all countries with the ability to integrate genomic surveillance and surveillance generally into their public health programs," said WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "Just as it has proven invaluable to us during the COVID-19 pandemic, the entire world has the power to stand together to defeat today’s health threats."

translations.source: COVID-19 ṣàfihàn ipa pàtàkì tí apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn ń kó nínú ìdáhùn sí àwọn ewu àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn. Láì sí tẹ̀lé-ǹ-tẹ̀lé ojú ẹsẹ̀ fún apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran SARS-COV-2, àjẹsára kì bá tí ṣiṣẹ́ bó ṣe yẹ, tàbí bí ó wà nílẹ̀ kíákíá báyẹn. Tuntun, ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn àdàmọ̀dì àkóràn kòkòrò ààrùn náà kì bá tí di mímọ̀ kíákíá. Apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran wà ní àárín gbùngbùn ìgbaradì àti ìdáhùn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé tó ń ṣiṣẹ́, bákan náà ni gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára ìtọpinpin àwọn oríṣiríṣi ààrùn tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́, látara àwọn ààrùn inú oúnjẹ àti ọ̀fìnkìn sí ikọ́-ife àti KASA. Lílò rẹ̀ nínú àmójútó ìtànkálẹ̀ jáàgùn KASA, fún àpẹẹrẹ, ti yọrí sí àwọn àkókò oògùn apa KASA tí ó ti dóólà àìmọye ẹ̀mí. "Àjọṣepọ̀ Àgbáyé nínú ìtọpinpin apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn ti ṣe pàtàkì gan-an pẹ̀lú bí gbogbo ayé ṣe ń gbógun ti COVID-19 lápapọ̀," Dr Rajiv J. Shan, Ààrẹ fún The Rockfeller Foundation ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "IPSN gùn lórí ìrírí yìí pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkànnì tó lágbára fún àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ káàkiri àwọn ẹ̀ka àti ẹnu-bodè láti sọ ọ̀rọ̀ ọlọgbọ́n, àwọn irinṣẹ́, àti àwọn ìṣesí láti rí i dájú pé ìdènà àti ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn di ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun tó sì pọ̀ ní ọjọ́ iwájú." Pẹ̀lú ìfẹ̀lójú àìpẹ́ yìí nínú ìkápá apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè nítorí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, ọ̀pọ̀ ni ó sì ń ṣàfẹ́kù àwọn ètò tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ yìí fún gbígbà àti ìyànnàná ohun àyẹ̀wò péréte tàbí lílo àwọn dátà wọ̀nyẹn láti ṣe àwọn ìpinnu ìlera àwujọ. Kò sí pínpín dátà, àwọn ìṣesí, àti àwọn ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun tí ó tó láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwòṣe ìtọpinpin ìlera àgbáyé tó lágbára. Àwọn ètò ìṣúná tí ó lọ sókè lásìkò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà, tó fi ààyè gba ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìkápá kíákíá, ni wọ́n ti ń rẹ́bùú báyìí, kódà ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n lọ́rọ̀ jùlọ.
translations.reference: COVID-19 highlighted the critical role pathogen genomics plays in responding to pandemic threats. Without the rapid sequencing of the SARS-COV-2 genome, vaccines would not have been as effective, or have been made available so quickly. New, more transmissible variants of the virus would not have been as quickly identified. Genomics lies at the heart of effective epidemic and pandemic preparedness and response, as well as part of the ongoing surveillance of a vast range of diseases, from foodborne diseases and influenza to tuberculosis and HIV. Its use in monitoring the spread of HIV drug resistance, for example, has led to antiretroviral regimes that have saved countless lives. “Global collaboration in pathogen genomic surveillance has been critical as the world fights COVID-19 together,” said Dr Rajiv J. Shah, President of The Rockefeller Foundation. “IPSN builds upon this experience by creating a strong platform for partners across sectors and borders to share knowledge, tools, and practices to ensure that pandemic prevention and response is innovative and robust in the future.” Despite recent scale-up in genomics capacity in countries as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, many still lack effective systems for collecting and analyzing samples or using those data to drive public health decision-making. There is not enough sharing of data, practices, and innovations to build a robust global health surveillance architecture. Budgets that soared during the pandemic, allowing a rapid build-up of capabilities, are now being slashed, even in the wealthiest countries.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"COVID-19 has highlighted the critical role of viral diagnostics in responding to emerging health threats. Without consistent and reliable testing for the SARS-COV-2 viral load, isolation measures have not been effective, or implemented as quickly as they should have been. Furthermore, many of the existing diagnostic tests for the disease have not been sufficiently sensitive. Viral diagnostics are at the heart of effective disease surveillance and response, both nationally and globally, and are particularly important among the various diseases that are spreading, including foodborne illnesses and those leading to death and cancer. Its use in the tracking of cancer cases, for example, has led to early detection of cancer cells. "The Global Fund's focus on viral diagnostics has been significantly amplified by how the entire world has been struggling with COVID-19 collectively," said Dr. Rajiv J. Shah, President of The Rockefeller Foundation. "The IPSN is leveraging this moment with a powerful coalition of partners across sectors and borders to share scientific knowledge, guidance, and best practices to ensure that diagnostics and surveillance become a cornerstone of public health and grow in importance in the future." With this recent advancement in viral diagnostics in countries due to the COVID-19 pandemic, many are now strengthening existing programs for scale and improving the quality of data or using that data to make public health decisions. There is still a lack of comprehensive data, findings, and forecasting to build a robust global health surveillance model. Surveillance programs that have been operating continuously during the pandemic, which have enabled the implementation of rapid response measures, are now being reinforced, even in the most advanced countries."

translations.source: "Orílẹ̀-èdè Argentina ń náwó gidi gan-an nínú ṣíṣe àfikún sí ìkápá orílẹ̀-èdè tiwa nínú apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn àti ìkápá àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè mìíràn," Josefina Campos, Olùdarí Ààyè fún Apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran àti Òye dátà Ìbí Orílẹ̀-èdè ní ANLIS Malbrán, orílẹ̀-èdè Argentina. "Àwọn ààrùn kìí bọ̀wọ̀ fún ẹnu-bodè: ewu àìsàn ní orílẹ̀-èdè kan tún jẹ́ ewu fún òmíràn. A ń fojú sọ́nà sí àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ IPSN láti ṣe àṣeyọrí èrò àjọni fún dídènà ààrùn àti dídóólà àwọn ẹ̀mí." IPSN máa kojú àwọn ìpèníjà wọ̀nyìí nípasẹ̀ ojú-òpó àgbáyé, síso àwọn ojú-òpó agbègbè àti ààrùn-pàtọ́ papọ̀, láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò àjọṣepọ̀ láti túnbọ̀ ṣàwárí, dènà àti dáhùn sí àwọn ewu ààrùn dáadáa. Àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ máa ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ ní àwọn ìsọ̀rí àkànṣe láti gbájúmọ́ àwọn ìpèníjà pàtó, tí wọn yóò ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún pẹ̀lú ìpèsè owó nípasẹ̀ IPSN láti fẹ àwọn èrò àti àwọn àkànṣe iṣẹ́ nínú apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn lójú. Pẹ̀lú síso àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè, àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè, àti ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ papọ̀, IPSN máa ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti mú àlékún bá ìkápá pàtàkì, gbé àwọn ohùn ní ìpele ẹkùn agbègbè àti orílẹ̀-èdè sókè, kí ó sì ró àwọn kàn-án-pá wọn lágbára. Nípa ìfilọ́lẹ̀ IPSN Wọ́n máa ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ IPSN bó ṣe yẹ ní ìta ìpàdé Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé ní Geneva. Ayẹyẹ ìfilọ́lẹ̀ náà máa wáyé ní 20 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023, 17:00 – 19:30h (CET) ní InterContinental Geneva, ó sì ṣí sílẹ̀ fún àwọn oníròyìn. Nínú rẹ̀ ni a ti rí àwọn kókó ọ̀rọ̀ láti ẹnu:
translations.reference: “Argentina is deeply invested in building our own country capacity in pathogen genomics and the capacity of other countries,” said Josefina Campos, Director of the National Genomics and Bioinformatics Center at ANLIS Malbrán, Argentina. “Diseases do not respect borders: a disease threat in one country is also a threat to others. We look forward to collaborating with IPSN members to achieve our common goal of preventing illness and saving lives.” The IPSN will tackle these challenges through a global network, connecting geographies and disease-specific networks, to build a collaborative system to better detect, prevent and respond to disease threats. Members will work together in dedicated groups focusing on specific challenges, supported by funding through the IPSN to scale-up ideas and projects in pathogen genomics. By connecting countries, regions, and wider stakeholders, the IPSN will help to increase critical capacity, amplify regional and country-level voices, and strengthen their priorities. About the IPSN launch The IPSN will be formally launched on the sidelines of the World Health Assembly in Geneva. translations.source: Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Leo Yee Sin, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ fún Ààyè Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Àwọn Ààrùn Àkóràn; singapore; Dr Mario Moreira, Ààrẹ, Fiocruz, Brazil; àti Dr Josefina Campos, Olùdarí Ààyè fún Apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran àti Òye dátà Ìbí Orílẹ̀-èdè ní ANLIS Malbrán, orílẹ̀-èdè Argentina. Ayẹyẹ náà máa wáyé ní ìlànà alárànbarà, tó ń pèsè ànfààní fún àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ IPSN àti àwọn olùgbọ́ tí ó kù láti jíròrò pẹ̀lú IPSN àti àwọn iṣẹ́ rẹ̀. Nípa IPSN IPSN jẹ́ ojú-òpó àgbáyé tuntun fún àwọn amúṣẹ́ṣe apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn, tí Ibùdó WHO gba àlejò rẹ̀ fún Ọgbọ́n Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé, láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú lórí ìfiránṣẹ́ apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn yá àti láti mú àyípadà bá ṣíṣe ìpinnu ìlera àwujọ. IPSN ní àfojúsùn àgbáyé kan níbi tí gbogbo orílẹ̀-èdè ní ànfààní tó dọ́gba sí ìkápá alálòpẹ́ fún tẹ̀lé-ǹ-tẹ̀lé jínnì àti ìyànnàná gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ọ̀kan lára ètò ìtọpinpin ìlera àwùjọ. Ó bẹ́rẹ̀ ìrìnàjò yìí láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ojú-òpó àgbáyé aṣàtìlẹyìn ànfààní fún àwọn amúṣẹ́ṣe ìtọpinpin apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran tó ń sọ di ńlá tó sì ń mú kí iṣẹ́ àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ rẹ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá ànfààní àti ìdọ́gba. IPSN ní àpapọ àwọn ẹgbẹ́ òṣìṣẹ́ mẹ́ta pàtàkì tí ó ń ṣe àkójọpọ̀ oríṣiríṣi ẹ̀yà àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀, tí Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ní Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO. Àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ń ní àjọṣepọ̀ ní Àwọn Àwùjọ Àwọn Òṣìṣẹ́ Ìlera (COPs) láti yanjú àwọn ìpèníjà àjọni, tí wọ́n sì ń gbèrò láti mú àlékún bá ìṣọ̀kan àti ìmọ̀-ọ̀tun nínú apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn. Nínú Amúṣẹ́yá Ìfẹ̀lójú Orílẹ̀-èdè (CSUA), àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti sọ àwọn ìgbìyànjú di ọ̀kan kí wọ́n sì lè jẹ́ kí àwọn Gúúsù-Gúúsù ó ṣe ìṣípòrọpò láti fẹ ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkápá orílẹ̀-èdè lójú.
translations.reference: Professor Leo Yee Sin, Executive Director of the National Centre for Infectious Diseases; Singapore; Dr Mario Moreira, President, Fiocruz, Brazil; and Dr Josefina Campos, Director of the National Genomics and Bioinformatics Center at ANLIS Malbrán, Argentina. The event will take place in a hybrid format, providing an opportunity for IPSN members and broader audiences to engage with the IPSN and its activities. About the IPSNThe IPSN is a new global network of pathogen genomic actors, hosted by the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence, to accelerate progress on the deployment of pathogen genomics and improve public health decision-making. The IPSN envisions a world where every country has equitable access to sustained capacity for genomic sequencing and analytics as part of its public health surveillance system. It sets out to create a mutually supportive global network of genomic surveillance actors that amplifies and accelerates the work of its members to improve access and equity. The IPSN consists of three main operational bodies that bring together different sets of stakeholders, supported by a Secretariat at the WHO Pandemic Hub. Partners collaborate in Communities of Practice (COPs) to solve common challenges, aiming to increase harmonization and innovation in pathogen genomics. In the Country Scale-Up Accelerator (CSUA), stakeholders work together to align efforts and enable South-South exchange to scale-up country capacity building.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

Esteemed Leo Yee Sin, Director of the International Centre for Infectious Diseases; Singapore; Dr Mario Moreira, Director, Fiocruz, Brazil; and Dr Josefina Campos, Director International for Human Genome Sequencing and Data Analysis at ANLIS Malbrán, Argentina. The meeting will be held virtually, benefiting IPSN members and those who are unable to interact with IPSN and its activities.

About IPSN: IPSN is a new global hub for human genome sequencing, which received the endorsement of the WHO’s Global Health Security and Emergency Response Department, to drive progress on human genome sequencing and to enable evidence-based public health decision-making. IPSN has a global network where all countries have access to a standardized protocol for timely and effective diagnosis of severe diseases such as one of the social health planning programs. It began this journey by establishing a global sustainability hub for human genome sequencing researchers, encouraging its members' work to enable access and strengthen. IPSN has a core group of three working groups that bring together various disciplines of professionals, supported by government agencies at the WHO’s Global Health Security Department. The partnerships are through Communities of Practice (COPs) to address daily challenges, and they work to promote harmonization and knowledge sharing in human genome sequencing. Within the Country Sequencing Advancement (CSUA), professionals work together to unify the challenges so that they can encourage the authorities to develop country-specific protocols.

translations.source: COP àti CSUA ń ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ láti ẹlẹ́ka-jẹ̀ka, àwọn ìpele ìpawówọlé, àti àwọn agbègbè, pẹ̀lú ìfọkànsìn sí ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ ilẹ̀-òkèèrè àti ìdọ́gba, àti ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe tó jinlẹ̀ nínú yálà apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran tàbí fífẹ àwọn ètò ìtọpinpin orílẹ̀-èdè lójú. Ẹgbẹ́ kẹta ni Àpèjọ Àwọn Olùpèsè-Owó láti darí àwọn olùpèsè-owó onínúure, ẹlẹ́gbẹ́-jẹgbẹ́ àti ìjọba tí wọ́n wà ní àyíká mú àlékún bá àkíyèsí òṣèlú àti ìpèsè owó tí ó tó fún ìtọpinpin apó ẹ̀yọ́-ìran afàìsàn. Àpẹ̀jọ Àwọn Olùpèsè-owó tún pèsè àfikún ìpèsè owó ìrànwọ́ fún àwọn iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ IPSN. Ìdásílẹ̀ IPSN náà ni ìpèsè owó ìjọba orílẹ̀-èdè Germany ti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún sí Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO. Nípa Ibùdó WHO fún Ọgbọ́n Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé Ó jẹ́ ọ̀kan lára Ètò Pàjáwírì Ìlera WHO, Ibùdó WHO fún Ọgbọ́n Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Káríayé (Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO náà), ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àjọṣepọ̀ àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé láti oríṣiríṣi ẹ̀ka tí ó ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè àti àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ láti kojú àwọn ewu àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé lọ́jọ́ iwájú pẹ̀lú ànfààní tó dára sí dátà, àwọn ìkápá ìyànnàná tó dára, àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ojú ìwòye tó dára fún ìpinnu-ṣíṣe . Pẹ̀lú àtìlẹyìn láti ọ̀dọ Ìjọba Federal Republic of Germany, wọ́n ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO ní oṣù Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2021 ní Berlin, ní ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, tí ó ṣàfihàn àìkájú-òṣùwọ̀n káàkiri àgbáyé pẹ̀lú bí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣe ń ṣàwárí, ṣàmójútó àti àkóso àwọn ewu ìlera àwujọ. Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ àti àwọn ọ́fíísì ẹkùn agbègbè àti orílẹ̀-èdè WHO láti ró àwọn ìkápá pínpín-dátà wọn lágbára àti láti mú kí àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé ní àjọṣepọ̀ kí wọ́n sì pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti ṣe àkójọ àti láti yànnàná dátà fún ìtọpinpin ìkìlò kíákíá. Pẹ̀lú wíwà rẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó ju 150 lọ, àwọn ọ́fíísì ẹkùn agbègbè mẹ́fà, àti olú-ilé-iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ ní Geneva, arọ́wọ́tó WHO fún wa ní ànfààní láti ṣe ìtọ́jú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn, àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé àti àwọn ewu ìlera àwujọ pẹ̀lú kánjú-kánjú àti àìṣèmẹ́lẹ́ tó dọ́gba káàkiri àgbáyé. Pẹ̀lú síso àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ abẹ́lẹ́, ẹkùn agbègbè, àti àgbáyé pọ̀, Ibùdó Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn WHO ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwùjọ àjọṣepọ̀ fún àwọn onímọ̀-ọ̀tun, àwọn onímọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ káàkiri oríṣiríṣi ẹ̀ka, tí ó ń fún wa ní ànfààní láti ṣàmúlò àti láti pín àwọn ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ tó ń léwájú àti dídarí àwọn iṣẹ́ wa fún ohun tí àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé nílò.
translations.reference: The COP and CSUA bring together organizations from across sectors, income levels, and geographies, with a commitment to international cooperation and equity, and deep expertise in either genomics or country scale-up of surveillance systems. The third body is the Funders Forum to coordinate philanthropic, multi-lateral and governmental donors around increased political attention and financing efficiency of pathogen genomic surveillance. The Funders Forum also catalyses additional grant funding for projects of IPSN members. The establishment of the IPSN has been supported by German government funding to the WHO Pandemic Hub. About the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence Forming part of the WHO Health Emergencies Programme, the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence (the WHO Pandemic Hub), facilitates a global collaboration of partners from multiple sectors that supports countries and stakeholders to address future pandemic and epidemic risks with better access to data, better analytical capacities, and better tools and insights for decision-making. With support from the Government of the Federal Republic of Germany, the WHO Pandemic Hub was established in September 2021 in Berlin, in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, which demonstrated weaknesses around the world in how countries detect, monitor and manage public health threats. The WHO Pandemic Hub works closely with Member States and WHO regional and country offices to strengthen their data-sharing capacities and enable partners from around the world to collaborate and co-create tools to gather and analyse data for early warning surveillance. With a presence in more than 150 countries, six regional offices, and its Geneva headquarters, WHO’s reach gives us the ability to treat pandemic, epidemic and public health risks with equal urgency and diligence around the globe. By linking local, regional, and global initiatives, the WHO Pandemic Hub fosters a collaborative environment for innovators, scientists and experts from across a wide spectrum of disciplines, allowing us to leverage and share cutting-edge technology and anchoring our work in the needs of stakeholders around the world.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

COP and CSUA are bringing together health facilities from various levels of care and regions, with a focus on rural health and resilience, and expertise shared within the framework of national health response plans. The third pillar is the Assembly of Taxpayers to lead taxpayers, corporate entities, and the government who are jointly responsible for the tax revenue that supports national health emergency response. The Assembly of Taxpayers also pays additional tax for the activities of IPSN members. The foundation of IPSN was supported by a tax contribution from the German Federal Government to the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Preparedness. Through the WHO Hub for Global Pandemic and Epidemic Intelligence and Response, which is one of the WHO’s Health Programme initiatives, the WHO Hub for Global Pandemic and Epidemic Preparedness (the WHO Hub for Epidemic and Pandemic Intelligence) supports global partnerships from various sectors that support countries and communities to mitigate the risks of epidemic and pandemic threats in the future with good access to data, effective response measures, and quality information and evidence for decision-making. With support from the Federal Republic of Germany, the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Preparedness was established in Berlin in September 2021, in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, which exposed the global vulnerability in how countries monitor, prepare for, and manage public health emergencies. The WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Preparedness works closely with Member States and regional and country WHO offices to strengthen their data-driven response capacities and to enable global partners to collaborate, and to provide advice to collect and analyze data for timely risk assessment. With a presence in over 150 countries, six regional offices, and its headquarters in Geneva, WHO’s expertise provides us with the opportunity to assess epidemics, pandemics, and public health emergencies with urgency and effectiveness across the globe. With the expansion of regional and global networks, the WHO Hub for Pandemic and Epidemic Preparedness supports a collaborative community for researchers, scientists, and policymakers across various sectors, providing us with the opportunity to innovate and align our work with the needs of communities worldwide.

translations.source: Gígùn lé àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ káàkiri àwọn ẹ̀ka-ẹ̀kọ́, àwọn ẹ̀ka àti àwọn ẹkùn agbègbè, ó máa ṣàmúlò àkójọ agbára WHO láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn ọ̀nà àbáyọ àgbáyé tí wọ́n gbé sórí àgbékalẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé àti ìgbẹkẹ̀lé.
translations.reference: Building on expertise across disciplines, sectors, and regions, it will leverage WHO’s convening power to foster global solutions built on an architecture of global collaboration and trust.
